The main content sits within a narrow band in the middle of the screen, with wide side margins. Near the top, a bold, left-aligned service title and concise description introduce the page. Below this, a clean card houses the plan calculator with simple form fields and subdued labels. The next section presents a horizontally scrollable or simple vertical strip of popular plans, still within the same column. Under that, a small status line reports search progress, followed by a vertically stacked list of matching plans, each occupying the full column width. Lower on the page, a comparison panel provides a sparse table-like view of selected plans, and at the very bottom, a request panel summarizes the chosen option and ends with a prominent, right-aligned submission button. System components such as loaders and URL state managers operate invisibly or as slim bars, never interrupting the central vertical flow. The result is a clear, decision-focused layout that guides users from understanding the service to configuring, exploring, comparing, and finally submitting a request, all within a single, visually coherent column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page data loader sits conceptually above the main content column and, when active, presents as a minimalist overlay that covers the central content band but not necessarily the entire viewport. A semi-transparent white or very light-gray sheet spans the same max-width column (roughly 640–720px) and is centered horizontally with the regular page margins still visible on the sides. In the vertical center of this column, a small Material-style circular progress indicator appears, with a single-line status label left-aligned just beneath it. When loading completes, this overlay collapses completely, returning full focus to the service description at the top.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears as a temporarily blocking overlay centered over the main column; otherwise not rendered. It scrolls with the page if the user has scrolled, but typically appears near the viewport center at the time loading occurs.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The service description section is the first visible content section in the main column, positioned about 64px below the top of the viewport. It spans the full width of the central column (up to around 640–720px) and is centered horizontally with wide left and right margins. Within this section, a bold, left-aligned H1 heading 'Overnight Care' sits at the top, occupying roughly one line and taking about one-third of the vertical space of the section. About 16–24px beneath the heading, a short paragraph of 2–4 lines explains what the Overnight Care service includes, also left-aligned but constrained within the same column width. The entire section does not use a card background; it rests directly on the page background to mimic the mockup’s simple heading + body pattern, with about 32px of space separating it from the calculator form below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section in the normal document flow; no fixed or sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The URL state manager is invisible and does not occupy visual space. It lives logically at the page level, managing query parameters and filters without presenting any UI. In terms of layout, it has no height or width and does not affect spacing between visible components in the central column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Purely functional, non-visual component that exists outside of the rendered layout; it neither scrolls nor fixes because it has no visual representation.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The plan calculator form appears directly beneath the service description section, separated by about 32px of vertical space. It is presented as a clean, rectangular card aligned to the center of the page, following the same max-width as the content column (around 640–720px). The card has a flat, very light-gray or white background with a subtle shadow or border, and internal padding of roughly 16–24px on all sides. At the top-left inside the card, a medium-weight title such as 'Configure your overnight care' sits with 16px of space below it. Below, the form fields (start date, visit frequency, nightly budget) are arranged in a simple vertical stack, each field spanning nearly the full width of the card, with 16–24px vertical spacing between them. Any inline helper text appears directly below its field, left-aligned and tightly associated. At the bottom of the card, a compact row may contain a single 'Update plans' or similar button, right-aligned within the card with about 16px spacing above it and 16–24px padding from the card’s bottom edge. The card itself is separated from the next section (popular plans) by about 24–32px of whitespace.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrolls with the page as part of the main vertical column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The popular plans strip sits immediately below the plan calculator form, with roughly 24–32px of vertical gap separating the bottom of the form card from the top of this section. It stays within the same central column width and alignment, without extending to full viewport width. Visually, it can be a low-profile horizontal list or a simple stacked group presented on a subtle light-gray background strip or a very lightweight card. A small, left-aligned section label such as 'Popular overnight plans' appears at the top of the strip, with about 8–12px of space beneath. Each popular plan item appears as a small pill-like card or a concise row, aligned left and spaced evenly (8–16px) between items. On larger screens, items can be arranged in a single horizontal row that still stays within the central column; if horizontally scrollable, the scroll area is subtle and retains the single-column feel. The entire section is visually secondary to the main calculator and results, so it is slightly more compact in height and separated from the search results below by approximately 24px.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main column; if horizontally scrollable, it scrolls horizontally only within its own region, while the section itself scrolls vertically with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The plan search results section is positioned beneath the popular plans strip, with about 24px of vertical separation. It is a major block occupying much of the middle of the page and follows the same central column width rules (around 640–720px, centered). At its top-left, a simple heading such as 'Matching overnight plans' appears, with around 8–12px space beneath. Directly under the heading, the first result item begins. Each plan result is presented in a flat card or row that spans the full width of the column, with generous internal padding (16–20px) and 16–24px vertical spacing between items. Basic plan details (name, nightly price, key features) are arranged in a minimal, left-aligned, vertical stack within each card. Any actions on a plan (e.g., 'View details', 'Add to comparison', 'Request this plan') appear as right-aligned buttons or links within each card, but still within the same column, with enough vertical padding above and below to feel uncluttered. The list can extend beyond the initial viewport height, continuing the vertical flow. At the very bottom of the results list, there is a comfortable 32–40px gap before the comparison panel begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrolls as part of the main vertical flow; the list can grow vertically with more results without changing the single-column pattern.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The search status indicator is a small, subtle element closely associated with the plan search results. It sits just above or just below the 'Matching overnight plans' heading, aligned to the left edge of the central column. For example, it may appear as a single-line, low-contrast text like 'Showing 8 plans for your dates' or a small spinner with text while loading. There is about 4–8px of vertical spacing separating it from the heading or the first result. It occupies only one line of height and does not use a card; instead, it sits directly on the page background to maintain the minimalist aesthetic. Its small footprint ensures that it does not disrupt the vertical flow from the popular plans strip into the results list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the results section; updates content dynamically but remains in the same position within the vertical layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The plan comparison panel is located below the search results section, separated by roughly 32px of vertical space to signal a new stage in the decision flow. It sits within the same central column, aligned and sized consistently (max width around 640–720px). The comparison panel is presented as a light, subtle card with flat background and around 16–24px of internal padding. At the top-left inside the card, a small heading such as 'Compare selected plans' appears. Below this, the panel either displays a minimal column-based layout (e.g., plans as columns with key attributes as rows) or a stacked list of selected plans, but always within the single-column constraints—no sprawling, full-screen tables. On desktop, two or three plans may align in a simplified grid that still respects the max column width; excess plans might be scrollable horizontally inside the card. Vertical spacing between compared attributes is moderate (8–16px) to keep the density low. The overall card height adapts to content but remains clearly separated from the request panel below by about 24–32px.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and scrollable with the rest of the page; if horizontal comparison overflow exists, internal horizontal scrolling is confined to the card while the card itself maintains its place in the vertical flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The plan request panel occupies the lower portion of the page, directly beneath the comparison panel with about 24–32px of vertical separation. It is treated as a prominent card within the same central column (max width around 640–720px), visually similar to the calculator form but with slightly stronger emphasis to highlight the final action. Inside the card, a concise heading such as 'Request this overnight care plan' is left-aligned at the top with 16px of space below. Next, a short summary of the selected plan (dates, frequency, estimated nightly cost) is presented in a small vertical stack, with each line separated by about 4–8px. Below this summary, simple form fields for contact details or special notes are arranged in a vertical stack, each spanning the card width with 16–24px vertical spacing. At the bottom of the card, a horizontal action row contains the primary 'Submit request' button right-aligned and any secondary action (e.g., 'Cancel' or 'Back to plans') aligned to its left. This button row closely mirrors the mockup’s bottom CTA arrangement, with the primary CTA occupying the bottom-right of the central column and about 16–24px padding to the card’s edges and 32–48px of whitespace below the card before the page ends or footer begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section in the normal scroll flow; serves as the visual anchor near the bottom of the main column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The request confirmation banner appears near the very top of the main content area when a request is successfully submitted. It spans the full width of the central column (aligned with the service description section) and sits either directly above the service description or directly below it, depending on implementation, with about 16–24px vertical spacing to adjacent elements. The banner is a slim, flat bar with a subtle success color (muted green or similar), internal vertical padding of roughly 8–12px, and left-aligned text stating the confirmation message. It is visually distinct but not tall—typically only one or two lines of text. When visible, it gently pushes the rest of the content downward rather than overlaying it, preserving the sequential vertical flow and the sense of spaciousness.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears inline in the normal flow at the top of the main column; may be temporarily sticky to the top of the viewport for a short duration if desired, but primarily behaves as a standard block element that scrolls with the content.

name: Memory & Dementia Care Service
title: Memory & Dementia Care Service
description: Page for viewing Memory & Dementia Care service details, browsing dementia care packages, comparing two eligible packages side-by-side, selecting the cheaper package, and choosing a start date before continuing to booking.

core_features: Load Memory & Dementia Care service description and available dementia care packages using getMemoryDementiaCarePageData.
Optionally prefilter packages by minimum visits per week using the minVisitsPerWeek URL parameter.
Allow users to select up to two dementia care packages (with at least 3 visits per week) for comparison.
Call compareDementiaCarePackages with the selected package IDs and display a side-by-side comparison including which package is cheaper and by how much.
Allow the user to explicitly confirm the cheaper package as their choice.
Allow the user to choose a desired start date for the cheaper package.
Call selectDementiaCarePackage with the chosen cheaper package and start date to create a CarePackageSelection.
Navigate to booking.html with carePlanId and startDate URL parameters after a successful selection.
user_workflows:
View service details and packages:
1. User navigates to service_memory_dementia.html (optionally with ?minVisitsPerWeek=<number>).
2. Page calls getMemoryDementiaCarePageData.
3. Service description and a list of dementia care packages are displayed.
4. If minVisitsPerWeek is present, only packages with visits_per_week >= that value are initially shown, or they are highlighted according to the filter logic.
Select two packages for comparison:
1. In the package list, the user sees available packages with their visits_per_week and monthly_price.
2. For each package with visits_per_week >= 3, the user can toggle a 'Select for comparison' control.
3. When the user selects the first package, the component sets the URL parameter compareA=<carePlanId> (or compareB if compareA is already in use).
4. When the user selects a second package (also with visits_per_week >= 3), the component sets compareB=<carePlanId>.
5. If the user deselects a package, the corresponding URL parameter (compareA or compareB) is cleared.
Compare selected packages and identify cheaper option:
1. When both compareA and compareB URL parameters are present, the page calls compareDementiaCarePackages with [compareA, compareB].
2. The comparison component displays both returned CarePlan objects side-by-side (including visits_per_week, monthly_price, and other relevant fields) and shows which package is cheaper based on the cheaperPackageId returned by the interface.
3. The component also displays the priceDifference and currency returned by compareDementiaCarePackages.
4. The cheaper package is clearly marked, and a 'Use this package' action is available only on the cheaper option.
Confirm cheaper package and choose start date:
1. After reviewing the comparison, the user clicks 'Use this package' (available on the cheaper package only).
2. The component records this choice by setting the URL parameter selectedCheaperPackageId=<cheaperPackageId>.
3. The start-date picker becomes enabled once selectedCheaperPackageId is present.
4. The user selects a start date from the date picker.
5. The selected date is stored in the URL parameter startDate=<ISO-date-string> (e.g., 2026-04-01).
Create selection and continue to booking:
1. When both selectedCheaperPackageId and startDate are present, the 'Continue' button becomes enabled.
2. On click, the page calls selectDementiaCarePackage with carePlanId=selectedCheaperPackageId and selectedStartDate=startDate.
3. If the interface returns a successful object (status such as 'pending_checkout'), the page navigates to booking.html?carePlanId=<selectedCheaperPackageId>&startDate=<startDate>.
4. If the call fails (e.g., validation error), an error message is shown and the user stays on the page to correct inputs.
interactions: On initial load or when the URL changes, the page reads minVisitsPerWeek, compareA, compareB, selectedCheaperPackageId, and startDate from the query string to initialize state.

When the user changes the minimum visits per week filter via a control, the component updates the minVisitsPerWeek parameter in the URL and re-applies the filter to the loaded packages (without recalling getMemoryDementiaCarePageData).

When the user toggles 'Select for comparison' on a package, the component validates that visits_per_week >= 3 before assigning the package ID to compareA or compareB in the URL; otherwise, it shows a validation error.
When both compareA and compareB are defined, the comparison component calls compareDementiaCarePackages exactly once per change set and stores the returned data in page state.
When the user clicks 'Use this package' on the cheaper package in the comparison view, the component sets selectedCheaperPackageId in the URL and updates the internal selection state.
When the user selects or modifies a start date, the date-picker component updates startDate in the URL and validates that the date is in an acceptable format before enabling 'Continue'.

When the user clicks 'Continue', a submit handler validates that selectedCheaperPackageId and startDate are present; then it calls selectDementiaCarePackage and, on success, redirects the browser to booking.html with carePlanId and startDate query parameters.

If any API call (getMemoryDementiaCarePageData, compareDementiaCarePackages, selectDementiaCarePackage) returns an error, the error component displays a message and the page avoids partial state updates based on that failed call.
page_functionality.state_logic:
URL parameters act as the single source of truth for key interactive state:
- minVisitsPerWeek (number, optional): If present on initial load, the page filters the dementia care packages from getMemoryDementiaCarePageData so that only packages with visits_per_week >= minVisitsPerWeek are displayed, or visually emphasizes those that meet the threshold. When the user changes the filter in-page, the component updates this parameter in the URL and re-filters in-memory data.
- compareA (string, optional): ID of the first package selected for comparison. Set or cleared whenever the user toggles comparison selection on a given package. If compareA refers to a package that is not in the current filtered set (e.g., after changing minVisitsPerWeek), the comparison component either hides that selection or prompts the user to adjust selections.
- compareB (string, optional): Same behavior as compareA for the second package. The comparison view is only shown when both compareA and compareB are present and correspond to dementia care packages with visits_per_week >= 3.
- selectedCheaperPackageId (string, optional): The package the user has chosen to move forward with. This must match the cheaperPackageId returned by compareDementiaCarePackages or it is ignored/cleared. It is set when the user clicks 'Use this package' and is used by the date-picker and submission components.
- startDate (string, optional): The selected start date in a standard date or date-time string format. Set by the date-picker; used as the selectedStartDate parameter for selectDementiaCarePackage and passed through to booking.html. If this parameter is missing or invalid, the 'Continue' button remains disabled.

Page reloads or sharing the URL will preserve the user's selections (filter value, chosen packages for comparison, confirmed cheaper package, and start date), because all those states are reconstructible solely from the query parameters without requiring localStorage or other client-side persistence.

components.functionality: On page initialization, calls getMemoryDementiaCarePageData to retrieve the Memory & Dementia Care service description and all dementia care packages. Applies the minVisitsPerWeek URL parameter as a client-side filter or highlight on the returned CarePlan list and exposes filtered and full lists to child components.
event_handlers: onPageLoad: Read URL parameters and invoke getMemoryDementiaCarePageData. Store service.careService, service.heroText, service.detailsHtml, and the packages array in page state.

onUrlChange: When minVisitsPerWeek changes, recompute filteredPackages by applying visits_per_week >= minVisitsPerWeek against the full packages list, without re-calling getMemoryDementiaCarePageData.

components.functionality: Displays the Memory & Dementia Care service information returned from getMemoryDementiaCarePageData, including heroText and detailsHtml, to explain the care approach, scope, and visit patterns.
event_handlers: onDataReady: Render heroText and detailsHtml from the service object provided by page-data-loader.

components.functionality: Provides a control (e.g., numeric input or dropdown) to adjust the minimum visits per week used to filter or highlight dementia care packages. Synchronizes this value with the minVisitsPerWeek URL parameter.
event_handlers: onInit: Read minVisitsPerWeek from the URL and display it in the filter control if present.

onFilterChange: When the user adjusts the minimum visits per week value, update the minVisitsPerWeek parameter in the URL and notify page-data-loader to re-apply filtering over the existing packages list.

components.functionality: Shows the list of dementia care packages (CarePlan objects) returned from getMemoryDementiaCarePageData after optional filtering. For each package, displays key data such as name, visits_per_week, monthly_price, and a 'Select for comparison' toggle if the package has at least 3 visits per week.
event_handlers: onRender: Consume the filteredPackages from page-data-loader and display them as a list.

onToggleCompare(packageId):
  - Validate that the package's visits_per_week >= 3 before allowing selection.
  - If compareA is empty in the URL, set compareA=packageId; else if compareB is empty and packageId is not already in compareA, set compareB=packageId.
  - If the packageId is already in compareA or compareB, remove the corresponding parameter from the URL.
  - After updating compareA/compareB, notify comparison-state-manager so it can update or clear comparison results.

components.functionality: Manages selection of two care plans for comparison, triggers compareDementiaCarePackages when both selections are present, and stores the comparison result (including cheaperPackageId, priceDifference, and currency) for the comparison view.
event_handlers: onUrlChange: When compareA or compareB changes in the URL, validate that both referenced IDs exist in the loaded packages list and correspond to dementia care packages with visits_per_week >= 3.

onSelectionsValid: If both compareA and compareB are valid and distinct, call compareDementiaCarePackages with carePlanIds=[compareA, compareB], then store the returned packages array, cheaperPackageId, priceDifference, and currency in the component state.

onInvalidSelection: If any selected ID is invalid or refers to a package that does not meet the 3+ visits_per_week requirement, clear the invalid compare parameter from the URL and display an error via feedback-banner.

components.functionality: Displays the result of compareDementiaCarePackages, showing both selected dementia care packages side-by-side with their key attributes, highlighting the cheaper package. Provides a 'Use this package' action on the cheaper package, which sets selectedCheaperPackageId in the URL.
event_handlers: onDataReady: When comparison-state-manager provides comparison data, render the two packages and visually indicate which one is cheaper according to cheaperPackageId.

onUseCheaperPackageClick(cheaperPackageId):
  - Set selectedCheaperPackageId=cheaperPackageId in the URL.
  - Clear any previous selectedCheaperPackageId that does not match cheaperPackageId.
  - Notify start-date-picker that a valid cheaper package has been chosen.

components.functionality: Allows the user to choose a start date for the selected cheaper package. The control is enabled only when selectedCheaperPackageId is present and valid. The chosen date is synchronized with the startDate URL parameter.
event_handlers: onInitOrUrlChange: Check for selectedCheaperPackageId in the URL. If absent, disable the date input and show a hint instructing the user to choose the cheaper package first.

onDateChange(selectedDate):
  - Validate format and basic constraints client-side (e.g., non-empty, valid date format as required by selectDementiaCarePackage).
  - Update the startDate parameter in the URL to the selected date string.
  - Notify continue-to-booking-button to re-evaluate its enabled/disabled state.

components.functionality: Submits the user's choice of cheaper care package and start date by calling selectDementiaCarePackage. On success, navigates to booking.html with carePlanId and startDate URL parameters.
event_handlers: onStateCheck: Enable the button only when both selectedCheaperPackageId and startDate are present and valid in the URL.

onClick: 
  - Read selectedCheaperPackageId and startDate from the URL.
  - Call selectDementiaCarePackage with carePlanId=selectedCheaperPackageId and selectedStartDate=startDate.
  - If the call returns a successful object (e.g., status='pending_checkout'), perform a client-side redirect to booking.html?carePlanId=<selectedCheaperPackageId>&startDate=<startDate>.
  - If the call fails, show an error via feedback-banner and do not redirect.

components.functionality: Displays validation messages and API error messages related to package selection, comparison, date selection, and submission.
event_handlers: onValidationError(message): Display the provided message (e.g., when the user attempts to compare a package with fewer than 3 visits per week).
onApiError(interfaceName, message): Display an error when getMemoryDementiaCarePageData, compareDementiaCarePackages, or selectDementiaCarePackage fails.
onDismiss: Allow the user to close the banner, clearing the current message.

components.functionality: Shows loading states while data is being fetched or submitted, such as during getMemoryDementiaCarePageData, compareDementiaCarePackages, or selectDementiaCarePackage calls.
event_handlers: onApiCallStart(interfaceName): Show a loading indicator for the relevant section (e.g., page load, comparison area, or submission area).
onApiCallEnd(interfaceName): Hide the loading indicator once the corresponding API call completes (success or failure).

layout.grid_system: Implicit single-column layout on a centered max-width container, likely aligned to an 8px or 4px Material grid. Content column roughly 600–720px wide with large outer margins.
layout.layout_pattern: All components are arranged in a single, vertically stacked column inside a centered, max‑width container (about 600–720px wide). Text is left‑aligned within this column, but the column itself is centered on the page. The structural order from top to bottom is: feedback-banner (when present), service-details-panel (acting as the main heading/intro), visits-filter-bar, package-list, comparison-view (conditionally shown), start-date-picker, and finally the continue-to-booking-button as the primary CTA at the bottom. Non-visual logic components (page-data-loader, comparison-state-manager) wrap or influence these visible sections without introducing additional layout complexity.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: All components are arranged in a single, vertically stacked column inside a centered, max‑width container (about 600–720px wide). Text is left‑aligned within this column, but the column itself is centered on the page. The structural order from top to bottom is: feedback-banner (when present), service-details-panel (acting as the main heading/intro), visits-filter-bar, package-list, comparison-view (conditionally shown), start-date-picker, and finally the continue-to-booking-button as the primary CTA at the bottom. Non-visual logic components (page-data-loader, comparison-state-manager) wrap or influence these visible sections without introducing additional layout complexity.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The page uses generous whitespace. The top 20–25% of the viewport is mostly blank, with the centered content column starting roughly 64–96px from the top edge. The core content (service details, filters, packages, comparison, date picker) occupies the central 40–50% of the viewport height, with 24–32px vertical gaps between each major section. The bottom 15–20% is visually lighter, with the date picker and a clearly separated button row, plus extra breathing room below. Horizontally, the content column occupies about 40–50% of the viewport width, with wide side margins on large screens; within that column, components generally span close to 100% of the column width while maintaining internal padding consistent with an 8px base grid.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density is deliberately low. Each major block has clear, generous padding (roughly 16–24px internally) and distinct vertical separation (~24–32px), creating a calm, readable flow suitable for a sensitive topic like dementia care. Text sections stay concise and are grouped into short paragraphs. List items (packages) are presented in simple, flat cards with minimal adornment. The comparison-view and start-date-picker are visually simple and clean, helping users focus on decisions rather than scanning dense UI.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye is guided top-to-bottom. First, any feedback-banner at the top of the column signals important status. Immediately below, the bold service-details-panel heading anchors the user in the context of Memory & Dementia Care. From there the eye moves down to the visits-filter-bar, then to the vertically stacked package-list cards. Once two packages are selected, attention shifts to the comparison-view card (a light, self-contained surface) that appears below the list. After reviewing, the user’s gaze naturally moves down to the start-date-picker and finally settles on the prominent continue-to-booking-button, aligned to the right of the column in a low-density button row. When active, the loading-indicator temporarily dominates visual attention by centering over the content with a subtle overlay.

layout.overall_description: The page presents as a calm, centered column on a neutral background, with wide side margins and ample top and bottom spacing. Near the top, after a comfortable vertical buffer, the user enters a narrow band of content that feels focused and controlled: first any unobtrusive feedback banner, then a strong, left-aligned service title and short explanation of Memory & Dementia Care. Below, a simple horizontal filter bar invites refinement of visit options, followed by a stack of clean, full-width package cards that sit one above the other with generous breathing room. Once the user selects two packages, a light gray comparison card appears in the same column, just below the list, containing a structured side-by-side view that still respects the overall single-column simplicity. Under that, a single date picker occupies its own clear section, leading the user’s eye down to a large, right-aligned primary button that feels like the natural end of the journey. The entire composition is sparse, flat, and orderly, echoing a system-style, trustworthy dialog: no sidebars, no decorative clutter, just a clear vertical path from understanding the service to selecting a package, choosing a start date, and continuing. When loading or processing, a centered status indicator temporarily overlays the column, reinforcing a controlled, system-like experience.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page-data-loader conceptually spans the full viewport, sitting behind all other components as a non-visual wrapper. When data is loading initially, the main content column area (centered and about 600–720px wide) remains structurally reserved but visually empty, allowing the loading-indicator to occupy the central focal point. Once data is available, the loader effectively becomes invisible, simply allowing the stacked content (from feedback-banner down to continue-to-booking-button) to fill the same central column space in normal top-to-bottom order.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Full-page logical wrapper with no visible surface; it governs when the main content column is rendered and when the loading-indicator overlays the view.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The service-details-panel sits near the top of the centered content column, roughly 64–96px below the top of the viewport, or directly beneath the feedback-banner if present, with a 24–32px gap. It spans almost the full width of the column (about 600–720px) and is left-aligned within it. The panel is visually simple: a bold service title at the top, followed by one or two short lines of description and key attributes stacked vertically. Internal vertical spacing between the heading and body text follows the 8px grid (e.g., 8–16px), and there is subtle top and bottom padding (around 16–24px total) to make the section feel like a distinct intro block without being a heavy card. It establishes context and anchors the rest of the page content beneath it.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block in the normal document flow, always near the top of the main column and scrolling away with the rest of the content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The visits-filter-bar is positioned directly below the service-details-panel, separated by roughly 24–32px of vertical space. It stretches the full width of the centered content column but remains visually light, resembling a slim, flat bar with controls (such as dropdowns or pill-shaped toggles) aligned to the left. The bar has modest vertical padding (about 8–12px) and horizontal padding (16–24px) to keep controls comfortably spaced while preserving the low-density aesthetic. It occupies only a small slice of vertical space (roughly 1/10 of the visible column height at most), clearly subordinate to the service details above and the package-list below. There is no heavy border; at most, a subtle bottom rule or a very light background tint differentiates it from the page background.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element in the vertical flow; it scrolls naturally with the content and does not stick or float, preserving the simple linear layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The package-list sits below the visits-filter-bar with a 24–32px vertical gap, occupying the main central region of the column. It takes the full width of the content column and consists of a vertical stack of flat, rectangular package cards. Each package card spans nearly the full column width with internal padding of approximately 16–24px on all sides, and there is generous vertical spacing (16–24px) between cards to maintain a low-density feel. Within each card, content (package name, brief description, price, and a comparison select control) is left-aligned and arranged in a simple top-to-bottom order, possibly with a single subtle horizontal division for secondary details. The list itself can extend beyond the viewport height, scrolling within the page, but always constrained to the central column with ample white space to the left and right.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrollable vertical list within the main document flow; it expands as needed in height, pushing the comparison-view and subsequent elements down the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The comparison-state-manager does not present any visible UI of its own; instead, it conceptually occupies a transparent layer over the package-list and comparison-view regions. It tracks which packages have been selected for comparison and determines when the comparison-view should appear. Spatially, it corresponds to the area from the top of the package-list down to where the comparison-view is inserted, but it does not add any additional height or visual surface.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, logical component with no direct spatial footprint; it influences the visibility and content of the comparison-view without affecting layout ordering or spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The comparison-view appears directly below the package-list once two eligible packages have been selected, positioned with a 32–40px vertical gap to clearly separate it from the last package card. It is presented as a self-contained, light gray card spanning the full width of the central column, with rounded corners (if consistent with the overall system style) and generous internal padding (24px on all sides). Inside, the content is organized in a simple, grid-like structure: a title row at the top, and below that two equal-width vertical columns aligned left and right within the card, each representing one package. The cheaper package column can be subtly emphasized (e.g., slightly stronger text weight or a faint highlight) while still keeping a minimalist appearance. The card’s height is enough to accommodate 4–6 key comparison rows with clear line spacing (8–12px between rows), but the overall block remains visually compact relative to the space around it, mirroring the recommendation card in the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static card in the vertical flow that appears or disappears based on selection state; when visible, it pushes the start-date-picker and continue-to-booking-button further down the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The start-date-picker sits below the comparison-view with approximately 32px of vertical space separating them. It occupies a full row across the content column but remains visually simple and compact. The label (e.g., “Choose a start date”) is left-aligned at the top of this block, with 4–8px of space above the date input control. The input itself spans roughly 50–60% of the column width, aligned to the left, leaving open whitespace to the right to preserve the airy, single-column feel. Vertical padding within this section is around 16–24px, ensuring it reads as a clear, standalone decision step before proceeding.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static form control in the normal flow; it neither sticks nor overlays and simply sits between the comparison-view and the final CTA button row.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The continue-to-booking-button is the final element in the main content column, positioned beneath the start-date-picker with about 24–32px of vertical separation. It is presented as a large, flat, high-contrast primary button, about 40–48px tall, aligned to the right edge of the content column. The left side of the button row remains mostly empty space, emphasizing the button as the endpoint of the vertical journey. The button’s width is sized to its label text with horizontal padding around 24–32px, making it feel substantial without stretching full-width. Below the button, an additional 48–64px of bottom margin keeps the layout from feeling cramped at the viewport edge.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static call-to-action at the end of the column; it scrolls into view as the user moves down the page and does not remain fixed or floating.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The feedback-banner, when present, appears at the very top of the centered content column, roughly 64px below the top of the viewport, and directly above the service-details-panel with a modest 16–24px gap below it. It spans the full width of the content column and is left-aligned, resembling a slim, flat notification bar with a subtle background tint (e.g., light green for success, light amber for warning) that stands out against the page background without feeling heavy. Internal padding is about 12–16px vertically and 16–24px horizontally. The text is concise, single- or two-line, keeping the overall height to roughly 1/8 of the column height or less. This placement mirrors the warning/notification positioning in the reference mockup—early in the visual flow but not dominating the entire page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static notification block at the top of the column; it appears and disappears based on state but does not overlay other content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loading-indicator appears centered both vertically and horizontally within the viewport, overlaying the entire page content when active. It typically takes the form of a Material-style spinner or progress indicator inside a small, simple container, with no extra decoration. A subtle semi-transparent scrim covers the rest of the page, dimming the main content column while leaving its structure faintly visible beneath. The indicator itself occupies only a small area (roughly a 64–96px square) but becomes the visual focal point thanks to its central placement and motion, echoing a system-level status screen. When inactive, it has no visible footprint.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Centered overlay that sits above all other components, fixed to the viewport so it remains in the center regardless of scroll position while active.

name: Find a Caregiver
title: Find a Caregiver
description: Search for in-home caregivers by location, apply filters (language, experience, rating, distance), view matching caregivers, and maintain a shortlist of favorites. All search and filter settings are reflected in the URL so the view can be shared or revisited.

core_features: Location-based caregiver search using ZIP code and distance/radius, powered by the searchCaregivers interface.
Advanced filters for language, minimum years of experience, and minimum rating, with available options loaded dynamically via getCaregiverFilterOptions.
Toggleable view modes to show either general search results or only favorited caregivers, controlled by the view URL parameter.
Results list showing caregivers returned from searchCaregivers with key profile details such as experience, rating, location, distance, languages, and specializations.
Ability to favorite or unfavorite caregivers directly from the results list using favoriteCaregiver and unfavoriteCaregiver, with visual indication via the isFavorited field from searchCaregivers.
Favorites view that displays the current shortlist of caregivers using getFavoriteCaregivers, independent of the location search.
URL-driven state management so that ZIP code, radius, filters (language, minExperience, minRating), sortOrder, and view mode are all encoded in query parameters and restored on page load.
user_workflows: Basic search by location: (1) User navigates to caregivers.html, optionally with URL parameters zip and radius. (2) On load, the page reads current URL parameters and initializes form fields (location, filters, view mode) accordingly. (3) The page calls getCaregiverFilterOptions to populate dynamic filter choices. (4) If a ZIP code is present (from URL or user input) and view is not set to 'favorites', the page calls searchCaregivers with zipCode, radiusMiles, filters (derived from URL parameters such as language, minExperience, minRating), and sortOrder (from URL if present). (5) The caregiver-results-list component displays the returned caregivers. (6) The user can adjust the ZIP or radius in the location-search-form and submit; the URL parameters are updated, searchCaregivers is called again, and the results list is refreshed.

Refining results with filters: (1) After an initial search, the user interacts with caregiver-filter-sidebar to select or change language, minimum experience, or minimum rating. (2) Each change updates the corresponding URL parameters (language, minExperience, minRating), resets the page parameter if used for client-side paging, and triggers a new searchCaregivers call with the updated filters. (3) The caregiver-results-list component re-renders using the new result set. (4) The user can clear all filters via a clear action, which removes these parameters from the URL, re-calls searchCaregivers with an empty filters object (or only those still present), and refreshes the results.

Viewing only favorites: (1) User chooses the favorites view using view-mode-toggle (or arrives with view=favorites in the URL). (2) The view parameter in the URL is set to 'favorites'. (3) In this mode, the page does not call searchCaregivers by default; instead, it calls getFavoriteCaregivers to retrieve the current shortlist. (4) The favorites-list-panel component displays the returned caregivers. (5) Location and advanced filter controls may remain visible for context but do not affect which caregivers appear in favorites mode unless explicitly designed to do so (the primary behavior is that favorites are shown regardless of location filters). (6) The user can switch back to the general search view, which changes view in the URL and resumes calling searchCaregivers based on location and filters.

Managing favorites from search results: (1) In general search mode (view not equal to 'favorites'), the caregiver-results-list shows each caregiver with its isFavorited status. (2) When a user clicks the favorite toggle on a caregiver that is not yet favorited, the component calls favoriteCaregiver with the caregiverId. (3) On a successful response, the item’s isFavorited state is updated locally and the totalFavorites value from the response can be used to update any favorites count display. (4) When a user unfavorites a caregiver, the component calls unfavoriteCaregiver with the caregiverId. (5) On success, isFavorited is updated to reflect the change and any favorites count is updated. (6) If the page is in favorites view, unfavoriting a caregiver also triggers a refresh via getFavoriteCaregivers so that the favorites list updates to remove the caregiver.

Restoring and sharing search state: (1) A user configures a search (ZIP, radius, filters, sort order, and/or favorites view). (2) Each change updates the browser URL query parameters without reloading the page, using the History API; for example, zip, radius, language, minExperience, minRating, sort, view, and page (if client-side pagination is used). (3) The user can copy the full URL and share it or bookmark it. (4) When the URL is revisited, the url-state-manager reads all query parameters and reinitializes each component’s state. (5) The page then loads filter options via getCaregiverFilterOptions and either calls searchCaregivers or getFavoriteCaregivers depending on the view parameter, recreating the previous state.
interactions: Location search interactions: Users enter or edit a ZIP code and optionally choose a radius in the location-search-form. Submitting the form updates the zip and radius query parameters, resets any client-side page parameter, and triggers a searchCaregivers call using these values and the current filters and sortOrder.

Filter interactions: Users select filter values (language, minimum experience, minimum rating) in caregiver-filter-sidebar. Each change updates the corresponding URL parameters (language, minExperience, minRating), then triggers searchCaregivers with an updated filters object. A clear-filters action removes these parameters from the URL and re-runs searchCaregivers with filters adjusted accordingly.

View mode interactions: Users toggle between general search results and favorites-only view using view-mode-toggle. Selecting the general search view sets view in the URL to a non-favorites value (or removes it) and triggers searchCaregivers. Selecting the favorites view sets view=favorites in the URL and triggers getFavoriteCaregivers instead of searchCaregivers; caregiver-results-list can be hidden and favorites-list-panel shown.

Sorting interactions: Users adjust sort criteria in caregiver-sort-control. The chosen sort value updates a sort parameter in the URL and triggers a new searchCaregivers call with the updated sortOrder, leading caregiver-results-list to re-render the caregivers in the new order.

Favorites interactions: Within caregiver-results-list and favorites-list-panel, users click a favorite toggle control on each caregiver item. If the caregiver is currently not favorited, favoriteCaregiver is called and the UI reflects the result (updating isFavorited and any favorites count). If the caregiver is already favorited, unfavoriteCaregiver is called. In favorites view, a successful unfavorite removes the caregiver from favorites-list-panel after a refresh via getFavoriteCaregivers.

Pagination or load-more interactions (client-side): If the results list is segmented client-side, users interact with pagination controls (such as next/previous or page number selection) in caregiver-results-list. This updates a page parameter in the URL and changes which subset of the already-fetched caregivers is displayed, without additional calls to searchCaregivers.

URL-driven interactions: When the URL changes via browser navigation (back/forward) or direct input, url-state-manager reads the new query parameters, updates component states accordingly, and reissues the appropriate API calls (searchCaregivers for general search view or getFavoriteCaregivers for favorites view), keeping the UI in sync with the URL.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page uses URL query parameters as the single source of truth for state. On load and whenever the URL changes, url-state-manager parses the following parameters: zip (location ZIP), radius (distance in miles), language (language filter code), minExperience (minimum years of experience), minRating (minimum rating), view (view mode, including a value that indicates favorites-only), sort (sortOrder value passed through to searchCaregivers), and page (current client-side page index if pagination is used). These values initialize the respective components: location-search-form reads zip and radius, caregiver-filter-sidebar reads language, minExperience, and minRating, view-mode-toggle reads view, caregiver-sort-control reads sort, and caregiver-results-list reads page. After initializing filters by calling getCaregiverFilterOptions, the page decides which data interface to call based on view: if view equals the value reserved for favorites-only, getFavoriteCaregivers is called and favorites-list-panel is shown; otherwise, searchCaregivers is called with parameters derived from the URL (zipCode from zip, radiusMiles from radius, filters object constructed from language, minExperience, minRating, and sortOrder from sort). Any interaction that changes these states (e.g., user edits ZIP, adjusts a filter, changes sort, switches view, or changes page) must first update the URL query parameters (using the History API to avoid reloads) and then re-trigger the appropriate data fetch. No state is persisted via localStorage or other client-side storage; reloading or sharing the URL reproduces the state from the query parameters alone.

components.functionality: Central controller that reads and writes URL query parameters, initializes component state from the URL on load, and decides which data interfaces to call (searchCaregivers or getFavoriteCaregivers) based on the current view parameter and filter parameters.
Captures location criteria for caregiver search, specifically ZIP code and distance/radius in miles, and triggers a search when submitted.
Manages advanced filter criteria (language, minimum experience, minimum rating) for the caregiver search, using dynamically loaded options and updating the URL and search results when filters change.
Allows the user to switch between general search results and favorites-only view by setting the view mode reflected in the URL.
Controls the sort order for caregiver search results (for example, by distance or rating) by updating a sort parameter in the URL and triggering a new searchCaregivers call.

Displays caregivers returned from searchCaregivers in general search view, including key profile information, distance, languages, and whether each caregiver is favorited; supports client-side pagination or load-more behavior.
Shows the user's current shortlist of favorited caregivers when the view is set to favorites, using data from getFavoriteCaregivers and allowing unfavoriting from this view.
Provides the per-caregiver control for adding or removing a caregiver from the favorites shortlist, updating both the backend favorites collection and the local presentation state.
Displays a summary of the current search state (such as number of caregivers returned by searchCaregivers, active filters, and view mode) to give users an overview of their current configuration.

layout.grid_system: Implicit single-column layout on a centered max-width container, likely aligned to an 8px or 4px Material grid. Content column roughly 600–720px wide with large outer margins.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a single, centered content column that all visible components stack into vertically. The column sits within a full-width background and is constrained to roughly 640–720px max-width. Components follow a linear, top-to-bottom sequence: location search at the top (analogous to the warning icon/heading focal point), followed by a compact results summary, then access to filters, then view/sort controls, then the caregiver results list, with the favorites panel treated as an optional overlay near the bottom. The URL state manager is non-visual and does not affect physical layout.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a single, centered content column that all visible components stack into vertically. The column sits within a full-width background and is constrained to roughly 640–720px max-width. Components follow a linear, top-to-bottom sequence: location search at the top (analogous to the warning icon/heading focal point), followed by a compact results summary, then access to filters, then view/sort controls, then the caregiver results list, with the favorites panel treated as an optional overlay near the bottom. The URL state manager is non-visual and does not affect physical layout.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Reserve a generous top margin (about the top 20–25% of the initial viewport) before the main search form, echoing the mockup’s airy header space. The central 40–50% of the initial viewport is dominated by the search form, summary bar, and the top of the result list controls, all within the narrow centered column. The lower portion of the viewport introduces the first few result cards and the entry point to the favorites panel. Horizontally, keep wide side margins so the content column occupies approximately 40–50% of the viewport width on desktop. Vertically, separate major sections (search form, summary bar, filter entry, controls row, results list, favorites panel) with 24–32px gaps.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain low density throughout: use generous white space between sections, card-like containers with 16–24px internal padding, and avoid visual clutter. Each component reads as a distinct, simple block: a single search form, a single summary bar, a compact controls row, and clean, well-spaced caregiver cards. No dense filter clusters or cramped multi-column layouts; instead, use vertical stacking and clear separation. Interactive targets (buttons, toggles) are large and well padded, reflecting the utilitarian, safety-focused aesthetic.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Guide the eye from the top-center location search form (first focal point) down to the results summary bar, then to the filter access area, then horizontally across the view/sort controls row (mimicking the button row in the mockup), and then vertically down through the caregiver cards. The favorites panel becomes a secondary focal area at the bottom center of the viewport when opened. The overall flow remains strongly vertical with minor horizontal scanning only where controls are intentionally grouped in a single row.

layout.overall_description: The page presents a clean, minimalist, centered layout on a light, neutral background. A tall band of whitespace occupies the top of the screen, with the location search form sitting slightly below the vertical center of that band, aligned to the center horizontally but with left-aligned labels and text fields inside. Below it, still within the same narrow column, a subtle results summary bar spans the column width, followed by a clear entry point to filtering (via a filter toggle/trigger tied to the sidebar). A few pixels lower, a single horizontal controls row appears, with the view mode toggle and sort control sharing the line, aligned within the same column and echoing the paired button arrangement from the mockup. Beneath this, the caregiver results list begins: vertically stacked, card-like entries fill the column, each with ample padding and spacing and a favorite toggle at the far right. When the favorites list is opened, a bottom-aligned sheet slides up over the lower portion of the page, still centered and matching the column width, showing a concise list of saved caregivers. Overall, the layout feels airy and focused, with a strong vertical reading flow and just one intentional horizontal row of controls, keeping the experience modern, calm, and highly legible.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component is non-visual and does not occupy physical space in the layout. It sits conceptually behind the centered content column, tracking all search, filter, and view state changes and reflecting them in the URL. It does not alter spacing or alignment of any visible element and should be treated as an invisible layer attached to the page rather than a rendered block.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, no rendered box; logically attached to the page root, listening to interactions from search, filters, sorting, view mode, results list, and favorites.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The location search form is the first visible, primary element in the centered column. It appears slightly below the top of the viewport, with about 64–96px of blank space above it, reinforcing a calm, low-density feel. The form is horizontally centered on the page and constrained to roughly 60–70% of the viewport width on desktop, with an absolute max-width around 640–720px. Within this centered block, all form elements (location input, optional radius field, submit button) are left-aligned, mirroring the mockup where the content is left-aligned inside a centrally positioned column. Vertical padding inside the form is generous (16–24px top and bottom), making the input field and primary search action appear as a unified, substantial control. There is 24–32px of vertical space beneath the form before the next section (the results summary bar).
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level element in the main flow of the single column; it scrolls away with the page content and does not stick to the top.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Directly below the location search form, separated by about 24–32px of vertical space, the results summary bar spans the full width of the centered content column. It has a low-profile, system-like appearance: a light gray or subtly tinted background, with 12–16px of vertical padding and 16–24px of horizontal padding, forming a simple pill- or bar-like strip. Text inside is left-aligned and concise (e.g., '24 caregivers found near Boston • 3 filters active'), with no heavy decoration. The bar’s height is modest, occupying roughly 1/10–1/12 of the initial viewport height, and it acts as a bridge between the search form and the controls/filters below. There is another 16–24px vertical gap between the summary bar and any filter entry or controls that follow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline block within the vertical flow; it moves with the page and does not float or stick.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter sidebar is accessed from within the main column (for example, via a small 'Filters' text button or icon placed near the results summary or controls row), but the sidebar itself manifests as a sliding side sheet rather than a permanent side column. When opened, it slides in from the right edge of the viewport, occupying roughly 30–35% of the screen width on desktop and extending from the top safe area down towards, but not fully covering, the bottom (e.g., 80–90% of viewport height). The side sheet’s content is vertically stacked filters (language, experience, rating, distance) with left-aligned labels and controls, generous vertical spacing (16–24px between filter groups), and internal padding of 16–24px on all sides. The underlying centered column (search, summary, results) remains visible but dimmed or slightly de-emphasized, keeping the sidebar clearly secondary. On smaller viewports, the same component can behave as a full-width overlay sliding up from the bottom, still honoring a single-column, vertically stacked filter layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Overlay side sheet anchored to the right edge of the viewport (or bottom sheet on smaller screens). It is positioned above the main content with a scrim and can be dismissed. It does not permanently consume layout width in the base single-column flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The view mode toggle sits within a horizontal controls row near the top of the results area, creating the one intentional horizontal grouping of controls in the otherwise vertical layout. After the results summary bar, leave about 16–24px of vertical space, then place a row that spans the full width of the centered column. Within this row, the view mode toggle is located toward the left or center-left, while the sort control is aligned to the right, echoing the mockup’s paired button arrangement. The row’s height is modest, around 48px, with 8–12px of vertical padding. The toggle itself is compact (e.g., a segmented control or icon buttons) with clear on/off or list/map states, but visually understated so that it does not overpower the sort control or results. There is 16–24px of vertical space below this controls row before the first caregiver result card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element within the main vertical flow; it shares a horizontal flex row with the sort control and aligns with the column’s left and right edges.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort control shares the same horizontal controls row as the view mode toggle, positioned on the far right edge of the centered column. It aligns visually with the right edge of the results cards below, creating a clean vertical line. The control might be a dropdown or a compact button labeled 'Sort', with an icon, sized so that its visual weight is similar to the primary button in the mockup but adapted to a neutral, system-like style. It has 8–12px of internal padding and stands out enough to be clearly tappable but remains minimalist. The controls row around it maintains about 16–24px of space above and below relative to neighboring components, so the sort control does not feel crowded. This right-aligned position mirrors the mockup’s emphasis on a primary action at the lower right of a section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element in the horizontal controls row; right-aligned within the centered column and scrolls with the content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The caregiver results list begins directly beneath the controls row, separated by 16–24px of whitespace. It occupies the full width of the centered content column (approximately 600–720px), with each caregiver represented as a card-like row. Cards are left-aligned, with 16–24px of padding on all sides and a subtle border or shadow that remains consistent with the minimalist, flat Material-inspired aesthetic. Vertical spacing between cards is 16–24px, keeping density low and information scannable. Each card’s content is arranged in a simple vertical stack: caregiver name and primary details at the top left, key attributes (experience, languages, rating, distance) in one or two lines beneath, and the favorite-toggle-button anchored to the far right of the card on the primary line. The list extends downward and becomes the main scrollable body of the page, occupying the central 40–80% of the viewport height depending on the number of results, while preserving generous margins at left and right.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, vertically stacked list within the main scrollable column; cards scroll under any overlay elements like the filter or favorites sheet when those are active.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The favorites list panel is treated as a secondary, optional view that appears as a bottom sheet rather than a fixed side column, preserving the single-column nature of the main layout. Its entry point (e.g., a 'Favorites' chip or icon with count) can be positioned just below or overlapping the lower edge of the main column, near the bottom center of the viewport when the user has scrolled through some results. When activated, the panel slides up from the bottom of the viewport, occupying roughly 35–45% of the viewport height and matching the width and horizontal centering of the main content column. Inside the panel, saved caregivers are listed in a compact, vertically stacked format similar to the main cards but slightly condensed (smaller spacing, possibly 12–16px gaps) to emphasize their secondary nature. The panel has 16–24px of internal padding and a clear top bar with a title ('Favorites') and a close icon aligned to the top-right. The underlying results list remains dimly visible and non-interactive while the panel is open, maintaining a clear hierarchy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Bottom sheet overlay anchored to the bottom center of the viewport; slides over the main content when opened and can be dismissed or partially collapsed. It does not permanently occupy vertical space in the default page flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The favorite toggle button appears as a small but clearly tappable icon button within both the caregiver-results-list and the favorites-list-panel. In each caregiver result card, it is anchored at the far right of the primary information row, aligned horizontally with the caregiver’s name or key headline. It sits flush with the card’s right padding (e.g., 16–24px from the edge) and vertically centered on the top line, so that every card presents a consistent right-edge control similar to the primary action button placement in the mockup. The button is visually minimal—a simple outline or filled heart/star icon—changing state (filled vs. outline) when toggled. In the favorites-list-panel, the same button is positioned similarly, on the right side of each line item, enabling removal with the same gesture. The size is large enough to meet accessibility guidelines (approx. 32–40px touch area) but visually light to preserve the minimalist look.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline element within caregiver cards and favorites items; its position is anchored to the right side of each card’s internal layout and scrolls along with those lists.

name: Book a Visit
title: Book a Visit
description: Page for configuring a one-time or short-term home visit, searching for available time slots, and selecting a visit to continue to guest booking.

core_features: Load and display a selectable list of bookable care services using the getBookableServices interface.
Read optional URL parameters to preconfigure the selected service, visit date, visit duration, and required specialization without using local storage.
Provide a visit details form (service, date, duration, specialization) and validate that required fields are present before searching.
Search for available visit time slots that match the chosen service, date, duration, and specialization using searchAvailableVisitTimeSlots.
Display search results as a chronologically ordered list of available time slots with associated caregiver information.
Allow users to select a specific time slot and confirm the choice using selectVisitTimeSlotForGuestBooking.
On successful time slot selection, navigate to booking_guest.html with the appropriate mode parameter for the guest booking flow.

Display optional context when the user arrives from another flow (e.g., an assessment or package selection) by reading carePlanId and startDate from URL parameters and echoing them in the booking context.
user_workflows: Workflow 1 – Direct visit and manual configuration: The user navigates to booking.html without parameters → The page loads available bookable services via getBookableServices → The user selects a service from the list → The user selects a visit date → The user enters or selects visit duration → The user optionally selects a required specialization → The user clicks a search action → The page calls searchAvailableVisitTimeSlots with the chosen serviceId, visitDate, durationMinutes, and requiredSpecialization → The page displays the returned time slots sorted by startDatetime → The user selects a preferred time slot → The user clicks a continue action → The page calls selectVisitTimeSlotForGuestBooking with the chosen timeSlotId → On success, the user is redirected to booking_guest.html?mode=guest.

Workflow 2 – Deep link with preselected service and visit details: The user arrives on booking.html with URL parameters such as serviceId, serviceType, date, durationMinutes, and requiredSpecialization → On load, the page reads these parameters → The page calls getBookableServices and preselects the service either by matching serviceId or by matching serviceType if serviceId is not present → The visit date, duration, and specialization fields are prefilled from the URL parameters → If serviceId (or a resolved service), date, and durationMinutes are available, the page automatically triggers searchAvailableVisitTimeSlots → Available time slots are displayed and the user directly chooses and confirms a slot → The page calls selectVisitTimeSlotForGuestBooking → On success, the user is redirected to booking_guest.html?mode=guest, preserving only the required mode parameter.

Workflow 3 – Visit booking in the context of a care package or assessment: The user continues from another page (such as an assessment or a care package page) to booking.html with parameters including carePlanId and startDate, and optionally serviceType, serviceId, date, durationMinutes, and requiredSpecialization → The page reads carePlanId and startDate and displays a contextual note indicating that this visit is related to a previously selected plan or flow (without fetching additional plan details) → The page uses the remaining URL parameters to preselect the booking form fields and, when sufficient information is present, to automatically search for time slots via searchAvailableVisitTimeSlots → The user can adjust any of the prefilled values and re-run the search → The user selects an available time slot and confirms it, which calls selectVisitTimeSlotForGuestBooking → On success, the page navigates to booking_guest.html?mode=guest where the guest completes the booking details for the visit associated with the selected time slot.
interactions: Service selection interaction: When the user changes the selected service in the service selector, the internal page state for serviceId is updated, the URL query parameter serviceId is updated, any conflicting serviceType parameter is updated or cleared to match the new selection, and any existing search results are cleared until a new search is performed.

Visit details interaction: When the user changes the visit date, duration, or required specialization in the visit details form, the corresponding internal state values and URL parameters (date, durationMinutes, requiredSpecialization) are updated; any previously displayed search results are marked as outdated until the user triggers a new search.

Search interaction: When the user submits the search form, the page validates that a service is selected, a visit date is set, and a duration is provided; if valid, it calls searchAvailableVisitTimeSlots with serviceId, visitDate, durationMinutes, and requiredSpecialization from state; while waiting for the response, a loading state is shown; when the response arrives, time slots are sorted by startDatetime and displayed in the results list; if the response contains no items, a ‘no availability’ state is displayed instead of results.

Time slot selection interaction: When the user clicks on an individual time slot in the results list, that time slot is marked as selected in page state; if another time slot was previously selected, it is deselected; the currently selected timeSlotId is stored in state and used to enable the continue action; the URL is not required to store timeSlotId because selection is transient and will be passed via selectVisitTimeSlotForGuestBooking.

Continue / confirm interaction: When the user clicks the continue or confirm button after selecting a time slot, the page calls selectVisitTimeSlotForGuestBooking with the selected timeSlotId; while waiting, the continue button is disabled and a loading state is shown; if the call is successful and returns success: true, the page automatically redirects the user to booking_guest.html?mode=guest; if success is false, an error message is displayed and the user remains on the page to select another time slot or retry.

Automatic search interaction from URL state: On initial page load, after getBookableServices has resolved and all required search parameters (serviceId or resolvable serviceType, date, durationMinutes) are available from the URL, the page automatically triggers searchAvailableVisitTimeSlots without requiring the user to click the search button; the results can then be refined by changing the form fields and performing a manual search.

Context display interaction: When carePlanId or startDate parameters exist in the URL, the booking context component displays a non-editable summary that these values were passed from a prior flow; if the user modifies the visit date in the form, the form state and date URL parameter change, but the original startDate parameter passed for context remains unchanged unless explicitly updated by navigation from another page.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page uses URL query parameters as the single source of shareable state. On initial load, the page parses the query string to read serviceType, serviceId, date, durationMinutes, requiredSpecialization, carePlanId, and startDate. It initializes internal state with these values. The page then calls getBookableServices to retrieve the list of CareService entities that can be booked. Once services are loaded, if a serviceId parameter exists and matches one of the retrieved services, that service is selected. If there is no serviceId but a serviceType parameter exists, the page selects a service whose service_type matches the serviceType parameter according to a defined matching rule; if multiple services match, the selection rule is defined by the implementation (for example, the first match in the retrieved list) but is not hardcoded in the design. If neither serviceId nor a matchable serviceType is available, no service is preselected and the user must choose one. The visit date, durationMinutes, and requiredSpecialization internal state fields are directly initialized from the date, durationMinutes, and requiredSpecialization URL parameters when present. After both the service selection and these visit details are ready, and provided that serviceId (or a resolved service), date, and durationMinutes are all populated, the page triggers an automatic search by calling searchAvailableVisitTimeSlots. The resulting timeSlots collection is stored in state and always sorted by startDatetime when presented. When users change service, date, duration, or required specialization, the corresponding URL parameters are immediately updated to reflect the changes so that reloading or sharing the link restores the same configuration. Changing any of these search criteria resets the selected timeSlotId state and removes any previously displayed selection. The carePlanId and startDate URL parameters are treated as contextual and read-only within the booking flow; they are used only to display contextual information and are not modified by interactions on this page. The selected time slot is not encoded into the URL; instead, it is passed to selectVisitTimeSlotForGuestBooking. After that interface confirms selection, navigation to booking_guest.html includes the mode=guest parameter exactly as specified, and the selectedVisit information is made available to the subsequent page through the internal state stored by the selectVisitTimeSlotForGuestBooking interface, not through additional URL parameters.

components.functionality: On page initialization, this component calls getBookableServices to retrieve the list of bookable services. It stores the returned array of CareService entities in shared page state. After loading, it checks the initial URL parameters (serviceId and serviceType) and coordinates with the service selector component to preselect a service if possible. It also triggers an automatic time slot search when all required visit parameters are available.

This component displays a selectable list (such as a dropdown or similar control) of services retrieved from the booking-service-loader. It exposes the currently selected serviceId to the rest of the page. On initialization, if a serviceId is provided in the URL and matches a service from the loaded list, that service is selected. If there is no matching serviceId but a serviceType URL parameter exists, it attempts to select a service whose service_type field matches the serviceType value. When the user selects or changes the service, it updates the internal state for serviceId, synchronizes the serviceId (and optionally the corresponding serviceType) URL parameters, clears any existing time slot selection, and broadcasts that the search criteria have changed so a new search can be performed when requested.

This component manages all non-service visit details required for searching time slots: visit date, visit duration in minutes, and required specialization. On load, it reads the date, durationMinutes, and requiredSpecialization URL parameters to prefill its fields. When any field changes, it updates internal state and immediately updates the corresponding URL parameters so that the configuration is preserved on refresh or share. The form also handles validation when the user initiates a search, ensuring that a visit date and durationMinutes are provided and that a service has been selected elsewhere on the page. On successful validation, it emits a search event with the current serviceId, visitDate, durationMinutes, and requiredSpecialization.

This component reads carePlanId and startDate from the URL and shows a non-editable summary that the current booking is related to a particular plan or prior step. Because this page has no interface to load CarePlan details, the component does not query additional data models and instead focuses on displaying the identifiers and dates passed to it, along with a simple textual explanation that this visit request is associated with those values.
