It remains visible as long as carePlanId or startDate is present in the URL and does not alter any search behavior beyond providing context.

This component listens for search requests from the visit details form and also triggers an automatic search after initial data loading when the URL provides sufficient parameters. On search, it compiles the current serviceId from the service selector and visitDate, durationMinutes, and requiredSpecialization from the visit details form, then calls searchAvailableVisitTimeSlots. While the search is in progress, it sets a loading flag used by the results list component. When results arrive, it stores the time slots array in state, sorts it by startDatetime, clears any previously selected time slot, and informs the results list that new data is available. If no time slots are returned, it sets a ‘no results’ state that the results list component can display. If the search fails, it records an error message for display by a shared error or notification area.

This component displays the array of available time slots managed by the time-slot-search-controller. It presents each time slot as a selectable row that includes the formatted start and end datetimes, duration, specialization, and caregiver-related information (such as caregiverName, isNurse, and rating) returned from searchAvailableVisitTimeSlots. The list is always rendered in chronological order based on the already sorted data. When the user clicks on a time slot, it updates the globally selected timeSlotId in page state and visually indicates the selection. If the search is loading, it shows a loading indicator. If there are no results, it displays a ‘no availability’ message. It does not modify the URL, as the time slot selection is transient and used only for the immediate booking step.

This component provides the final action to proceed with the selected time slot. It observes the currently selected timeSlotId set by the available-time-slots-list and enables the continue or confirm button only when a time slot is selected. When the user clicks the continue button, the component calls selectVisitTimeSlotForGuestBooking with the selected timeSlotId and shows a loading state while awaiting the response. If the call returns success: true, it redirects the user to booking_guest.html with the query parameter mode set to guest. If success: false, or if the call fails, it displays the returned message or a generic error via the shared notification mechanism and leaves the user on the page to choose another time slot or retry.

This component centralizes synchronization between page state and the URL query string. On initial mount, it parses serviceType, serviceId, date, durationMinutes, requiredSpecialization, carePlanId, and startDate from the URL and initializes shared state used by other components. It then listens for state changes from the service selector and visit details form and updates the corresponding URL parameters without adding unrelated parameters. It never writes timeSlotId into the URL, ensuring that slot selection remains session-bound. It also ensures that when serviceId changes, any previously incompatible serviceType parameter is adjusted or removed to prevent stale or inconsistent configuration in the URL. The component does not call any interfaces directly but coordinates with others to decide when an automatic search is eligible based on completeness of the parameters.

This component displays validation messages, error messages from failed searches or failed time slot selections, and informational messages such as ‘no available time slots found’. Other components signal messages to this notification area instead of handling user-facing text individually. It does not directly interface with any data models or APIs, but it is updated by the search-controller and time-slot-selection-footer components when errors or notable states occur.

layout.grid_system: Implicit single-column layout on a centered max-width container, likely aligned to an 8px or 4px Material grid. Content column roughly 600–720px wide with large outer margins.
layout.layout_pattern: All content sits in a single, vertically stacked column centered on the page. The column has a fixed max width of about 640–720px and is horizontally centered with generous side margins. Within this column, elements are horizontally centered as blocks, but text and form controls are left-aligned. The sequence from top to bottom is: global page header (outside this spec), booking-notification-area, booking-service-selector, booking-visit-details-form, booking-context-summary (as a light card), time-slot-search-controller, available-time-slots-list, and finally the time-slot-selection-footer as the action area near the bottom. The booking-service-loader overlays this column when active, and booking-url-state-sync has no visible presence.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: All content sits in a single, vertically stacked column centered on the page. The column has a fixed max width of about 640–720px and is horizontally centered with generous side margins. Within this column, elements are horizontally centered as blocks, but text and form controls are left-aligned. The sequence from top to bottom is: global page header (outside this spec), booking-notification-area, booking-service-selector, booking-visit-details-form, booking-context-summary (as a light card), time-slot-search-controller, available-time-slots-list, and finally the time-slot-selection-footer as the action area near the bottom. The booking-service-loader overlays this column when active, and booking-url-state-sync has no visible presence.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The top 20–25% of the viewport is mostly clean whitespace, with the first visible booking element (notification or selector) starting slightly below center of that top band. The central 40–50% is dedicated to the configuration and search components (service selector, visit details, context summary, search controller, and results list) arranged with consistent 24–32px gaps between each section. The bottom 15–20% is reserved for the time-slot-selection-footer, which has extra vertical breathing room and visually anchors the page. The content column itself occupies roughly 40–50% of the viewport width, centered, with wide margins on larger screens and full width with inner padding on very small screens.
layout_strategies.content_density: Overall density is low and calm. Each main block (selector, form, card, controller, list, footer) has clear vertical separation using 24–32px spacing, and internal padding within cards and interactive regions is about 16–24px. Form fields and controls are not tightly packed; there is comfortable vertical spacing (8–16px) between individual inputs. There are no decorative elements; only necessary labels, inputs, and buttons are shown, in line with a utilitarian, Material-like pattern.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow is strictly top-to-bottom. The eye first encounters any active notification at the top of the content column (analogous to the warning icon region), then moves to the booking-service-selector as the primary starting decision. It continues down into the booking-visit-details-form, then the booking-context-summary card which acts as a concise confirmation of the configuration. Below that, the time-slot-search-controller guides the user into action, followed by the available-time-slots-list where the main choice happens. The flow ends at the time-slot-selection-footer, where primary and secondary CTAs are aligned with emphasis on the primary action on the right, mirroring the mockup’s bottom CTA emphasis. When visible, the loader temporarily becomes the focal point by overlaying this flow.

layout.overall_description: The page presents a calm, minimalist booking flow in a single centered column against a full-width, neutral background. After a generous top margin, a slim notification area appears when needed, followed by a clean, left-aligned service selector and visit details form stacked vertically. Slightly further down, a light, full-width context card summarizes the visit configuration in a compact, reassuring block. Beneath that, a simple horizontal search controller strip leads into a vertically stacked list of available time slots, each row clearly separated with ample white space. At the bottom of the viewport, a wide footer bar with two main actions (primary on the right, secondary on the left) anchors the experience with extra breathing room above and below, echoing the system-like warning layout. Everything is visually quiet, with consistent 8px-based spacing and no decorative clutter. When data is loading, a subtle overlay centered in the same column dims the underlying content, maintaining the same strict central alignment. Invisible state-sync logic runs in the background without occupying any visual space, preserving the integrity of the minimalist layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When active, the loader appears as a semi-opaque overlay that covers the entire centered content column from just below the global header down to just above the time-slot-selection-footer area, effectively spanning roughly 60–70% of the viewport height depending on content. It is horizontally centered and matches the width of the main content column (about 640–720px max). At the visual center of this overlay (both vertically within the column and horizontally), a compact, system-style loading indicator (spinner or progress) and a brief status message are displayed. There is generous empty space around the indicator (at least 32–48px padding on all sides inside the overlay), keeping the look sparse and focused. The rest of the page content remains visible but dimmed behind the overlay, preserving the single-column structure while clearly indicating that interaction is temporarily disabled.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Positioned as a layered overlay relative to the main content column; fixed within the viewport for the central column area so it stays centered even when the page scrolls. It temporarily sits above all other components with a high z-index and blocks interaction until loading is complete.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The service selector occupies the upper section of the main content column, beginning after a generous top margin of about 64px from the top of the viewport or global header. It spans the full width of the column (approx. 600–720px) and is horizontally centered, with its internal content (heading, description, selector control) left-aligned. Above and below the selector block there is clear whitespace: roughly 24–32px above before the next block and 24px below before the visit details form. The visual structure is linear: a concise label or title at the top, followed by the selection control(s) (e.g., dropdown, segmented buttons) stacked vertically with 8–12px spacing. The overall height of this block is moderate, occupying roughly 10–15% of the column’s vertical flow, keeping it visually important but not heavy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the vertical flow; it scrolls naturally with the page and reflows responsively while always remaining centered as part of the main column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Directly beneath the service selector, separated by about 24–32px of vertical space, the visit details form forms the core content band of the page. It spans the full width of the centered column, with internal left and right padding of about 16–24px to keep fields comfortably inset. Text labels are left-aligned, and form controls are stacked vertically with 8–16px gaps between fields, maintaining low density. Groups of related fields (e.g., date, duration, special notes) may be separated by slightly larger 16–24px gaps to create visual sub-sections within the form. Overall, the form block takes up a noticeable portion of the central viewport area (roughly 20–30% of the page height on a typical laptop screen) but still feels airy due to the consistent spacing and absence of borders or heavy lines.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in the vertical stack; the form expands vertically as needed but never breaks the single-column pattern. On smaller screens, fields simply continue stacking in the same left-aligned configuration.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The context summary appears immediately below the visit details form, with about 24–32px of vertical separation to clearly distinguish it as a follow-up overview rather than part of the form itself. It is presented as a light, subtle card spanning the full width of the content column, with a slightly contrasting background (e.g., light gray) and rounded or squared corners consistent with a system-style card. The card has generous internal padding—around 16–24px on all sides—and its content is left-aligned: a short title or label at the top and a concise multi-line summary of the current booking configuration below, potentially highlighting key values in a slightly bolder weight. The card’s height is relatively compact (roughly 15–20% of the column’s central region), with plenty of white space inside so the text doesn’t feel dense. It visually echoes the optional card in the original mockup, serving as a reassuring, non-intrusive confirmation.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline block within the central column. It scrolls with the content and does not stick to any edges. On narrow screens it still spans nearly 100% of available width with its internal padding preserving the card feel.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Below the context summary card—separated by another 24–32px—the time-slot search controller is arranged as a concise horizontal control strip that still respects the single-column layout. It occupies the full column width, with about 16–24px horizontal padding and 12–16px vertical padding, making it feel like a lean control band rather than a heavy card. Inside, elements such as filters, date navigation, or refresh controls are arranged in a left-to-right sequence but aligned within the same column: left-aligned primary controls on the left, and any secondary controls or indicators on the right, both sharing the same horizontal baseline. On small screens, these controls wrap into two lines while keeping a tidy vertical spacing of 8–12px between rows. The strip’s vertical size is modest, roughly 8–12% of the page height, marking it as an action initiator leading into the slot list below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the flow. It stays attached to the top of the available-time-slots-list as the user scrolls, without any sticky behavior, preserving the clean, predictable scroll behavior of the mockup.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The available time slots list follows directly under the search controller with a 24px vertical gap, occupying the bulk of the mid-to-lower portion of the page. It spans the width of the main column and may extend well beyond the initial viewport height, creating a vertical scroll zone. Each time slot is presented as a clean row or compact card with left-aligned text, consistent internal padding (12–16px), and clear separation between items via 8–12px vertical space or subtle dividers. This results in a low-density list where each slot is easy to scan and tap/click without feeling crowded. The whole list can take up 30–50% of the page height or more depending on the number of slots, but always in the context of the narrow central column and generous side margins, which prevent the page from feeling busy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and scrollable as part of the main page scroll. It does not use infinite scroll mechanics visually; instead, new items simply appear as the user scrolls in the same linear column. The list naturally stops above the time-slot-selection-footer, which may remain partially or fully visible if configured as sticky.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The time slot selection footer anchors the bottom of the experience, echoing the CTA button row in the original mockup. It is positioned at the bottom of the viewport once reached, spanning the full width of the content column and visually separated from the slot list by a generous 32–40px margin above. The footer itself can appear as a minimal, lightly elevated or subtly bordered bar with internal padding of about 16–20px vertically and 16–24px horizontally. Inside this bar, two main buttons are horizontally aligned: the primary action (e.g., ‘Continue to guest details’) is placed on the right, and the secondary action (e.g., ‘Cancel’ or ‘Back’) on the left, with at least 16–24px spacing between them. Buttons are large, Material-style CTAs with 12–16px vertical padding and 24–32px horizontal padding, contributing to the low-density, high-clarity feel. There is additional whitespace (24–32px) below the footer before the bottom edge of the viewport or page ends, emphasizing stability and calm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky to the bottom of the viewport within the main content area on larger screens, so that once the user scrolls past the slots list, the footer remains pinned to the bottom edge while its content stays centered with the column. On very small screens, it may behave as a natural bottom block but still visually attached to the lower edge with a slight shadow or separator to indicate its persistent action role.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component has no visible UI representation. It lives logically alongside the other components but does not occupy any space in the layout. It operates entirely in the background, tracking and syncing URL parameters with the current booking state without adding to the visual hierarchy or disrupting the clean, single-column structure.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual and non-spatial. It behaves purely as a background logic layer and does not affect spacing, alignment, or scroll behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The notification area appears near the top of the main content column, just below the global page title or top margin, analogous to the warning icon/label region in the mockup. It occupies the full width of the central column, with modest vertical height depending on the message (typically 1–3 lines of text). When visible, it sits about 48–64px from the top of the viewport and is separated by about 24px from the booking-service-selector below. The notification is styled in a minimalist, system-like banner: subtle background color indicating the message type (info, warning, success) with 12–16px vertical and 16–24px horizontal padding, and left-aligned concise text. There are no intrusive icons unless required, and if present they are small and aligned to the left of the text, preserving the formal, utilitarian look. When no message is present, this area collapses entirely, and the service selector shifts upward to occupy that space, keeping the layout visually balanced.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the top of the column. It appears and disappears by taking or releasing vertical space, without overlapping other components or sticking to the viewport edges. It scrolls out of view as the user moves down the page.

name: Guest Booking & Confirmation
title: Guest Booking & Confirmation
description: Page where a guest user reviews the selected home visit details, enters essential contact information, confirms the visit, and views the resulting booking confirmation. Also supports reopening an existing confirmed booking using a bookingId URL parameter.

core_features: Load and display a summary of the currently selected visit (service, caregiver if applicable, date, time, duration, specialization) using getGuestBookingSummary when no bookingId URL parameter is present.
Provide a guest-only contact details form (e.g., client name, phone, email, notes) required to confirm the visit.
Submit the guest contact details via confirmGuestBookingAsGuest to create a Booking associated with the currently selected visit.
Display an on-page booking confirmation state using the data returned from confirmGuestBookingAsGuest, including bookingId, status, visit date/time, service name, and caregiver name.
Update the URL to include bookingId after a successful confirmation, so that the same confirmation view can be re-opened directly.
When opened with a bookingId URL parameter, display an existing confirmation state and suppress the guest contact form and pre-confirmation actions.
Provide navigation from the confirmation state to manage the booking (booking_manage.html?bookingId=...) and to return to the home page (index.html).
user_workflows:
Workflow 1 – Guest confirms a new visit in one session (no bookingId in URL):
1. User arrives on booking_guest.html without a bookingId URL parameter (mode may be 'guest' or absent).
2. On page initialization, the page-state controller calls getGuestBookingSummary.
3. If hasSelection is true, the guest-booking-summary panel displays the selected service, caregiver (if available), date, time, duration, and specialization.
4. User reviews the summary and proceeds to fill in the guest contact details form (e.g., first name, last name, phone, email, optional notes).
5. User submits the form. The form component validates required fields (e.g., last name, at least one contact method) and emits a submit event with a contactDetails object.
6. The page-state controller calls confirmGuestBookingAsGuest(contactDetails) and shows a submitting/loading state.
7. If the call returns success = true, the controller stores the returned confirmation data (bookingId, status, visitDate, startDatetime, endDatetime, serviceName, caregiverName, message) in page state, transitions to the confirmation state, and updates the URL to append bookingId as a query parameter.
8. The booking-confirmation panel renders the confirmation details and displays links/buttons to: (a) open booking_manage.html with the bookingId, and (b) go to index.html.
9. User may choose to manage the booking or return home.

Workflow 2 – Page opened directly with an existing bookingId (confirmation re-open):
1. User opens booking_guest.html with a bookingId URL parameter.
2. The page-state controller detects the bookingId and sets the page state to confirmation-only mode without calling getGuestBookingSummary or rendering the contact form.
3. The page expects confirmation details to be available in the initial page payload for the given bookingId (e.g., server-rendered or otherwise provided to the front end).
4. The booking-confirmation panel displays the existing confirmation details (bookingId, status, visit date/time, service name, caregiver name, and any message).
5. User can click the 'Manage this booking' link to navigate to booking_manage.html with the same bookingId parameter, or click 'Back to Home' to go to index.html.

Workflow 3 – Handling missing selection or API errors before confirmation:
1. User arrives on booking_guest.html without a bookingId URL parameter.
2. The page-state controller calls getGuestBookingSummary.
3. If getGuestBookingSummary returns hasSelection = false, the summary panel shows a functional message indicating that no visit has been selected and disables or hides the contact form submission.
4. If getGuestBookingSummary fails (e.g., network or server error), an error state is shown with an option to retry the load (which triggers getGuestBookingSummary again).
5. Once a valid selection is available, the normal Workflow 1 path resumes.

Workflow 4 – Handling errors on confirmation submission:
1. User has a valid selection displayed and submits the contact details form.
2. The page-state controller calls confirmGuestBookingAsGuest(contactDetails).
3. If the call fails or returns success = false, the controller keeps the page in pre-confirmation mode, displays an error message in the form area, and allows the user to correct information and retry submission.
4. When a subsequent call returns success = true, the workflow proceeds to the confirmation state as in Workflow 1.
interactions: On page load (onInit), the page-state controller reads URL parameters (mode, bookingId) and decides whether to load a new summary (no bookingId) or to show an existing confirmation (bookingId present).
When no bookingId is present, the controller triggers getGuestBookingSummary and updates state based on hasSelection and any returned selection fields.

User can interact with the guest-booking-summary panel to review the selected service, caregiver, date, time, duration, and specialization; if the summary is missing or invalid, the form submission interaction is disabled.

User fills in the guest contact details form fields (e.g., first name, last name, phone, email, notes). Each input change updates local form state and clears field-level validation errors when appropriate.

On contact form submission (onSubmit), client-side validation runs (required fields, basic format checks). If validation passes, the form emits a submit event containing a contactDetails data object consistent with the Booking model requirements.
The page-state controller receives the submit event and calls confirmGuestBookingAsGuest(contactDetails). During this call, the submit button is disabled and a loading indicator is displayed.

If confirmGuestBookingAsGuest returns success = true, the controller stores the returned booking confirmation data, transitions the UI to a confirmation state, and updates the URL to include bookingId while preserving any existing mode parameter.

If confirmGuestBookingAsGuest returns success = false or yields an error, the controller sets a submission error message in state, re-enables the form, and allows the user to correct details and re-submit.
In the confirmation state, clicking 'Manage this booking' triggers navigation to booking_manage.html with bookingId taken from the confirmation data (e.g., booking_manage.html?bookingId=<bookingId>).
In the confirmation state, clicking 'Back to Home' triggers navigation to index.html.

When the page is opened with a bookingId URL parameter, the confirmation panel interactions (links to manage the booking or return home) remain available, but the contact form submission is disabled or hidden to prevent creating duplicate bookings for the same visit.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is driven by URL search parameters (mode, bookingId) and the results of getGuestBookingSummary and confirmGuestBookingAsGuest. On initialization, the page parses the URL: if a bookingId parameter is present, the page enters 'confirmation-only' mode, assumes confirmation details for that bookingId are available from the initial page payload, and skips any calls to getGuestBookingSummary as well as hiding the contact form. If bookingId is absent, the page enters 'pre-confirmation' mode and calls getGuestBookingSummary. If getGuestBookingSummary.hasSelection is true, the summary panel is shown and the contact form is enabled; if hasSelection is false, the page enters a 'missing selection' state where the user cannot submit a booking. When the contact form is submitted successfully, the controller calls confirmGuestBookingAsGuest with the contactDetails object, then, upon success, switches the page to a 'confirmation' state and programmatically updates the URL to add bookingId without reloading the page. The mode parameter (e.g., mode=guest) is read but not required; when present, it can control minor textual cues such as reinforcing that the user is booking without an account, but does not change the functional flow. At any time, if the URL changes (e.g., due to navigation within a single-page application shell), the page-state controller can re-evaluate mode and bookingId to determine whether to display the summary + form (no bookingId) or the confirmation-only view (bookingId present). All dynamic visit details and booking information are sourced from getGuestBookingSummary and confirmGuestBookingAsGuest or from server-provided data keyed by bookingId; no static visit or booking values are hardcoded in the page logic.

components.functionality: Coordinates URL parameter parsing, initial data loading, transitions between pre-confirmation and confirmation states, and orchestration of calls to getGuestBookingSummary and confirmGuestBookingAsGuest. Holds global page state such as mode, bookingId, loading flags, error messages, summary data, and confirmation data. Determines which child components (summary panel, contact form, confirmation panel) are visible based on state.

Displays a read-only summary of the currently selected visit when no bookingId is present. Shows dynamic fields sourced from getGuestBookingSummary.selection, including serviceName, caregiverName (if provided), startDatetime, endDatetime, durationMinutes, and specialization. If the controller indicates hasSelection is false, this component shows a functional 'no selection' state and communicates that the user must select a visit on the previous step before confirming.

Collects guest contact details required to create a Booking. Typical fields map to Booking fields such as client_first_name, client_last_name, client_phone, client_email, and notes. Performs client-side validation (e.g., ensuring last name is provided and at least one contact method such as phone or email is present). When valid, emits a submit event with a contactDetails object that the state controller passes to confirmGuestBookingAsGuest. Hidden or disabled entirely when the page state is 'confirmation' or when a bookingId URL parameter indicates that an existing booking is already confirmed.

Renders the booking confirmation details once a Booking has been successfully created or when the page is opened with a bookingId URL parameter. Displays dynamic fields from the confirmation data (bookingId, status, visitDate, startDatetime, endDatetime, serviceName, caregiverName, and any message returned by confirmGuestBookingAsGuest or provided by the server for an existing booking). Provides functional links or buttons to navigate to booking_manage.html with the bookingId parameter for further management, and to index.html to return to the site home. This panel is the primary visible component when the page is in confirmation or confirmation-only mode.

Displays loading indicators and error messages for both the summary load (getGuestBookingSummary) and the confirmation submission (confirmGuestBookingAsGuest). For summary: shows a loading state while the summary is being fetched, and an error state with a retry control if the call fails. For confirmation: shows a submitting state when confirmGuestBookingAsGuest is in progress and shows any returned or detected errors near the contact form if the submission fails.

layout.grid_system: Implicit single-column layout on a centered max-width container, likely aligned to an 8px or 4px Material grid. Content column roughly 600–720px wide with large outer margins.
layout.layout_pattern: All content is organized in a single, vertically stacked column centered on the page. A narrow main content band (about 40–50% of the viewport width, max 640–720px) holds all visible components. The state-controller determines which central block is visible at any time: (a) loading/error state, (b) review-and-confirm state (summary + contact form + CTAs), or (c) confirmed state (summary + confirmation + CTAs). Components never sit side-by-side; the layout is strictly top-to-bottom.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: All content is organized in a single, vertically stacked column centered on the page. A narrow main content band (about 40–50% of the viewport width, max 640–720px) holds all visible components. The state-controller determines which central block is visible at any time: (a) loading/error state, (b) review-and-confirm state (summary + contact form + CTAs), or (c) confirmed state (summary + confirmation + CTAs). Components never sit side-by-side; the layout is strictly top-to-bottom.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The top 20–25% of the viewport is mostly open space, with the status/loading/error display (and its icon) visually starting the column slightly below the top. The middle 40–50% of the viewport is where the guest booking summary and either the contact form or the confirmation panel live, each behaving like distinct blocks separated by 24–32px vertical gaps. The lower 15–20% holds the final CTA section (inside the form or confirmation panel), given generous vertical padding and bottom margin for breathing room. Horizontal margins remain wide, centering the narrow column on all screen sizes.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content is intentionally sparse. Each block (status message, summary panel, form, confirmation panel) has generous internal padding (12–16px vertical, 16–24px horizontal) and clear separation between lines of text and fields. There are no dense tables or tightly packed fields; form fields are stacked with comfortable vertical spacing, and the summary and confirmation panels read as light, uncluttered cards. The page uses whitespace as a primary tool to convey calmness and trust.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye first lands at the top center on the status/loading/error message (which may include an icon), then moves down to the booking summary, then to the main interactive area (either the contact form or confirmation panel), and finally to the CTA buttons at the bottom right of the main column. Text within each block is left-aligned, even though the blocks themselves are horizontally centered. In error or loading states, the status indicator takes over the central focus, mirroring the warning-screen pattern; in normal states, the primary flow is summary → form/confirmation → CTAs.

layout.overall_description: The page appears as a clean, centered column floating in a wide, calm background, following a minimalist Material-style warning/utility screen. Starting roughly one quarter down the viewport, a narrow content column (around 640px wide) begins with a simple, system-like status area: a concise title and message that explain whether the booking is being loaded, needs confirmation, or has been confirmed. Below this, separated by a modest 24–32px gap, a light, card-like booking summary panel presents the selected home visit details in a single, left-aligned stack of rows. Another similar gap leads into the main interactive block: before confirmation, this is a softly padded contact details form; after confirmation (or when reopening a booking), this area is replaced by a confirmation panel displaying booking success and key details. Inside that main block, the bottom edge is reserved for a horizontal CTA row: a primary, wide, system-style button aligned to the right, with an optional secondary button to its left, both sized generously to feel safe and decisive. The entire composition breathes; there are no decorative elements, just flat colors, clear typography, and consistent spacing that guide the user down the page in a calm, predictable sequence from status, to details, to action.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component is non-visual and does not render any visible UI. Conceptually, it sits at the top of the page’s structure and determines which main content block appears in the central column. It controls whether the user sees: (1) the loading/error display occupying the central column by itself, (2) the booking summary followed by the contact details form and its CTA row, or (3) the booking summary followed by the booking confirmation panel and its CTA row. It does not occupy space or affect the visual spacing directly; instead, it switches which of the other components are mounted into the same vertical column, preserving the single-column, evenly spaced layout pattern.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual controller only; no spatial presence. It simply governs conditional rendering of other components in the single centered column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The summary panel is the first substantial block in the main column after the status/loading message. It is centered horizontally within the page, with a max width of about 640–700px and full width on small screens, leaving generous side margins. There is a vertical gap of approximately 24–32px below the status/loading/error display before this panel starts. The panel itself reads like a light card: subtle background (e.g., very light gray or surface color), 16–24px horizontal padding, and 12–16px vertical padding. Inside, information about the home visit (date, time, duration, nurse, location) is arranged in a simple, left-aligned stack with comfortable 8–12px gaps between rows. The panel occupies roughly a third of the central column height in the review state, but shrinks naturally to fit content. Below the panel, another 24–32px of whitespace separates it from whatever main block follows (contact form or confirmation panel), maintaining the low-density, stacked feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block in the vertical flow. Always appears above either the form or the confirmation panel in the single column and scrolls naturally with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact details form sits directly below the summary panel when the booking has not yet been confirmed. It uses the same centered, single-column card-like container as the summary: max width around 640–700px, aligned centrally with wide outer margins, and separated from the summary panel by about 24–32px of vertical space. Inside the form, the title and any explanatory text appear at the top-left of the form card, followed by a vertical stack of input fields (name, phone, email, address, etc.), each taking the full width of the inner column with 12–16px vertical spacing between fields. There is generous padding around the content (16–24px sides, 16–20px top and bottom) and plenty of whitespace below the last input before the CTA row. At the bottom of the form card, separated by about 24px from the last field, a horizontal CTA row is right-aligned within the form’s width: a prominent primary button (e.g., 'Confirm home visit') on the far right, and an optional secondary text or outlined button to its left, spaced 8–16px apart. These buttons are fairly large, with ample vertical padding (12–16px) and full Material-style rectangular shapes. The entire form block, including buttons, usually occupies the central 40–50% of the viewport height, with extra empty space above and below so the interaction feels calm rather than crowded.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrollable block. It replaces the confirmation panel in the vertical flow when the booking is not confirmed, preserving the same spacing relationships to the summary above and page bottom below.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The confirmation panel appears in the exact vertical position that the contact form would otherwise occupy, directly below the summary panel and separated from it by 24–32px of whitespace. It uses the same centered, card-like container with a max width of about 640–700px and wide side margins. At the top-left inside the panel, a clear confirmation heading (e.g., 'Booking confirmed') is shown, optionally accompanied by a small success icon aligned with the text, echoing the utilitarian system-message style. Below the heading, one or two short paragraphs summarize the confirmed home visit and highlight key identifiers (booking ID, visit time, nurse name) with bold or slightly stronger text—always left-aligned and spaced 8–12px apart. Additional details such as preparation notes or next steps can follow, still in a vertical stack, maintaining generous line spacing. At the bottom of this panel, mirroring the form layout, a right-aligned CTA row holds the main post-confirmation actions: a primary button (e.g., 'Done' or 'Return to homepage') on the far right and an optional secondary button (e.g., 'Download confirmation') to its left. This CTA row sits about 24px below the last line of text, and another 32–48px of empty space sits below the panel before the end of the page, so the user visually lands on the CTAs as the final step in the vertical flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrollable block that is mutually exclusive with the contact form. The state-controller swaps this panel in once a booking is confirmed or loaded from an existing booking, without changing the overall single-column structure.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component takes on the role of the system-style warning/status area from the mockup. When active (loading, error, or critical status), it occupies the top of the central column and can temporarily be the only visible block or sit above the summary panel depending on the state. It starts roughly 64–96px from the very top of the viewport, maintaining a large blank area above. In error or special-status modes, a prominent icon (e.g., a warning or info symbol) appears at the top center of this component, roughly 48–64px in size, with 16–24px of space below it. Beneath the icon, a bold, short heading (e.g., 'Unable to load booking' or 'Checking your booking details') is left-aligned within the central column while still visually centered as part of the block. Following this, a multi-line explanatory paragraph is shown, still left-aligned, with key technical references (like 'bookingId=…' or error codes) highlighted in a slightly bolder or monospace style, mimicking the technical error code pattern from the mockup. In error conditions that prevent normal use, this component may be the sole occupant of the central column, with the summary, form, and confirmation panels hidden; the bottom of the message area includes a right-aligned CTA row in the same style as the main page (e.g., primary 'Back to home' button and a secondary 'Try again'), placed after about 24px of vertical space. In lighter states (brief loading), the component may only show an icon/spinner and message while keeping the rest of the layout reserved, but it always anchors the top of the flow and visually mirrors the system-warning layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the vertical flow when visible, with no overlay behavior. In blocking error or loading states, it replaces the other content in the column; in mild states, it can appear above the summary panel while preserving the same single-column alignment and spacing.

name: Manage Bookings
title: Manage Bookings
description: Page for clients to look up an existing home visit booking using a confirmation number and last name, view the booking details, and reschedule the visit to a different available date and time.

core_features: Booking lookup form where users enter a confirmation/booking number and client last name, with optional prefill from URL parameters, and submit to the lookupBookingByConfirmation interface.
Display of booking details returned from lookupBookingByConfirmation, including service name, caregiver name, visit date, start time, end time, duration, status, and any specialization required.
Conditional rescheduling capability when the booking’s status allows changes (for example, when it is still scheduled), exposing a reschedule flow instead of read-only details.

Reschedule date selection using a date-picker that allows the user to choose a new visit date, then calls getRescheduleOptionsForBooking with the bookingId and selected date to retrieve available time slots.
Display of available time slots for the selected date as a selectable list, each option tied to a timeSlotId from getRescheduleOptionsForBooking.

Reschedule confirmation action that calls rescheduleBooking with the current bookingId and the selected timeSlotId and, on success, updates the on-page booking details to reflect the new date and time.

User feedback and error handling for all key actions, including booking not found, no reschedule options for the chosen date, invalid input, and reschedule failures, using the message fields from the interfaces when available.
Contextual navigation to contact.html with subject=Booking support when a booking cannot be found or cannot be rescheduled, via a prominent "Need help?" or similar support link.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Open page with prefilled booking details from URL
1. User opens booking_manage.html with bookingId and lastName query parameters present in the URL.
2. On page load, the booking-lookup-form component reads bookingId and lastName from the URL and pre-populates the form fields.
3. The form automatically triggers lookupBookingByConfirmation using the prefilled bookingId and lastName, unless a previous explicit failure is encoded in the URL.
4. If lookupBookingByConfirmation.found is true, the booking-details-panel displays the returned booking information and the booking status message is updated.
5. If the booking’s status allows changes, a reschedule entry point (button or link) is shown within the booking-details-panel; otherwise, the user only sees read-only details and guidance.

Workflow 2: Manual booking lookup and handling of not-found cases
1. User navigates to booking_manage.html from site navigation without query parameters.
2. The booking-lookup-form renders empty fields for confirmation number and last name.
3. The user enters a confirmation number and last name and submits the form, which calls lookupBookingByConfirmation.
4. If lookupBookingByConfirmation.found is true, the booking-details-panel displays the booking information and the state is updated to show the result on the page; the URL is updated (e.g., via history push/replace) to include the bookingId and lastName used.
5. If lookupBookingByConfirmation.found is false, the booking-status-message component displays the provided message (if any) and indicates that no booking was found; the booking-details-panel remains hidden.
6. A support-link-block is displayed prominently, allowing the user to click through to contact.html with the subject URL parameter set for booking support.

Workflow 3: Rescheduling an existing booking
1. After a successful lookup, the booking-details-panel shows the current booking details and a "Reschedule" action if the booking’s status allows rescheduling.
2. When the user activates the reschedule action, the reschedule-date-selector component becomes visible, initialized to either the current visit date or a neutral state.
3. The user selects a desired new visit date, which triggers a call to getRescheduleOptionsForBooking with the current bookingId and the chosen newVisitDate.
4. When getRescheduleOptionsForBooking returns, the reschedule-timeslot-list component displays each available time slot as a selection option based on the returned array.
5. If there are no available time slots, the reschedule-timeslot-list shows a no-availability message and prompts the user to select a different date or use the support-link-block to contact support.
6. When the user selects a time slot and confirms rescheduling, the component calls rescheduleBooking with the bookingId and selected timeSlotId.
7. If rescheduleBooking.success is true, the booking-details-panel updates with the new visitDate, startDatetime, endDatetime, durationMinutes, and status from the returned booking object, and the reschedule-confirmation-summary presents a success message.
8. If rescheduleBooking.success is false, an error message is displayed using the returned message, and the user remains in the reschedule flow with the ability to choose a different date or slot or navigate to contact support.
interactions: Typing into the confirmation number and last name inputs updates the local form state used by booking-lookup-form; submitting the form triggers lookupBookingByConfirmation with those values.

On successful lookup, clicking the "Reschedule" action toggles the reschedule UI state for that booking, making the reschedule-date-selector and reschedule-timeslot-list visible while keeping the booking-details-panel visible for reference.

Changing the date in the reschedule-date-selector triggers validation (e.g., ensuring a valid date format and that a booking is currently loaded) and then calls getRescheduleOptionsForBooking; while waiting for the response, a loading state is shown in place of the time slots.
Selecting an individual time slot in reschedule-timeslot-list marks it as the currently chosen option in component state; the confirm reschedule button remains disabled until a time slot is selected.
Clicking the confirm reschedule button triggers rescheduleBooking; during the API call, the button enters a loading state and is temporarily disabled to prevent duplicate submissions.

On rescheduleBooking success, the page refreshes in-place booking data using the returned booking object, clears any temporary reschedule selection state, and displays a confirmation message; the URL can optionally be updated with a flag indicating the booking has just been updated.

If any interface call returns an error state or a false success flag, the booking-status-message component shows the error; users can then change inputs, retry the action, or click the support-link-block to navigate to contact.html with the appropriate subject parameter.
Clicking the support link navigates the user to contact.html, appending a subject query parameter configured to indicate booking support, without altering any booking state on the current page.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary persistent state is managed via URL query parameters and transient in-page state. On initial load, the page reads bookingId and lastName from the URL. If both are present and non-empty, the booking-lookup-form pre-fills these fields and initiates a lookupBookingByConfirmation call, unless an additional URL flag explicitly disables auto-lookup (for example, a flag indicating a previous error or a user-initiated reset). When a user manually submits the lookup form, the page updates the URL query string to reflect the latest bookingId and lastName used so that refreshing the page or sharing the link will reproduce the same lookup behavior. The currently selected reschedule date and time slot are kept in client-side component state and are not required to be reflected in the URL; they are reset when the user changes the active booking or leaves the reschedule flow. After a successful rescheduleBooking call, the page updates the booking details using the returned booking object and may append or update a lightweight URL flag indicating a recent reschedule event so that, upon reload, the page can display a contextual confirmation message without replaying the reschedule action. All long-term state that must survive refreshes or direct navigation (such as which booking to load) is encoded via URL parameters rather than localStorage.

components.functionality: Collects confirmation/booking number and client last name, initializes from URL parameters if present, and calls lookupBookingByConfirmation to retrieve an existing Booking. Controls whether initial auto-lookup should occur on page load.
Displays informational, success, or error messages related to booking lookup and rescheduling using messages from the interfaces or validation logic.

Shows the details of the currently loaded Booking including service name, caregiver name, visit date, time range, duration, status, and specialization required. Determines whether to render reschedule actions based on booking status.
Allows the user to select a new visit date for the active booking. On date selection, calls getRescheduleOptionsForBooking and manages loading state while available time slots are fetched.
Displays available time slots returned by getRescheduleOptionsForBooking as selectable options. Maintains which timeSlotId is selected and exposes a confirm action that triggers rescheduleBooking.

After a successful rescheduleBooking call, presents a concise summary of the updated booking details and confirmation that the new visit date and time are scheduled. Can also surface relevant messages from the rescheduleBooking response.
Provides a clear pathway to contact support when a booking cannot be found or rescheduled, constructing a link to contact.html with the subject parameter set for booking support.
Shows and hides loading indicators over specific sections (lookup form submission, time slot retrieval, reschedule confirmation) based on the in-flight status of the three interfaces.

layout.grid_system: Implicit single-column layout on a centered max-width container, likely aligned to an 8px or 4px Material grid. Content column roughly 600–720px wide with large outer margins.
layout.layout_pattern: All components are arranged in a single, vertically stacked column inside a centered container. The container is fixed to a comfortable reading width (about 600–700px, ~40–50% of a desktop viewport), with all modules aligned on the same vertical axis. Text inside each component is left-aligned, but the components themselves are horizontally centered. The stack order from top to bottom is: booking-lookup-form at the top of the content column, followed by the booking-status-message banner, then the booking-details-panel, then the reschedule-date-selector, followed by the reschedule-timeslot-list, then the reschedule-confirmation-summary, and finally the support-link-block near the bottom of the page. The loading-indicator-layer sits above all of these as a full-page overlay when active.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: All components are arranged in a single, vertically stacked column inside a centered container. The container is fixed to a comfortable reading width (about 600–700px, ~40–50% of a desktop viewport), with all modules aligned on the same vertical axis. Text inside each component is left-aligned, but the components themselves are horizontally centered. The stack order from top to bottom is: booking-lookup-form at the top of the content column, followed by the booking-status-message banner, then the booking-details-panel, then the reschedule-date-selector, followed by the reschedule-timeslot-list, then the reschedule-confirmation-summary, and finally the support-link-block near the bottom of the page. The loading-indicator-layer sits above all of these as a full-page overlay when active.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The first 15–20% of the viewport height is mostly open whitespace with the booking-lookup-form starting in the upper-middle of the screen, echoing the airy top section of the mockup. The central 50–60% of the page is the main working area that hosts the booking details and rescheduling controls (details panel, date selector, timeslots), all in a narrow column with generous vertical spacing between them. The last 20–25% of the page holds the confirmation summary as a prominent card and, slightly below that, the low-emphasis support-link-block, both surrounded by breathing room. Side margins remain wide at all times, with the column never exceeding ~720px width, ensuring a narrow, focused interaction band in the middle of the screen.
layout_strategies.content_density: The page maintains low content density consistent with the warning-style mockup: each major component is separated vertically by 24–32px, with larger gaps (40–64px) before the first and after the last major section. Internal padding within panels and cards uses 16–24px on all sides, creating clear, uncluttered blocks. There are no multi-column layouts or dense tables; information is broken into simple, short blocks that are easy to scan. Interactive controls (form fields, date cells, time slots, buttons) are sized generously with comfortable spacing so the page feels calm, safe, and controlled rather than busy.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow starts at the booking-lookup-form in the upper-middle as the functional equivalent of the mockup’s warning icon and heading: it is the first thing users see and interact with. Immediately below, the booking-status-message banner provides quick feedback, acting like the smaller status/error code in the mockup. The eye then moves naturally downward into the booking-details-panel, which anchors the context. Beneath that, attention flows through the reschedule-date-selector and then to the reschedule-timeslot-list, mirroring the mockup’s progression from explanation text into an actionable card. Finally, the eye settles on the reschedule-confirmation-summary card, which hosts the key confirmation information and primary rescheduling action, with the support-link-block as a secondary, quiet escape option at the very bottom. When active, the loading-indicator-layer overlays the entire page, temporarily taking full focus while preserving the centered, minimalist aesthetic.

layout.overall_description: The page presents a clean, centered column of content floating within generous whitespace, echoing a minimalist system dialog rather than a busy dashboard. At first load, the user’s eye is drawn to the booking-lookup-form located in the upper-middle of the viewport, aligned to a narrow column centered on the page. Below it, a compact booking-status-message banner appears inline, reserving just enough space to be noticed without dominating the layout. As the user successfully looks up a booking, the column extends downward into the booking-details-panel, followed by a sequence of rescheduling tools: first the date selector, then a list of available times. These modules are separated by consistent vertical gaps, so each feels like a clear step in a safe, linear process. Near the bottom of the column, a light, card-like reschedule-confirmation-summary sits with extra padding and a subtle elevation, acting as the final check and action area. Below that, slightly detached with additional space, the support-link-block appears as a low-key textual exit route. Throughout, side margins remain wide, the background is flat and calm, and the typography and buttons are straightforward and Material-like. When operations are in progress, a semi-opaque loading-indicator-layer gently dims the entire screen while centering a spinner or progress indicator, reinforcing the sense of controlled, system-level processing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The booking-lookup-form sits at the upper-middle of the page inside the central content column. There is a generous vertical margin from the top of the viewport to the form—approximately 64px–96px—creating a calm buffer before interaction begins. The form’s outer width is constrained to roughly 600–680px and centered horizontally, so on large screens it occupies about 40–45% of the viewport width. Inside, the form fields (confirmation number, last name) are stacked vertically, left-aligned along the same text axis as all subsequent components. The primary lookup button is right-aligned within the form’s width, similar to a Material dialog’s action row, and has 16–24px top spacing from the last field. Vertical padding around the form’s content is around 24px (top and bottom), ensuring it feels like a distinct, but not heavy, section. There is a 24–32px gap below the form before the booking-status-message can appear.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the normal page flow, centered in the main column. The form expands vertically as needed for validation messages without affecting the column’s horizontal alignment.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The booking-status-message appears directly beneath the booking-lookup-form, separated by a modest 16–24px gap that keeps it visually tied to the form. It spans the same width as the form (about 600–680px), centered in the page, maintaining a perfectly aligned left text edge. Visually, it behaves like a slim feedback banner: a low-height bar or lightly filled container with 12–16px of vertical padding and 16–24px of horizontal padding, containing one or two lines of text. The banner does not dominate; it occupies only about one-tenth of the column’s visible height and uses a flat background color aligned with the severity or success of the message. When no message is present, its space collapses to preserve the minimalist feel, letting the booking-details-panel move up. There is a 24–32px gap below this banner before the booking-details-panel begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the content flow, appearing and collapsing without changing the column’s width or center alignment.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The booking-details-panel is positioned below the booking-status-message within the same centered column, separated by approximately 24–32px of vertical space to signal a new section. It adopts a light, card-like presentation: a subtle, flat background (e.g., light gray) with 24px padding on all sides, but without heavy borders or decorative elements, reflecting the utilitarian Material style. The panel width matches the column (around 600–720px), leaving wide page margins on both sides. Inside, content (service type, nurse name, visit address, current date and time) is arranged in a simple, vertical stack, left-aligned. Short labels and values are separated by small 8–12px gaps, maintaining clarity without visual clutter. The panel typically occupies about 20–30% of the central vertical space once populated. Below this panel, a clear 32px gap introduces the reschedule-date-selector as the next step.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block within the main flow; grows vertically as more details are shown, but always respects the column’s max width and central alignment.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reschedule-date-selector sits directly beneath the booking-details-panel, separated by about 32px to mark a distinct interaction phase. It shares the same centered width as the rest of the column (roughly 600–700px) with all text and controls aligned to the left edge. The date selector is presented in a simple, system-like style: a clear section label at the top, then the date selection UI (e.g., a compact calendar grid or date list) beneath. The section label has 8–12px of space above the control to maintain an airy hierarchy. The overall height of this component is moderate—about 15–20% of the central column’s active space—allowing it to feel important but not overwhelming. There is a 24px vertical gap below the date selector before the reschedule-timeslot-list starts, preserving the vertical rhythm from the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, in-flow component that may expand vertically slightly if additional instructions or error messages appear, but always remains in the single-column layout with no horizontal expansion.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reschedule-timeslot-list is positioned immediately under the reschedule-date-selector, separated by a consistent 24px vertical gap so it reads as the next logical step. It maintains the same centered column width (600–700px) with left-aligned text. Time slots are presented as a vertical list of selectable rows or pill-like buttons, each with ample vertical padding (12–16px) and at least 8–12px of space between items to keep density low. The list height adjusts to the number of available slots, but the design avoids scrollable sub-areas when possible; instead, the overall page scrolls, keeping things simple and system-like. The list typically occupies up to 25–35% of the central area when many options are available. Below the last time slot, there is a larger 32–40px gap before the reschedule-confirmation-summary begins, visually separating selection from confirmation.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the page flow; lengthens vertically with more time slots, pushing the confirmation summary further down while preserving the central column alignment.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reschedule-confirmation-summary appears below the reschedule-timeslot-list as a prominent, self-contained card that visually echoes the recommendation card from the mockup. It is separated from the time slots by a slightly larger gap (32–40px) to highlight its role as a final checkpoint. The card spans the same central column width (up to ~720px), centered on the page, with generous 24–32px padding on all sides. Inside, the new appointment date and time are displayed prominently at the top in a slightly stronger type style, followed by a brief line or two confirming what will change. Any primary confirmation button (e.g., “Confirm new visit time”) is aligned to the right within the card’s bottom area, mirroring the mockup’s right-aligned primary action. The card height is moderate, occupying roughly 15–20% of the column, and floating in ample whitespace above and below to reinforce its importance and safety. There is about 32–48px of space beneath the card before the support-link-block.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, non-sticky card within the main flow. Its importance is communicated through spacing and padding rather than special positioning.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The support-link-block is positioned near the bottom of the content column, directly after the reschedule-confirmation-summary with a clear 32–48px vertical gap so it feels distinct and non-competitive with the main actions. It is simple and text-based, likely one or two lines of left-aligned text with a single inline or small standalone link such as “Need help? Contact support.” It spans the same column width but uses minimal vertical padding (8–12px), blending seamlessly into the background without a card or strong container. On most screens, it sits within the lower 15–20% of the scrollable area, functioning as a quiet safety net. Extra whitespace (at least 48px) below this block before the bottom of the page gives the layout a soft landing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in-flow text block; no special behavior, remains at the bottom of the content stack.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loading-indicator-layer is an overlay that sits above all other components when active. It covers the entire viewport, edge to edge, regardless of scroll position, with a semi-opaque flat background (for example, a translucent white or very light gray) that gently dims the underlying page while keeping its structure faintly visible. At the exact center of the viewport—both horizontally and vertically—a simple spinner or progress indicator appears, sized modestly (occupying around 5–8% of the viewport width), with a short label if needed. The overlay respects the minimalist, system-style aesthetic: no heavy borders or decorative elements, just a clear indication that the system is processing. When inactive, this layer is completely absent, revealing the single-column layout underneath without altering its spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed-position, full-screen overlay anchored to the viewport, not the scrollable content. It disables interaction with all underlying components while visible and remains centered on window resize.

name: Care Needs Assessment
title: Care Needs Assessment
description: This page guides users through a short care needs assessment, submits their responses to generate recommended services, and allows them to schedule a follow-up consultation directly from the recommendations view.

core_features: Load and display active care needs assessment questions and options using getActiveAssessmentQuestions in a guided, step-by-step flow.
Capture user responses for each assessment question, including single choice, multiple choice, and free text answers.
Submit all assessment responses with submitAssessmentResponsesAndGetRecommendations to create an assessment result and receive recommended care services.
Display a recommendation summary and a structured list of recommended services returned from submitAssessmentResponsesAndGetRecommendations.
Provide per-service actions from the recommendations view: open services.html focused on the recommended service type, and navigate to booking.html to start booking the recommended service.
Offer a primary call-to-action from the recommendations view to schedule a consultation (e.g., phone or video) related to the completed assessment.
Allow users to select a consultation date and channel, then fetch available consultation time slots for that date/channel using getConsultationTimeSlotsForAssessment.
Allow users to select one available consultation time slot and confirm it via bookConsultationFromAssessment, creating a ConsultationBooking tied to the most recent assessment result.
Handle loading and error states for all interface calls without using any hardcoded service names, times, or other static data.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1 – Starting and completing the assessment:
1. User navigates to assessment.html, optionally with a URL parameter autostart.
2. The page reads the autostart parameter. If autostart is present with a value indicating automatic start, the page immediately requests active questions via getActiveAssessmentQuestions. Otherwise, the user can manually click a 'Start assessment' action, which triggers the same call.
3. When getActiveAssessmentQuestions succeeds, questions (including their options and answer types) are stored in page state and displayed one-by-one or in small groups.
4. The user answers each question. For single_choice and multiple_choice questions, the user selects from options; for free_text questions, the user types a response. Navigation controls allow moving to the next or previous question while keeping already-entered answers in memory.
5. When all required questions have valid responses, the user submits the assessment. The page constructs a responses payload based on the collected answers and calls submitAssessmentResponsesAndGetRecommendations.
6. On success, the page stores the returned assessmentResult and recommendedServices in memory, updates the visible section to the recommendations view, and updates the URL state (e.g., step=recommendations) without reloading the page.

Workflow 2 – Viewing and acting on recommendations:
1. After Workflow 1, the recommendations view shows a summary text from assessmentResult and a list of recommendedServices.
2. For each recommended service, the user may trigger a 'Learn more' action. This navigates to services.html with a URL parameter focus set to the service_type of the selected recommended service.
3. For each recommended service, the user may trigger a 'Book this recommended service' action. This navigates to booking.html with URL parameters serviceId set to the service id and serviceType set to the service_type field.
4. The user can also choose a primary call-to-action such as 'Schedule a consultation' to remain on assessment.html and open the embedded consultation scheduler associated with the just-completed assessment. When this action is used, the URL parameter step is updated to indicate that the consultation section should be shown (e.g., step=consultation).

Workflow 3 – Scheduling a consultation after assessment:
1. From the recommendations view, the user clicks the consultation scheduling call-to-action, which reveals the consultation scheduler component and updates the URL parameter step to a consultation-oriented value.
2. The consultation scheduler shows controls for selecting a date and communication channel (e.g., phone or video). If the URL already contains consultationDate and channel parameters, those values are preselected in the controls; otherwise, default logic is applied (for example, defaulting to the next available date in the date picker and a default channel).
3. When the user selects or changes the date and/or channel, the page updates the corresponding URL parameters and calls getConsultationTimeSlotsForAssessment with the selected date and channel.
4. The page displays the list of available time slots returned by getConsultationTimeSlotsForAssessment. Only slots indicated as available are shown as selectable. The user selects a time slot, which updates in-page selection state and may also update a selectedSlotId parameter in the URL.
5. The user confirms the booking action. The page calls bookConsultationFromAssessment with the selected consultationTimeSlotId.
6. If bookConsultationFromAssessment succeeds, the page stores the returned consultationBooking, updates the view to show a confirmation state with the booked start/end time and channel (all from the API response), and may update the URL parameter step to a value representing a completed booking state.
7. If there is an error (for example, the slot becomes unavailable before booking), the page shows an inline error message and prompts the user to choose another slot, reusing getConsultationTimeSlotsForAssessment if necessary.

Workflow 4 – Using URL parameters and navigation between steps:
1. A user can arrive from index.html with autostart specified. The page immediately initiates getActiveAssessmentQuestions and skips any optional introductory step.
2. As the user completes the assessment, submits it, and then moves into the consultation scheduler, the page updates the step parameter to reflect the current logical section (e.g., step=questions, step=recommendations, step=consultation, step=consultation-confirmed). These changes are done via the browser's history API without reloading the page.
3. If the user modifies the consultationDate or channel controls, the page synchronizes these changes to consultationDate and channel URL parameters, then calls getConsultationTimeSlotsForAssessment using these values. If the user navigates backward/forward using the browser controls, the page re-reads these URL parameters to reconstruct which section to show and what date/channel should be used for the next fetch, within the limits of in-memory state.
4. From the recommendations view, when the user chooses 'Learn more' or 'Book this recommended service', the page navigates to services.html or booking.html using the appropriate URL parameters focus, serviceId, and serviceType derived solely from the selected recommended service data received from submitAssessmentResponsesAndGetRecommendations.
interactions: Click 'Start assessment' button: triggers retrieval of questions via getActiveAssessmentQuestions if they are not already loaded, sets the current step to the first question, and updates the URL step parameter accordingly.

Change of answer on any question: updates the in-memory response for the corresponding questionId, validates the answer according to answerType, and updates the progress indicator but does not yet call any interface.

Click 'Next' in the question flow: validates that the current question(s) have valid responses; if valid, advances the current question index; if moving from the final question, triggers the overall assessment submission handler which calls submitAssessmentResponsesAndGetRecommendations.

Click 'Previous' in the question flow: moves back to the previous question index without losing previously entered answers, does not call any interface, but updates the progress indicator and URL step if applicable.

Submit assessment (e.g., 'See recommendations' button): compiles all stored responses into the responses payload and calls submitAssessmentResponsesAndGetRecommendations. On success, stores assessmentResult and recommendedServices, then switches the visible section to recommendations and updates the URL step parameter.

Click 'Learn more' for a recommended service: reads the selected recommended service's service_type and constructs a navigation URL to services.html with focus equal to that service_type. Uses standard anchor navigation or client-side routing, without any additional API calls.

Click 'Book this recommended service' for a recommended service: reads the selected recommended service's id and service_type and navigates to booking.html with serviceId and serviceType URL parameters derived from the selected service object.

Click 'Schedule a consultation' from the recommendations section: reveals the consultation scheduler component, sets or updates the URL step parameter to a consultation-related value, and initializes or reuses consultationDate and channel URL parameters if present.

Change date in the consultation date picker: updates the consultationDate URL parameter and triggers getConsultationTimeSlotsForAssessment with the newly selected date and the currently selected channel, then updates the time slot list based on the returned data.

Change consultation channel (e.g., select phone or video): updates the channel URL parameter and triggers getConsultationTimeSlotsForAssessment with the current consultationDate and new channel, then refreshes the time slot list.
Click on a consultation time slot: marks that slot as selected in in-page state, optionally updates the selectedSlotId URL parameter, and enables the 'Confirm consultation' action.

Click 'Confirm consultation' after selecting a time slot: calls bookConsultationFromAssessment with the selected consultationTimeSlotId. On success, displays the consultationBooking details and updates the URL step parameter to indicate a confirmed consultation; on failure, shows an error and leaves the scheduler visible for another attempt.

Browser back/forward navigation: when the URL changes, the page reads the current step, consultationDate, channel, and any other defined parameters. It adjusts which section is visible (questions, recommendations, or consultation scheduler), and if appropriate, re-issues a getConsultationTimeSlotsForAssessment call using consultationDate and channel, within the limits of data available in the current page session.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary page state is controlled via a combination of in-memory state and URL query parameters, without using localStorage. The URL parameters are used as follows: 1) autostart: If present when the page first loads with a value indicating automatic start, the page immediately triggers the assessment loading process by calling getActiveAssessmentQuestions and sets the logical step to the question flow. If autostart is absent or indicates no automatic start, the page initially shows a minimal introductory control that allows the user to start the assessment manually. This parameter is only read on initial load or hard refresh. 2) step: Represents the current logical section of the page. Example values can identify the questions, recommendations, consultation selection, or consultation confirmation sections. When the user moves between sections (e.g., from questions to recommendations, or from recommendations to consultation scheduling), the page updates step using the browser history API to keep navigation and the address bar in sync. On any URL change, the page reads step and determines which components should be visible; if the underlying in-memory data needed by that section is not available (for example, refreshing directly into a later step), the page will guide the user to restart the necessary earlier steps because there are no interfaces on this page to reload previous assessment results by id. 3) consultationDate: Stores the currently selected date in the consultation scheduler. Whenever the user selects or changes a date, the page immediately writes the new date to the URL query and calls getConsultationTimeSlotsForAssessment with that date and the current channel. When the page loads or the URL changes and step indicates the consultation section, the page uses consultationDate from the URL (if present) as the initial value for the date picker and for getConsultationTimeSlotsForAssessment. 4) channel: Stores the currently selected consultation channel.
