Whenever the user selects or changes the channel in the scheduler, the page writes the new value to the URL and calls getConsultationTimeSlotsForAssessment with the new channel and current consultationDate. When the page loads or the URL changes in a state where the consultation section should be shown, the channel parameter is used to initialize the channel selector and to control which consultationTimeSlots are fetched. 5) selectedSlotId (optional): May be used to reflect the currently selected consultation time slot in the URL. When the user selects a time slot, this value is set; if the user navigates with browser history while still within the same session, the page can re-select the corresponding slot in the UI by matching the id against the list returned by getConsultationTimeSlotsForAssessment. Because the assigned interfaces for this page do not include a mechanism to re-fetch past assessment results, any page reload will clear in-memory state related to responses, recommendations, and consultation selections. URL parameters therefore only control which UI sections are visible and how the consultation scheduler should fetch and present its data within the active session.

components.functionality: Reads URL parameters (such as autostart, step, consultationDate, channel, and optionally selectedSlotId) on initial load and on history changes, determines which major section of the page should be visible (intro, question flow, recommendations, consultation scheduler, confirmation), and coordinates showing or hiding child components accordingly. It also updates the URL parameters when the user moves between steps, ensuring forward/back navigation is consistent with in-page state.
data_binding: URL query parameters: autostart, step, consultationDate, channel, selectedSlotId
AssessmentQuestion (indirectly, as it determines when assessment questions should be loaded via getActiveAssessmentQuestions)
AssessmentResult (indirectly, as it controls when recommendations are visible)
ConsultationTimeSlot (indirectly, as it controls when scheduler components fetch and show slots)

components.functionality: Initiates and manages the loading of active assessment questions and options. It is invoked when the user starts the assessment (either automatically based on autostart or via a manual action). It calls getActiveAssessmentQuestions once per session, stores the returned questions in memory, and exposes them to the question flow component.

Presents assessment questions sequentially or grouped, captures user answers, manages validation for each question based on answerType, and tracks the user's position through the question set. It maintains an in-memory collection of responses keyed by questionId, and coordinates with the progress indicator and submit controller to enable submission only when all required responses are valid.
data_binding: AssessmentResponse (client-side representation prior to server submission)

components.functionality: Displays the user’s progress through the assessment, such as the current question index relative to the total number of questions. It updates in response to navigation and answer changes in the question flow, and may provide non-editable progress information (for example, a step counter) to reassure users while not calling any interfaces itself.
data_binding: AssessmentResponse (to determine which questions have been answered)

components.functionality: Aggregates all collected responses from the assessment-question-flow component and submits them to the backend via submitAssessmentResponsesAndGetRecommendations. It handles the full submission lifecycle: disabling repeated submissions, showing loading states, processing success and error responses, and passing the returned assessmentResult and recommendedServices to downstream components.
data_binding: AssessmentResponse (client-side structure to be sent as the responses parameter)

components.functionality: Displays the assessment summary text and the list of recommended services returned by submitAssessmentResponsesAndGetRecommendations. For each recommended service, it shows key fields provided by the API response and offers three main interactions: navigating to services.html with a focus parameter for more information, navigating to booking.html with serviceId and serviceType to start booking, and opening the consultation scheduler section to schedule a follow-up consultation.

Allows the user to select the desired consultation date and channel for the consultation related to their assessment. It reads initial values from the URL (consultationDate and channel), updates those parameters when the user makes changes, and triggers fetching of available time slots via getConsultationTimeSlotsForAssessment whenever the date or channel changes.

Displays the list of consultation time slots returned by getConsultationTimeSlotsForAssessment for the selected date and channel. It filters and presents only those slots where isAvailable is true as selectable, highlights the selected slot, and exposes the selected consultationTimeSlotId to the booking component. It can also react to URL changes involving selectedSlotId to reselect the appropriate slot when possible within the active session.

Receives the currently selected consultationTimeSlotId from the consultation-timeslot-list component and, when the user confirms, calls bookConsultationFromAssessment to create a ConsultationBooking tied to the most recent assessment result. It manages loading and error states during booking and passes the booking details to the confirmation display component. On success, it can also trigger a step change in the state router to show a confirmation state.

Shows confirmation details after a successful consultation booking, using the data returned by bookConsultationFromAssessment. It presents the scheduled start and end times, channel, and status of the consultation. It does not call any interfaces itself but listens for updates from the booking controller and adjusts its display accordingly.
data_binding: ConsultationBooking (consultationBookingId, startDatetime, endDatetime, channel, status from bookConsultationFromAssessment)

components.functionality: Displays non-intrusive error messages for failures in any of the interfaces used on this page. For example, if getActiveAssessmentQuestions, submitAssessmentResponsesAndGetRecommendations, getConsultationTimeSlotsForAssessment, or bookConsultationFromAssessment returns an error, this component shows a context-aware message and may provide retry actions. It keeps no static content and uses only error messages or codes derived from the failed interface calls or generic fallback text defined in the application.

layout.grid_system: Implicit single-column layout on a centered max-width container, likely aligned to an 8px or 4px Material grid. Content column roughly 600–720px wide with large outer margins.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a single, centered content column (max-width ~640–720px) with a strictly linear top-to-bottom flow. At the very top of the column sits the global error banner (only when needed), followed by the assessment progress indicator. Below that, the main stateful content appears: either the assessment question flow, the recommendations view with scheduling controls and timeslots, or the booking confirmation. Each of these occupies the same central column, never side-by-side. CTAs for each state are grouped into a horizontal button row at the bottom of that state’s main card, aligned primarily to the right, mirroring the mockup’s CTA pattern.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a single, centered content column (max-width ~640–720px) with a strictly linear top-to-bottom flow. At the very top of the column sits the global error banner (only when needed), followed by the assessment progress indicator. Below that, the main stateful content appears: either the assessment question flow, the recommendations view with scheduling controls and timeslots, or the booking confirmation. Each of these occupies the same central column, never side-by-side. CTAs for each state are grouped into a horizontal button row at the bottom of that state’s main card, aligned primarily to the right, mirroring the mockup’s CTA pattern.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The layout preserves the mockup’s generous negative space. The top 20–25% of the viewport remains mostly blank, with the central column starting visually around that area; the first meaningful element (error banner or progress indicator) sits with ample top padding (~64px). The main content card (question flow, recommendations, or scheduling) occupies the central 40–50% of the viewport height, with wide side margins leaving the column at roughly 40–50% of the viewport width. The lower 15–20% of the viewport is mostly whitespace around the CTA row, giving the primary action extra breathing room. Vertical gaps between sections (banner → progress → card → CTA row) are 24–32px, with 16–24px padding inside cards.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density is intentionally low. Each state shows only the essential elements: a concise heading, short explanatory paragraphs, a compact list (questions, recommendations, or timeslots), and a clearly separated CTA row. Cards have generous internal padding and clear section breaks, avoiding visual clutter. Controls like schedulers and pickers are grouped but not crowded, maintaining Material-style flat surfaces and high contrast while minimizing decorative elements.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow is top-to-bottom and slightly right-weighted at the bottom, mirroring the warning screen. Attention starts at the upper-central element (error banner if present, otherwise the progress indicator or the main card’s heading), moves down through the body text or primary interactive area, then to any secondary text like IDs or notes, and finally lands on the primary CTA at the bottom right of the main card. Each state reinforces this flow: a clear top focus, a calm middle for reading/interaction, and a decisive action area at the bottom.

layout.overall_description: The page sits on a clean, flat background with a single, centered content column approximately 640–720px wide, framed by generous left and right whitespace. Near the top, after a tall band of empty space, a slim error banner (when active) and a simple progress indicator quietly introduce the assessment context. Below them, one main card-like section is visible at a time: during the assessment, a lightly elevated, full-width card presents one question at a time with left-aligned text and plenty of breathing room; after submission, a similar card presents recommended services; when scheduling, another card holds the timeslot picker; and finally, a sparse confirmation state uses the same column to confirm booking. In all cases, the core content lives in the central mid-height band of the viewport, with the bottom portion reserved for a horizontally aligned button row anchored towards the right, giving the primary action visual weight. The overall effect is modern, calm, and utilitarian—everything is clearly stacked in a vertical sequence, easy to scan, and consistent with a Material-inspired, system-style interface.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The state router occupies the full viewport as an invisible structural layer, defining a single centered content column where all visible components appear. It anchors the column in the horizontal center of the page with a max-width of about 640–720px and wide left/right margins, and vertically it respects generous top and bottom padding (~64px top, ~64px bottom). Depending on the current assessment stage, it shows exactly one primary content stack in this column: either the assessment-question-flow beneath the progress indicator, or the recommendations-view plus scheduling components, or the consultation-booking-confirmation. It ensures that only one of these flows is visible at a time and that they always align to the same vertical rhythm and column width, maintaining the linear, single-column layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual orchestration layer; statically defines the central column and switches which child components occupy that space without itself consuming visible layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When active, the loader appears as a centered overlay within the same central column, temporarily replacing the main card content. A semi-transparent scrim can subtly dim the background, while a small, minimalist loading panel is vertically and horizontally centered in the viewport. This panel spans roughly 40% of the column width (around 280–320px), with 16–24px padding and a simple spinner above a short status message, both center-aligned. There is significant empty space above and below, emphasizing the low-density, system-status feel while keeping all other structural elements (such as the global-error-notification or progress indicator) either hidden or visually subdued during loading.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily overlays or replaces the current main content within the central column, fixed in the visual center of the viewport until data loading completes.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The question flow sits in the middle of the central column, beneath the progress indicator, as a full-width, light-surface card. It begins roughly one-quarter down the viewport, leaving generous blank space above. The card spans nearly the entire column width (about 90% of the 640–720px column), with 16–24px internal padding on all sides. Inside, the question title is placed at the top left of the card, followed by a short explanatory line or helper text, then the input controls (radio buttons, sliders, etc.) stacked vertically with 16px gaps. The lower portion of the card, occupying the last 15–20% of its height, is reserved for navigation controls: optional inline validation or hint text appears left-aligned just above a horizontal button row. In that row, the primary button (e.g., “Next” or “Continue”) is aligned to the far right edge of the card, with the secondary action (e.g., “Back”) to its left with a small 8–16px gap, mirroring the mockup’s CTA alignment. Below the card, there is a 32–48px vertical gap before any subsequent content (or the bottom of the page), ensuring a spacious, uncluttered feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the central column, appearing only in the assessment phase. It expands vertically as needed for content but remains clearly separated from other sections by fixed vertical spacers.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The progress indicator is positioned near the top of the central column, directly below any global error banner and above the main question or recommendation card. It sits roughly 64px from the top of the viewport when visible, occupying about 60–70% of the column width and centered horizontally within the column, leaving equal margins on both sides. Vertically, it is a slim element—either a narrow horizontal bar or a step indicator—with about 8–12px height and 8–16px padding around it. A 24–32px vertical gap below the indicator separates it clearly from the assessment-question-flow card, reinforcing the stacked, linear hierarchy. This placement allows users’ eyes to naturally move from the banner (if present) to the progress, then down into the main card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and scrolls with the page; it remains at the top of the visible content column and does not stick to the viewport once scrolled past.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The submit controller has no independent visual container but manifests as subtle inline messages and button state changes within the assessment-question-flow card. Error messages or submission status appear directly above the CTA button row, left-aligned, in a single line or short block of text, with a small 8–12px vertical gap separating them from the buttons. This keeps the card’s overall shape and spacing intact while clearly associating technical or validation feedback with the primary action area at the bottom. The controller never adds new cards or banners; it operates strictly inside the existing card layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual logic that only affects elements inside the question-flow card; it may temporarily introduce small text blocks without changing the column’s overall structure.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The recommendations view replaces the question-flow card in the same central column and alignment. It appears as a vertically oriented content block starting at the same height as the previous card, occupying about 90% of the column width. At the top left of this block, a bold heading such as “Your care recommendations” appears, with a 8–12px gap below it. Next, a short explanatory paragraph describes what the recommendations mean; this text is left-aligned and limited to 2–4 lines to maintain low density. Below this, one or more recommendation items are grouped inside a light-gray card that spans almost the full column width, with 16–24px internal padding and 16px vertical spacing between items. Each item is left-aligned with a clear title and possibly a single line of descriptive text. Beneath the card, a small line of technical or reference text (such as “Assessment ID: #12345”) appears in a lighter style, left-aligned, echoing the small error code in the mockup and separated from the card by about 16px. A 24–32px gap below this block leads into the scheduling controls, maintaining the same vertical rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static main content within the central column, occupying the mid-height band of the viewport and pushing scheduling components downward while preserving generous spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The scheduler controls sit directly below the recommendations view, with a clear 24–32px vertical gap separating them from the last line of recommendation content. They are contained within the same central column width but are visually lighter—no heavy card, just a compact horizontal strip of controls (e.g., date picker, location filter) aligned to the left. The control row occupies roughly 60–70% of the column width, leaving blank space to the right, which contributes to the airy, minimalist feel. Vertical height is modest, with 12–16px internal spacing between labels and fields if they stack on smaller screens. A 24px gap beneath this row separates it from the timeslot list below, making each functional area feel distinct but closely related.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline section in the central column between recommendations and timeslot list; it grows slightly taller if controls wrap but never breaks the single-column structure.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The timeslot list appears below the scheduler controls within the same central column, separated by about 24px of vertical space. It is presented inside a light-surface card that extends nearly the full column width (about 90% of the column), with 16–24px padding. Inside the card, timeslots are arranged in a simple vertical list or in a shallow grid (2–3 columns) but always contained within the single card to maintain the one-column page layout. Each timeslot pill or row has 8–12px of vertical spacing from the next, preserving low density. The lower portion of the card, similar to the assessment card, reserves space for action controls: a primary confirmation button is right-aligned in a horizontal row at the bottom edge of the card, with any secondary action (like “Cancel” or “Skip for now”) placed to its left with a small gap. A 32–48px gap below the card creates a clear buffer before the page bottom or any subsequent status messages, echoing the breathing room seen around the CTA area in the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static main card in the scheduling state, expanding vertically as needed for available timeslots but always centered within the single content column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The booking controller does not introduce a new visual block; instead, it manages micro-states within the timeslot card and its CTA area. When a booking is in progress or fails, short inline messages appear just above the bottom button row inside the timeslot card, left-aligned and constrained to one or two lines, with an 8–12px gap before the buttons. On success, it transitions the view to show the consultation-booking-confirmation in the same column. It may also temporarily disable the primary button or show a subtle loading indicator aligned to the right of that button, all while preserving the card’s shape, padding, and spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual logic layer; it affects the state of components inside the timeslot card and triggers the switch to the confirmation state without changing the structural layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: In the confirmation state, the main content column displays a sparse, centered confirmation block that replaces the recommendations and scheduling components. This block starts around one-quarter down the viewport, leaving substantial blank space above. At the top center of the block, an optional simple icon (e.g., a subtle check mark) sits, followed by a bold heading like “Consultation booked” left-aligned within the column but visually near the center horizontally. Below, a short paragraph (2–3 lines) confirms the details and next steps, with key items (such as date/time) highlighted in a slightly stronger weight. A small reference code or summary line appears further down, echoing the mockup’s technical code. At the bottom of this block, a horizontal button row runs along the lower edge of the content area: the primary action (e.g., “Back to home” or “View visit details”) is placed at the far right of the column, with a secondary action (like “Book another consultation”) to its left, separated by a small gap. There is ample whitespace (32–64px) below this row before the end of the viewport, reinforcing the quiet, complete-state feeling.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, final-state content centered within the central column; it occupies a relatively short vertical height and is surrounded by generous whitespace, similar to a system confirmation screen.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The global error notification appears at the very top of the central content column, just below the main page top padding. It spans the full width of the column (640–720px), forming a slim, high-contrast banner with 12–16px vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding. Text is left-aligned and concise, possibly with a small error icon on the far left. When visible, it pushes all other content (progress indicator, main cards) downward, preserving a 16–24px gap below the banner before the next element. The overall presentation mirrors a system-style warning bar: flat color, no heavy shadows, and clear separation from the rest of the content, but still aligned to the same central axis as everything else.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky to the top of the viewport within the central column while active, so as the user scrolls, it remains pinned to the top edge until dismissed or the page is refreshed, ensuring that the error state is always visible.

name: Resources & Blog
title: Resources & Blog
description: Browse, filter, search, and open educational articles about home nursing care, including a dedicated view for saved resources.

core_features: Load dynamic filter options (categories, date ranges, sort options) via getArticleFilterOptions and initialize the filter controls and defaults.
Search and filter the list of articles using searchArticles based on URL-driven category, date range, sort order, and search query.
Provide a view switch between all resources and saved/bookmarked articles, controlled by the URL parameter `view` and powered by getSavedArticles.
Display article summaries in a list; clicking an article card navigates to article.html with the article slug as a URL parameter.
Keep all filter, search, sort, and view settings encoded in the URL parameters so that the current state is shareable and supports browser back/forward navigation.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1 – Default browse (no URL parameters):
1. User navigates to resources.html without URL parameters.
2. Page controller calls getArticleFilterOptions.
3. Page controller derives default filter values from the returned options (e.g., first date range and first sort option) and sets no category filter.
4. Page controller calls searchArticles with (categorySlug = null, dateRangeCode = default, sortOrder = default, query = null).
5. Articles list displays returned articles; filters and search field show the current default state.

Workflow 2 – Deep-linked filtered view (with URL parameters from index.html):
1. User clicks a link on index.html that forwards them to resources.html with some combination of category, dateRange, sort, and query parameters.
2. On load, page controller reads URL parameters and treats them as the initial state for category, dateRange, sort, and query.
3. Page controller calls getArticleFilterOptions, then aligns selected filter controls to the corresponding codes/slugs from the URL (if they exist in the options; otherwise falls back to defaults).
4. Page controller calls searchArticles with (categorySlug = category from URL or null, dateRangeCode = dateRange from URL or default, sortOrder = sort from URL or default, query = query from URL or null).
5. Filters and search field show the preselected values; article list shows the matching results.

Workflow 3 – Adjusting filters and search on the page:
1. User changes category, date range, or sort option in the filter bar, or types a term in the search field and submits.
2. Filter bar component emits a filter-change or search-submit event with the new filter values.
3. Page controller updates the URL parameters (category, dateRange, sort, query) to reflect the new state and removes `view` if it was set to `saved` (to ensure the user is in "all resources" mode when applying filters).
4. Page controller calls searchArticles again with the updated parameters.
5. Articles list refreshes to show the new results; the browser’s address bar reflects the new filter/search state.

Workflow 4 – Viewing saved articles (from article.html or directly):
1. User arrives on resources.html with URL parameter view=saved (e.g., by following a link from article.html), or switches to the saved view using the view toggle.
2. Page controller sets its internal view mode to "saved" based on the URL parameter.
3. Page controller calls getSavedArticles.
4. Article list displays the saved articles collection returned by getSavedArticles, ignoring category/dateRange/sort/query for the listing logic (these parameters may remain in the URL but are not used when view = 'saved').
5. If the user switches back to the all-resources view, the page controller removes or alters the `view` parameter and re-calls searchArticles using the current or default filter values.

Workflow 5 – Opening an article from the list:
1. User clicks on an article card in the article list (either from all resources or saved view).
2. Article list component looks up the clicked article’s slug.
3. Browser navigates to article.html with a URL parameter slug set to the article’s slug value.
4. The article detail page is loaded and displays the full article content (handled by article.html).

Workflow 6 – Using browser back/forward navigation:
1. User changes filters, search query, or view mode several times; each change updates the URL.
2. User presses the browser back or forward button.
3. Page controller listens for URL changes, re-parses the category, dateRange, sort, query, and view parameters, and synchronizes its internal state and UI controls.
4. Based on `view`, the page controller calls either searchArticles or getSavedArticles.
5. The rendered article list and filter controls update to match the URL state.
interactions: On page load: read URL parameters (category, dateRange, sort, query, view), call getArticleFilterOptions, decide current view mode, then call either searchArticles or getSavedArticles.
On filter change (category/dateRange/sort) in the filter bar: update corresponding URL parameters, clear any `view` parameter, then call searchArticles with the new filter state.
On search input submit: update the `query` URL parameter (or remove it if cleared), clear any `view` parameter, then call searchArticles with the updated query.

On clear-all-filters/reset action: remove category, dateRange, sort, and query parameters from the URL (or set them to default values derived from getArticleFilterOptions), clear any `view` parameter, then call searchArticles with the reset filters.

On view mode toggle (e.g., switching between all resources and saved resources): update or remove the `view` URL parameter; if `view` becomes `saved`, call getSavedArticles; if `view` is removed or changed away from `saved`, call searchArticles using the current filter state.
On article card click: navigate to article.html with the selected article’s slug in the URL (e.g., article.html?slug=<slug>).
On URL change due to browser navigation: re-parse URL parameters and re-invoke either searchArticles or getSavedArticles so that the displayed state matches the URL.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page uses URL parameters as the single source of truth for filters, search query, sort order, and view mode. On initialization, the page reads `category` (mapped to categorySlug for searchArticles), `dateRange` (mapped to dateRangeCode), `sort` (mapped to sortOrder), `query` (mapped to query), and `view`. After calling getArticleFilterOptions, the page validates that `category`, `dateRange`, and `sort` correspond to available options; any invalid values are ignored and replaced with defaults derived from the returned options (e.g., the first item in each options list when applicable). When `view` equals a value that represents the saved view (such as 'saved'), the page uses getSavedArticles and does not call searchArticles for the main article listing; when `view` is absent or any other value, the page calls searchArticles with the URL-derived filters and query. User interactions that alter category, dateRange, sort, or query always update the corresponding URL parameters and remove `view` when it was set to the saved view, ensuring that applying filters always refers to the all-resources list. Switching the view mode updates the `view` parameter without modifying the existing filter parameters (they may remain in the URL but are ignored when in saved view). The page listens for URL changes (including browser back/forward) and re-invokes the appropriate interface (searchArticles or getSavedArticles) so that the rendered article list and filter controls always reflect the current URL state. No persistent client storage such as localStorage is used; all sharable and restorable state is held in the URL query string.

components.functionality: Coordinates page initialization, URL parameter parsing, data fetching via getArticleFilterOptions, searchArticles, and getSavedArticles, and distributes state (filters, view mode, article lists) to child components.

Displays and manages the filter controls (category selector, date range selector, sort selector) and the search input, using dynamic options from getArticleFilterOptions and current selections derived from URL parameters and controller state.
Allows the user to switch between viewing all resources and viewing only saved resources. It reflects and updates the `view` URL parameter and triggers the appropriate data fetch.
Shows contextual information about the current result set, such as the count of articles in the current list and a textual summary of active filters based on the controller state.

Renders a list of article cards using data from either searchArticles (for all-resources view) or getSavedArticles (for saved view). Each card includes the article title, summary, primary category name (if available), and publish date. Clicking a card navigates to article.html with the article slug as a URL parameter.

Displays contextual messaging when there are no articles in the current list, either because filters/search returned no matches or because there are no saved articles. Suggests adjusting filters or switching view modes as appropriate.

Shows or hides loading status while getArticleFilterOptions, searchArticles, or getSavedArticles requests are in progress. It may distinguish between loading filters and loading articles based on controller state.
Displays error messages when calls to getArticleFilterOptions, searchArticles, or getSavedArticles fail, and can provide a retry action that re-invokes the respective interface.

layout.grid_system: Implicit single-column layout on a centered max-width container, likely aligned to an 8px or 4px Material grid. Content column roughly 600–720px wide with large outer margins.
layout.layout_pattern: All components sit in a single, vertically stacked content column centered in the viewport. The page uses a linear top-to-bottom flow: page controller at the top, followed by the filter bar and view toggle as a control cluster, then the result summary as a small technical line, and finally the main content area (article list, or empty/loading/error states) occupying the central and lower portion of the page. Text is left-aligned within the central column, but the column itself is horizontally centered.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: All components sit in a single, vertically stacked content column centered in the viewport. The page uses a linear top-to-bottom flow: page controller at the top, followed by the filter bar and view toggle as a control cluster, then the result summary as a small technical line, and finally the main content area (article list, or empty/loading/error states) occupying the central and lower portion of the page. Text is left-aligned within the central column, but the column itself is horizontally centered.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: A full-width, light background extends across the viewport with a centered content column of roughly 600–720px (about 40–50% of the viewport width on desktop). The top 20–25% of the initial viewport is mostly generous whitespace with the page controller occupying a relatively small, centered area. The middle 40–50% holds the filters, view toggle, result summary, and the upper part of the article list or state indicators. The bottom portion shows the continuation of the list; on short result sets, it appears more open and airy. Major vertical gaps between component blocks follow a 24–32px rhythm, with large top and bottom padding (~64px) framing the entire column.
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout is intentionally low-density: each component has generous top and bottom spacing, and internal padding respects a Material-like 8px grid (12–16px vertically, 16–24px horizontally for any card-like regions). There are no dense toolbars or cramped multi-column sections; instead, controls are grouped into a simple, uncluttered cluster. The article list uses comfortable spacing between items, making scanning easy and maintaining a calm, professional feel appropriate for a home nursing care context.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow starts near the top center with the page controller (title and optional icon or short description), then moves down to the filter bar, continues to the right-aligned view toggle, then to the small result summary line, and finally drops into the main content area (article list or empty/loading/error state). When error or empty states appear, they are centered in the main content area, adopting a card-like, system-style notice that naturally becomes the focal point, similar in hierarchy to a warning screen. Overall, the eye travels from top-center into left-aligned text and then down through increasingly interactive elements.

layout.overall_description: The Resources & Blog page appears as a clean, centered column on a full-width, light neutral background. At the top, after a generous band of empty space, the page title and brief description (page controller) sit in a narrow column, left-aligned but visually centered in the viewport. Below this, a simple, flat filter bar stretches across the column like a discreet card, with enough padding to feel substantial but not heavy. Just beneath and aligned to the right edge of the same column, a compact view toggle allows switching between list or saved views, keeping controls visually grouped but not cramped. A subtle result summary line follows, left-aligned and relatively small, acting like a technical sub-label for the current state of the content. The rest of the page is dominated by the main content area: either a vertically stacked list of article cards, each with comfortable breathing room, or a centered empty, loading, or error state that echoes system-style alert layouts. Throughout, the page maintains a restrained, utilitarian aesthetic with clear hierarchy and generous whitespace, supporting a calm and trustworthy reading experience.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page controller is positioned near the top of the centered content column, starting roughly 64px–80px from the top of the viewport. It spans the full width of the content column (about 600–720px max, or roughly 40–50% of the viewport on desktop) and is horizontally centered on the page. Inside this region, all text is left-aligned. A subtle system-style icon or small label (if used) appears at the top-left of this block, with the main page title immediately below, set in a bold, larger typographic style that visually anchors the page. A short supporting description sits under the title in regular weight, forming a concise multi-line paragraph. The entire page-controller block occupies only a small vertical portion of the screen, leaving plenty of blank space above and around it. There is a vertical gap of about 32px beneath this block before the filter bar begins, preserving the sparse, formal feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, participates in normal document flow; remains at the top of the scrollable column and scrolls away naturally as the user moves down the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter bar sits directly below the page controller with approximately 32px of vertical separation. It spans the full width of the central content column and is horizontally centered in the viewport. Visually it reads like a flat, light-gray, system-style card: a simple rectangular band with 12–16px vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding on both sides, following the Material 8px grid. Filter controls inside the bar are arranged horizontally where space allows, but the overall bar preserves a clean, uncluttered appearance with room between each control. There is a clear 16–24px gap between the bottom edge of this bar and the next element (the view toggle), ensuring the filter area feels distinct and self-contained rather than integrated into the list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in the main flow; it scrolls with the page and does not stick to the top, maintaining the simple linear layout of the original mockup.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The view toggle is placed immediately below the filter bar, separated by about 16–24px of vertical space. It occupies only as much horizontal space as needed for its toggle controls (e.g., segmented buttons for 'All resources' vs 'Saved'), and is aligned to the right edge of the central content column. This creates a subtle right-anchored control cluster that balances the left-aligned text but still feels part of the same section as the filter bar. The view toggle itself has compact internal padding (8–12px vertical, 12–16px horizontal), clean edges, and flat colors to match the minimalist, system-style aesthetic. Below the view toggle, there is approximately 16px of vertical spacing before the result summary line appears, maintaining clear separation while keeping these controls visually grouped.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and inline with the document flow; it sits as a discrete control row and scrolls normally with the rest of the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The result summary appears as a slim text line directly beneath the view toggle, with about 16px of vertical separation. It is left-aligned to the central column and spans the full width of that column, but its text content is short—usually a single line indicating the number of results, active filters, or the current view. Typographically it is smaller and lighter than the page title, resembling the small technical error code line in the original mockup. This gives it a tertiary, informational feel rather than drawing too much attention. There is a more generous gap of about 24–32px between the bottom of the result summary and the start of the article list (or any state indicator), which visually transitions from the control section to the main content area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in the vertical stack; it scrolls with the page and always precedes the main content area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article list occupies the main body of the page, starting approximately 24–32px below the result summary. It fills the entire width of the central content column, with each article represented as a flat or lightly outlined card spanning nearly the full column width, leaving only small internal insets consistent with the 8px grid (e.g., 16px horizontal padding inside each card). Cards are stacked vertically with 16–24px of vertical spacing between them, creating a calm, readable rhythm. The initial portion of the list sits around the central 40–50% of the first viewport, with additional items naturally extending below and introducing scrolling. There are no competing sidebars or secondary columns: all attention in this section is on the linear list of resources. If the user scrolls, this article-list region expands to dominate the screen while the header and filters move off-screen, preserving the single-column, top-to-bottom reading flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Primary scrollable content. It extends vertically as needed and defines the main scroll height of the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When there are no resources to display, the empty state occupies the same spatial region as the article list, starting where the list would normally begin (about 24–32px below the result summary). Instead of multiple cards, a single, vertically centered block is shown within the central content column. This block is horizontally centered and constrained to the same 600–720px width. At the top of this block, a neutral or softly colored system-style icon appears at the horizontal center, occupying a modest portion of the mid-page area, echoing the warning icon placement of the original mockup. Beneath the icon, a bold but modest-sized heading is left-aligned within the column, followed by a short, multi-line explanatory paragraph that clearly states that no resources match the filters or that saved resources are empty. Optionally, a subtle supporting line (similar to an error code) may appear below as a smaller hint. If there is a suggested action (e.g., 'Clear filters'), it appears as a single primary button centered horizontally beneath the text, with generous top margin (24–32px). The overall empty-state block sits visually in the middle of the viewport if content height is short, surrounded by ample whitespace that emphasizes the message.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Mutually exclusive with the article list for a given state: it replaces the list content area. It is static and scrolls with the page, but is typically short enough to appear mostly within a single viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loading indicator appears in the main content area where the article list would be, centered within the central column. When active, it is placed roughly 48–64px below the result summary line so it sits visually in the central band of the viewport. The indicator itself (spinner or progress bar) is horizontally centered, with a short, single-line label beneath it in a small, neutral type style. There is generous whitespace above and below the indicator, avoiding any clutter while loading. If loading is the only state (initial load), no list items appear behind it; the indicator functions as the sole content in that region, echoing the sparse, utilitarian feel of a system status screen.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally shown in place of the list content region. It does not float or overlay the entire page; instead, it sits inline within the flow between the result summary and where list items would appear.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: On error, the error notification replaces the article list area with a system-style error block that mirrors the structure of the original warning mockup. It is positioned starting about 24–32px below the result summary and centered within the main content column. At the top of this block, a prominent but flat, minimalist error icon (e.g., a red or high-contrast symbol) is centered horizontally, occupying a noticeable but not overwhelming portion of the mid-page area—similar in scale to the warning icon reference. Directly below the icon, a bold error heading is left-aligned within the column, followed by a multi-line explanatory paragraph that clearly states that the resources could not be loaded, possibly highlighting key technical terms or domains in bold. A smaller, tertiary line beneath this acts like a technical error code or short diagnostic note. Finally, a row of CTAs is placed below the text: typically a primary button such as 'Retry' aligned towards the right side of the column, with an optional secondary action (e.g., 'Go to home') to its left, following a simple horizontal button row with ample space around it. Generous vertical padding (24–32px) separates each subsection (icon, heading, body text, code, and button row), recreating the sparse, formal rhythm of a system warning screen.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Mutually exclusive with the article list and empty state. It appears inline in the main flow where the list would be and can extend vertically as needed, maintaining a single-column, scrollable layout.

name: Article Detail
title: Article Detail
description: Displays a single resource article with full content, lets users save it, copy a shareable link, and browse related articles.

core_features: Load and display full article details (title, publish date, author, content, primary category) based on the 'slug' URL parameter using getArticleDetail.
Show an article action toolbar with a Save/Bookmark control that calls saveArticle to store the article in the user's saved resources collection.
Provide a Share control with a 'Copy link' action that calls getArticleShareLink, then copies the returned URL to the clipboard.
Offer navigation back to the broader topic/category list via a 'Back to category' or 'More in this topic' link that routes to resources.html with category and sort URL parameters.
Display a list of related articles (from getArticleDetail.relatedArticles) and allow navigation to another article detail by updating the slug URL parameter.
Handle loading and error states when fetching article data, including missing slug or failed API responses.
user_workflows:
View an article from Resources or Home:
1. User clicks an article card on resources.html or index.html.
2. Browser navigates to article.html?slug={selectedArticleSlug}.
3. On page load, the Article Data Loader reads the slug URL parameter.
4. The page calls getArticleDetail(articleSlug = slug) and enters a loading state.
5. On success, the article header, content body, toolbar, back-to-category link, and related articles list are populated from the response.
6. User scrolls and reads the article content.

Save/bookmark an article:
1. While viewing an article, user clicks the Save/Bookmark button in the article action toolbar.
2. The toolbar calls saveArticle(articleId = article.id).
3. While waiting, the Save button shows a saving state (e.g., disabled or with a spinner).
4. On success, the toolbar updates local page state to mark the article as saved and may show confirmation text using the message and savedAt/totalSaved fields.
5. On failure, the toolbar shows an error message and keeps the article as not saved.

Copy a shareable link:
1. User clicks the 'Copy link' button in the article action toolbar.
2. The toolbar calls getArticleShareLink(articleId = article.id).
3. On success, the component takes the returned shareUrl and writes it to the clipboard via the browser clipboard API.
4. A transient feedback message is shown to indicate success.
5. If the API call or clipboard write fails, an error feedback message is shown instead.

Return to category / more in this topic:
1. User clicks the 'Back to category' or 'More in this topic' link near the article header.
2. The component reads article.primaryCategorySlug (or equivalent category-identifying field exposed by getArticleDetail).
3. Browser navigates to resources.html?category={article.primaryCategorySlug}&sort=most_popular.
4. The Resources page then shows articles filtered to that category, sorted by most popular.

Open a related article from the current article:
1. At the end or side of the article, user sees a list of related articles loaded from getArticleDetail.relatedArticles.
2. User clicks a related article card.
3. Browser navigates to article.html?slug={relatedArticle.slug}.
4. The Article Detail page reloads (or re-runs its data loader), reads the new slug, and calls getArticleDetail(articleSlug = newSlug).
5. The view updates with the newly selected article and its related articles.
interactions:
Initial page load:
- Trigger: Page load or navigation to article.html with slug in the query string.
- Behavior: Article Route Guard validates presence of slug. If present, Article Data Loader calls getArticleDetail(articleSlug = slug) and sets loading state; on success, article and relatedArticles are stored in page state; on error, error state is shown.

Missing slug:
- Trigger: Page load when no slug URL parameter is present.
- Behavior: Article Route Guard prevents API calls, sets an error state, and displays a message with guidance to go back to the Resources or Home page (via standard navigation, not additional APIs).

Click Save/Bookmark:
- Trigger: User clicks Save/Bookmark button in Article Action Toolbar.
- Behavior: If not already saving, call saveArticle(articleId = currentArticle.id). Show saving state until response returns. On success, update local saved state and surface message from response.message. On failure, display an error message and allow retry.

Click Copy Link:
- Trigger: User clicks 'Copy link' button in Article Action Toolbar.
- Behavior: Call getArticleShareLink(articleId = currentArticle.id). On success, attempt to write shareUrl to clipboard. Show success or failure feedback via Clipboard Feedback Toast. No navigation occurs.

Click Back to category / More in this topic:
- Trigger: User clicks the back-to-category or more-in-topic link.
- Behavior: Read the primary category identifier from article (e.g., article.primaryCategorySlug) and navigate to resources.html?category={article.primaryCategorySlug}&sort=most_popular using standard browser navigation (URL change).

Click Related Article Card:
- Trigger: User clicks a related article card in the Related Articles List.
- Behavior: Read relatedArticle.slug from the clicked card. Navigate to article.html?slug={relatedArticle.slug}. This causes a full or partial reload; the Article Data Loader then fetches the new article via getArticleDetail.

Scroll / read interactions:
- Trigger: User scrolls through the article content.
- Behavior: No additional API calls; only the visible portion of content changes. Optional internal events may be used to support analytics, but no new interfaces are invoked on this page.

Error retry:
- Trigger: API failure notification shown by Article Error State, user clicks a 'Retry' action.
- Behavior: The page re-invokes getArticleDetail(articleSlug = currentSlug) and returns to loading state.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The primary driver of page state is the 'slug' URL query parameter on article.html.

- URL to state mapping:
  - When article.html loads or the browser navigates to it, the page reads the 'slug' query parameter from the URL.
  - If slug is present and non-empty, the Article Data Loader calls getArticleDetail(articleSlug = slug) and sets internal state: { loading: true, error: null, article: null, relatedArticles: [] }.
  - On successful response, state becomes { loading: false, error: null, article, relatedArticles }, where article and relatedArticles are bound directly to UI components.
  - If getArticleDetail fails or returns no article, state becomes { loading: false, error: { type: 'load_failed' | 'not_found' }, article: null, relatedArticles: [] }.

- State changes due to navigation:
  - Clicking a related article card triggers navigation to article.html?slug={relatedArticle.slug}. This updates the slug URL parameter, which resets the page's state (loading, article, relatedArticles) and causes a new getArticleDetail call.
  - Clicking Back to category does not change this page's state; it navigates away to resources.html with category and sort parameters. If the user returns via the browser back button, slug remains unchanged and the article state is restored or re-fetched depending on implementation.

- Save/Bookmark state:
  - Initial saved state cannot be derived from the provided interfaces and is therefore initialized as 'unsaved' when the article loads.
  - When the user clicks Save, the page calls saveArticle(articleId = article.id). On success, a local in-memory flag (e.g., articleSaved = true) is set for the current session, and supplementary data like savedAt and totalSaved may be displayed.
  - This saved state is not persisted via URL parameters and is not read from other APIs; it resets if the page is reloaded or the user navigates away and back.

- Share link state:
  - The share URL is not stored in the URL parameters. Each time the user presses 'Copy link', the page calls getArticleShareLink(articleId = article.id) to obtain the current shareUrl.
  - A temporary UI state (e.g., clipboardStatus = 'success' | 'error' | null) is used to show feedback via a toast and then reset after a short duration.

- Loading and error views:
  - While loading is true, the Article Loading Indicator shows and the primary content components may be hidden or disabled.
  - If error is set, the Article Error State component displays appropriate messaging and a retry option, which re-uses the current slug from the URL to re-call getArticleDetail.

No use is made of localStorage or other persistent client storage; all state is derived from the URL parameter 'slug', API responses from getArticleDetail, and transient in-memory flags for the current session.

components.functionality: Validates that the 'slug' URL parameter is present for article.html and controls whether data loading proceeds or an error state is shown.
event_handlers: onInit: Read the 'slug' query parameter from the URL. If missing or empty, set a global/page error state (e.g., error.type = 'missing_slug') and prevent getArticleDetail from being called.
onParamChange: When the URL changes (e.g., via navigation within SPA), re-read 'slug'. If slug changed, clear previous article state and allow Article Data Loader to fetch new data.

components.functionality: Fetches full article content and related articles from the backend using getArticleDetail based on the slug URL parameter, and exposes loading, error, article, and relatedArticles to other components.
event_handlers: onReadyToLoad: Triggered when Article Route Guard confirms a valid slug. Calls getArticleDetail(articleSlug = slugFromUrl). Sets state.loading = true and state.error = null before the call.
onLoadSuccess: Receives the response object with properties { article, relatedArticles }. Sets state.article and state.relatedArticles, then sets state.loading = false.
onLoadError: If getArticleDetail fails or returns an invalid response, sets state.error = { type: 'load_failed', details: errorInfo } and state.loading = false.
onRetry: When invoked (e.g., from Article Error State), re-calls getArticleDetail(articleSlug = currentSlugFromUrl) using the same logic as onReadyToLoad.

components.functionality: Displays the article's title and primary metadata such as author name, publish date, and primary category label, using data loaded by the Article Data Loader.
event_handlers: onArticleChange: When state.article changes from null to a loaded Article, bind title, author_name, publish_datetime, and any available primary category fields (e.g., primaryCategoryId, primaryCategorySlug, or primaryCategoryName) to the view.

components.functionality: Provides a contextual link such as 'Back to category' or 'More in this topic' that navigates the user back to the Resources list filtered by the article's primary category and sorted by most popular.
event_handlers: onArticleChange: When article is available, read the primary category identifier (e.g., article.primaryCategorySlug or a mapping derived from article.primaryCategoryId and category information provided by getArticleDetail). Construct the target URL resources.html?category={article.primaryCategorySlug}&sort=most_popular.
onClick: Navigate the browser to the constructed Resources page URL. No additional interface calls are made.

components.functionality: Renders the main body of the article, including full text content and any structured content segments provided in article.content.
event_handlers: onArticleChange: When a new article object is loaded, bind article.content (and optionally article.summary) to the main content area.
onScroll: Optionally used for internal state or analytics; does not trigger any interfaces.

components.functionality: Displays primary actions for the current article: Save/Bookmark and Share (Copy link). Manages transient saved state and triggers saveArticle and getArticleShareLink.
event_handlers: onArticleChange: Reset local toolbar state when a new article is loaded (e.g., articleSaved = false, saveInProgress = false, shareInProgress = false, lastSaveMessage = null).
onClickSave: If article is loaded and saveInProgress is false, set saveInProgress = true and call saveArticle(articleId = article.id).
onSaveSuccess: When saveArticle returns successfully, set articleSaved = true, saveInProgress = false, and store savedArticleId, savedAt, totalSaved, and message for display in the UI.
onSaveError: When saveArticle fails, set saveInProgress = false and update a local error field (e.g., saveErrorMessage) for display; allow user to retry.
onClickCopyLink: If article is loaded and shareInProgress is false, set shareInProgress = true and call getArticleShareLink(articleId = article.id).

onCopyLinkSuccess: When getArticleShareLink returns, attempt to write response.shareUrl to the clipboard. If clipboard write succeeds, set shareInProgress = false and dispatch an event to Clipboard Feedback Toast indicating success.
onCopyLinkClipboardError: If the clipboard write fails, set shareInProgress = false and dispatch an event to Clipboard Feedback Toast indicating error.
onCopyLinkApiError: If getArticleShareLink fails, set shareInProgress = false and dispatch an error event to Clipboard Feedback Toast.

components.functionality: Shows related articles returned in relatedArticles from getArticleDetail, each as a selectable card that navigates to its own Article Detail page via slug.
event_handlers: onRelatedArticlesChange: When state.relatedArticles updates, render a list of cards bound to each related Article (title, summary, publish_datetime, etc.).

onClickRelatedArticleCard: When a card is clicked, read relatedArticle.slug from that card and navigate to article.html?slug={relatedArticle.slug}, which triggers the Article Route Guard and Article Data Loader for the newly selected article.

components.functionality: Displays a loading state while article data is being fetched by getArticleDetail.
event_handlers: onLoadingStateChange: Observe state.loading from Article Data Loader. When true, show the loading indicator and optionally hide content components; when false, hide the loading indicator.

components.functionality: Shows appropriate error messages when the article cannot be loaded (missing slug, API error, or article not found) and provides a retry action where applicable.
event_handlers: onErrorStateChange: Observe global/page error state (e.g., error.type). If error.type is 'missing_slug', show a missing-article message with guidance to navigate back. If error.type is 'load_failed' or 'not_found', show corresponding messages.
onClickRetry: For recoverable errors (e.g., 'load_failed'), invoke Article Data Loader's onRetry handler to re-call getArticleDetail(articleSlug = currentSlugFromUrl).

components.functionality: Shows transient feedback messages after a copy-link attempt (success or error) initiated by Article Action Toolbar.
event_handlers: onCopyLinkSuccessEvent: When Article Action Toolbar emits a success event, display a success toast for a short duration, then auto-hide.

onCopyLinkErrorEvent: When Article Action Toolbar emits an error event (API error or clipboard failure), display an error toast with retry guidance and auto-hide after a duration or on user dismissal.
onDismiss: User manually closes the toast, which clears any active feedback state.

layout.grid_system: Implicit single-column layout on a centered max-width container, likely aligned to an 8px or 4px Material grid. Content column roughly 600–720px wide with large outer margins.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a strict single-column layout centered on the page, with all components stacked vertically in a linear top-to-bottom flow. The main content column spans roughly 40–50% of the viewport width on desktop (max-width ~640–720px), centered horizontally. Text inside this column is left-aligned, but the column itself is visually centered. Route-guard, loading, and error states each occupy the main column as alternative states, not alongside the article content. In the default article state, the order is: back-to-category link at the top of the column, then the article header, then the article body, then a light-gray related-articles card, and finally a horizontal action toolbar at the bottom.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a strict single-column layout centered on the page, with all components stacked vertically in a linear top-to-bottom flow. The main content column spans roughly 40–50% of the viewport width on desktop (max-width ~640–720px), centered horizontally. Text inside this column is left-aligned, but the column itself is visually centered. Route-guard, loading, and error states each occupy the main column as alternative states, not alongside the article content. In the default article state, the order is: back-to-category link at the top of the column, then the article header, then the article body, then a light-gray related-articles card, and finally a horizontal action toolbar at the bottom.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Maintain generous top and bottom whitespace and wide side margins. The top 20–25% of the viewport is mostly negative space with the back link and article header occupying a relatively small area near the top-center. The central 40–50% of the viewport height is dedicated to the article body and related-articles card. The lower 15–20% is reserved for the action toolbar and breathing room below it. On wider screens, the central content column remains fixed-width while side gutters expand to preserve a narrow reading band. Vertical gaps between major sections are in the 24–32px range, with top and bottom page paddings in the 64px+ range.
layout_strategies.content_density: Keep content density intentionally low: generous line height for text, consistent 24–32px separation between key components (header, body, related card, toolbar), and ample padding inside any cards or toolbars (12–16px vertical, 16–24px horizontal). Avoid clutter around the article body—no dense sidebars or multiple columns. Related articles are grouped into a single card block rather than scattered, and the toolbar uses a small number of clear actions with sufficient spacing between buttons.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Visual flow remains top-to-bottom and centered: the eye first lands on the top-centered column with the subtle back link and bold article title, then moves down into the article body text. After the body, attention shifts to the light-gray related-articles card, which is visually separated and slightly elevated with padding. Finally, the eye settles on the right-aligned primary action (e.g., Save or Back) in the action toolbar at the bottom of the column. In error or guarded states, a prominent icon and heading at the top of the column mimic the original warning-screen flow: icon → heading → explanation → technical code (if any) → recommendation card (if used) → primary action button row.

layout.overall_description: The page presents a calm, minimalist reading experience: a full-width, neutral background with a single, narrow content column centered on the screen. At the top, after a generous band of whitespace, a subtle back-to-category link and a strong, left-aligned article title introduce the page, both sitting inside the centered column. Below, the article body flows in a comfortable line length with plenty of white space between sections, giving the content a safe, uncluttered feel suitable for home nursing care topics. After the main text, a softly framed light-gray card appears containing related articles, visually separated but clearly part of the same linear narrative. At the bottom of the column, a horizontal toolbar aligns key actions (like saving or copying a link) to the right, echoing the dual-CTA row from the mockup with generous spacing around the buttons and extra whitespace below. When loading or error states occur, the article content is replaced by centered status elements that follow the same single-column structure and vertical rhythm, while a small bottom-floating toast briefly appears when the user copies a link, maintaining the minimalist, system-like feel of the original design.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The route-guard appears as a full-viewport blocking state when triggered, dimming or replacing the underlying article detail content. It uses the same centered single-column layout as the warning mockup: a max-width ~640px column is centered horizontally and vertically biased slightly upward (its top edge starting around 20–25% down from the top of the viewport). At the top of this column, a prominent warning or info icon is centered, followed by a bold heading left-aligned within the column but still visually in the center band of the page. Below that, a short explanatory paragraph and optional technical note stack with 24–32px gaps. If the guard offers an alternative (e.g., go back to articles), that appears in a light-gray card centered in the same column, and a horizontal row of action buttons sits at the bottom of the column, right-aligned within the column width. Side margins remain wide, leaving the guarded message floating in the center of the page with ample negative space.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed overlay covering the full viewport, centered column with vertical stacking; it prevents interaction with the underlying page while active.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The data-loader governs the presence of loading, error, or content states but is itself visually minimal. When active and no article data is yet available, it dedicates the same centered main column area to a loading indicator instead of the article content. The loader positions its content (spinner and optional text) at the vertical center of the viewport, inside a max-width ~640px column that is horizontally centered, maintaining the wide side gutters and clean, single-column appearance. There is abundant blank space above and below the loading indicator, echoing the low-content-density system-like feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual controller component; when in a loading state, it centers its child loading indicator in place of the usual page content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article header sits near the top of the main content column, following a generous top margin (roughly 64px from the top of the viewport or page content start). It is left-aligned within a centered column that spans about 40–50% of the viewport width (max-width ~640–720px). The header includes the article title in a bold, large type at the very top of this column, with any meta information (date, reading time, category) placed directly beneath it with 8–16px spacing, preserving the minimalist, system-like typography. There is 24–32px of vertical space between the header and the start of the article body, making the header clearly distinct without any decorative separators.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrolls naturally with the page as part of the main content column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The back-to-category link is positioned directly above the article header within the same centered column, aligned to the left edge of that column. It sits in the upper portion of the content area, roughly 16–24px above the article title, using a subdued text style that is still clearly legible but visually lighter than the title. This link does not stretch full width; it occupies only as much horizontal space as the text needs, leaving plenty of whitespace to the right. The placement at the top-left of the main column creates a clear starting point for the user’s reading flow, while the ample whitespace above reinforces the minimalist, calm aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, part of the main content flow; remains at the top of the column and scrolls off-screen as the user scrolls down.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article content body occupies the central band of the page within the same centered main column, starting 24–32px below the article header. It spans the full width of the column but maintains a comfortable line length due to the column’s constrained width (~640px). Paragraphs are left-aligned with generous line spacing and 16–24px vertical gaps between sections or subheadings, keeping the density low. There are no sidebars or multi-column layouts; any inline images or callouts are also constrained to this column width and centered horizontally within it. The body continues downward, consuming most of the vertical space in the mid-section of the viewport, before giving way to the related-articles card below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrollable content within the main column, expanding vertically as needed while preserving fixed horizontal bounds.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The action toolbar is located near the bottom of the scrolling content column, positioned after the related-articles card with a 32px vertical gap above it. It is a horizontal strip that spans the full width of the main column but remains visually light—no heavy framing, possibly just subtle padding of 12–16px vertical and 16–24px horizontal. Within this strip, action buttons (e.g., Save article, Copy link, Back to category) are arranged from right to left, with the primary action (e.g., Save or Back to safety) placed at the far right. Buttons are large, with comfortable spacing (8–16px) between them, mirroring the CTA row from the mockup. There is additional empty space beneath the toolbar (at least 48–64px) before the end of the page content, emphasizing the finality of the actions and keeping the layout airy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, part of the scrollable content; not sticky. It naturally becomes visible as the user reaches the bottom of the article.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The related-articles list is presented as a self-contained card-like section directly below the article body, separated by about 32px of vertical space. The card aligns with the main content column (same width and centered position) but sits on a light-gray background with slightly rounded corners or a subtle outline, echoing the recommendation card from the mockup. Inside the card, the title (e.g., 'Related articles') is left-aligned at the top with 16–24px padding from the card edges, followed by a vertically stacked list of related items with 12–16px spacing between each. Each related article entry is left-aligned, uses simple text with minimal embellishment, and does not break the single-column pattern. The card has 16–24px padding on all sides, and there is a 24–32px gap between the bottom of the card and the action toolbar below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, integrated into the main content flow as a distinct but inline block-level card.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When displayed, the article-loading indicator appears in place of the article header, body, related card, and toolbar, occupying the center of the main column. The indicator—typically a spinner or progress bar—is centered horizontally within the max-width ~640px column and vertically around the middle of the viewport, with generous negative space above and below. Any accompanying text (e.g., 'Loading article…') is placed directly beneath the spinner with 8–12px spacing, left-aligned within the column but visually near the page center. No other content competes for attention, preserving the minimalist, system-status feel of the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Ephemeral, replaces core page content while loading; vertically centered within the viewport and scroll-locked in typical use (since content is minimal).

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article error state occupies the main column in place of the usual article content when a load or fetch error occurs. It mimics the warning layout: at the top of the centered ~640px column, a small to medium alert icon (error or warning) appears near the top-center band of the viewport, followed by a bold error heading left-aligned within the column but sitting visually in the upper-middle of the screen. Below, a concise explanatory paragraph and, optionally, a small technical error code are stacked with 16–24px spacing. If recovery actions are available (such as a 'Retry' or 'Back to category' button), they appear in a horizontal row at the bottom of this column, right-aligned and separated by 8–16px, similar to the CTA row. The rest of the page around this column remains empty, creating a clear, system-like error screen within the article layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main content area but effectively becomes the sole significant element on the page while active.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The clipboard feedback toast appears as a small, unobtrusive system-style notification overlay near the bottom of the viewport, not within the main content column but visually aligned with it. It is horizontally centered relative to the column (or slightly right of center) and positioned about 24–32px above the bottom edge of the screen. The toast is a compact rectangular bar with subtle elevation and 8–12px vertical and 16–24px horizontal padding, containing a single line of left-aligned text such as 'Link copied'. It does not stretch full width; instead, it is just wide enough to fit its content comfortably. The toast floats above any page content, including the action toolbar, without obscuring key controls due to its small size and transient appearance.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporary floating overlay fixed to the viewport near the bottom center/right; automatically appears and disappears without affecting page layout or scroll position.

name: Care Cost Estimator
title: Care Cost Estimator
description: Interactive page where users configure a weekly home care plan by selecting services and hours, see an automatically calculated weekly cost against a target budget, and submit their contact details to request a personalized estimate based on the current plan.

core_features: Load estimator configuration (including available services, their descriptions, base hourly rates, currency, and a default weekly budget) using getCareCostEstimatorConfig on page initialization.

Allow users to build and edit a weekly care plan by selecting services and specifying hours per week for each selected service, then send the current selection to buildWeeklyCarePlanEstimate to create or update a draft estimate.
Display a real-time summary of the draft weekly care plan estimate, including total hours, total weekly cost, and per-service line items returned by buildWeeklyCarePlanEstimate.

Provide budget input and guidance by comparing the total weekly cost from buildWeeklyCarePlanEstimate with a target budget derived from the budgetPerWeek URL parameter or the defaultBudgetPerWeek returned by getCareCostEstimatorConfig.
Enable users to iteratively adjust service hours (and add or remove services) while seeing immediate recalculation of the total weekly cost and budget status.

Collect user contact details (full name, email, phone) in a form and submit them via submitEstimateRequest, which uses the current weekly care plan estimate, then show an inline confirmation with the response from submitEstimateRequest.

Manage the estimator state (selected services, hours per week, target budget, preset key) without localStorage, while using URL parameters to initialize and optionally reflect the current configuration so the estimator can be revisited with the same context.
user_workflows:
Workflow: Start a weekly care plan from configuration or preset
1. User navigates to cost_estimator.html, optionally with preset and/or budgetPerWeek parameters in the URL.
2. The page-init controller reads URL parameters and calls getCareCostEstimatorConfig.
3. After configuration loads, the controller determines the initial target budget using the budgetPerWeek URL parameter if provided, otherwise using the defaultBudgetPerWeek from getCareCostEstimatorConfig.
4. If a preset parameter is present, the controller applies preset-specific initialization rules to generate an initial set of selected services and hours per week; otherwise, it starts with an empty plan or a minimal default plan as defined by global configuration.
5. The controller calls buildWeeklyCarePlanEstimate with the initial services selection to obtain a draft estimate (including estimateId, totals, and service items).
6. The service-selection form and weekly summary panel render the returned estimate so the user can see the starting point of their weekly care plan.

Workflow: Adjust services and hours to align with a weekly budget
1. The user views the weekly plan summary panel showing total weekly cost and total hours, plus a budget input field displaying the current target budget.
2. If the current total weekly cost is above the target budget, the budget guidance component indicates that the estimate exceeds the budget and may suggest reducing hours or removing services.
