3. The user interacts with the service-selection form to add an additional service, change hours for an existing service, or remove a service entirely.
4. Each change triggers buildWeeklyCarePlanEstimate with the updated list of services and hours per week.
5. The estimator response updates the weekly summary panel and the budget guidance component, which recalculates the difference between totalWeeklyCost and the target budget.
6. The user repeats adjustments until they are satisfied with the combination of services, hours, and estimated weekly cost relative to their target budget.
7. At any point, the user can update the target budget via the budget input; this immediately updates the budget guidance without needing to call buildWeeklyCarePlanEstimate, since the underlying plan does not change.

Workflow: Submit contact details to request a personalized estimate
1. Once the user is satisfied with their weekly care plan estimate, they scroll to or navigate to the contact details form section.
2. The contact form is enabled only when a draft estimate exists (i.e., after at least one successful call to buildWeeklyCarePlanEstimate), ensuring submitEstimateRequest can be associated with an existing estimate.
3. The user enters their full name, email address, and phone number into the contact form fields.
4. On form submission, the estimate-request form validates required fields on the client side (e.g., non-empty values and basic formatting checks) and, if valid, calls submitEstimateRequest with fullName, email, and phone.
5. While the request is in progress, the form is temporarily disabled and a submission state is shown to prevent duplicate submissions.
6. If submitEstimateRequest returns success, the controller moves the page into a confirmation state and passes the estimateRequestId and message to the confirmation component for display.
7. If submitEstimateRequest indicates failure, the form displays an error message and allows the user to retry after correcting any issues.
8. After a successful submission, the user can optionally change the plan and submit another request (if business rules allow) or navigate away; the internal estimate state remains available for the current session until the page is reloaded.
interactions: On page load, the page-init controller reads the preset and budgetPerWeek URL parameters (if present), triggers getCareCostEstimatorConfig, and then initializes the selected services, hours per week, target budget, and calls buildWeeklyCarePlanEstimate to create the first draft estimate.

When the user selects a service from a service dropdown or list in the service-selection form and clicks an 'add' control, a new service item with an initial hours-per-week value is added to the client-side selection and buildWeeklyCarePlanEstimate is called with the updated list.

When the user changes the hours per week for a selected service (via a numeric input, stepper, or similar control), the service-selection form updates the local selection model and calls buildWeeklyCarePlanEstimate with the modified list of services.

When the user removes a selected service, the service-selection form updates the selection to exclude that service and calls buildWeeklyCarePlanEstimate with the reduced list, resulting in updated totals and line items.

When buildWeeklyCarePlanEstimate returns, the weekly-plan summary panel updates its bound fields (total hours, total weekly cost, currency, and per-service line items), and the budget guidance component recalculates whether the estimate is under, on, or over the current target budget.

When the user edits the target budget value in the budget input, the budget guidance component updates its internal representation of the target budget and recomputes the budget status and any guidance messages, without making additional API calls.

When the user focuses or clicks on a line item in the weekly-plan summary panel, the user can be scrolled or navigated to the corresponding service-hours control in the service-selection form to make adjustments (purely client-side interaction).

When the user starts entering contact details, the estimate-request form reads the current estimate state (e.g., presence of an estimateId from the last buildWeeklyCarePlanEstimate call) to determine whether submission is allowed; if no estimate exists, it prompts the user to select services first.

On contact form submission, client-side validation is performed; if valid, submitEstimateRequest is called. The submission button is disabled and a loading state is shown until a response is received.
On successful submitEstimateRequest, a confirmation component displays the returned message and estimateRequestId, and the contact form transitions to a submitted state (e.g., disabled or collapsed).
On failed submitEstimateRequest, the contact form remains visible with an error state, allowing the user to correct inputs and resubmit.

Whenever the selected services, hours, preset key, or target budget change, the URL-state-sync component can update corresponding URL parameters using the history API, so refreshing or sharing the URL preserves or reconstructs the estimator state, subject to parsing logic on page initialization.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page uses a combination of URL parameters and in-memory state, without localStorage, to manage the estimator lifecycle. On initial load, the controller parses the preset and budgetPerWeek parameters from the query string. It then calls getCareCostEstimatorConfig to retrieve available services, currency, and a default weekly budget. The initial target budget is set either from budgetPerWeek (if present and valid) or from the configuration's defaultBudgetPerWeek. If a preset parameter exists, the controller applies preset-specific initialization rules (defined outside this page's static code) to create an initial list of service selections (service identifiers and hours per week). If no preset is supplied, the controller can start from an empty or minimal default selection, again determined dynamically. This initial selection is passed to buildWeeklyCarePlanEstimate, which returns a draft WeeklyCarePlanEstimate, including an estimateId, total hours per week, total weekly cost, currency, status, and a list of WeeklyCarePlanServiceItem objects. These values populate the service-selection form and weekly-plan summary panel. Subsequent interactions (adding/removing services, changing hours) update the in-memory selection and trigger new calls to buildWeeklyCarePlanEstimate, replacing the previous estimate state with the latest response. The contact form remains disabled until at least one valid estimate has been created, ensuring submitEstimateRequest operates on an existing estimate managed internally by the estimator logic. The submitEstimateRequest call relies on this current estimate context; the UI does not manually pass the estimateId. On success, the page transitions to a 'submitted' state for the current estimate and surfaces the estimateRequestId. Throughout the session, whenever key fields (such as the active preset, the target budget, or a compact representation of selected services and hours where feasible) change, the URL-state-sync component can update the query string via history.replaceState, enabling the estimator to reconstruct a similar state on refresh or when the URL is revisited. No state is persisted to localStorage or other client-side storage; only the URL and in-memory variables control the current view.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial page load: reads URL parameters, retrieves estimator configuration, determines the starting target budget and plan preset, initializes selected services and hours per week, and triggers the first buildWeeklyCarePlanEstimate call. Maintains the central in-memory state for the current weekly care plan estimate and exposes it to other components.

Allows users to select one or more care services and specify hours per week for each selected service. Maintains a local list of selected services and hours, and sends this list to buildWeeklyCarePlanEstimate whenever it changes. Receives updated items and totals from the controller and synchronizes displayed values (such as hours per week) with the latest estimate.

Displays the current weekly care plan estimate as returned by buildWeeklyCarePlanEstimate, including total hours per week, total weekly cost, currency, and a breakdown of per-service weekly costs and hours. Provides interaction hooks (e.g., clicking a line item) that focus corresponding controls in the service-selection form.

Manages the user's target weekly budget and provides guidance on how the current estimate compares to that budget. Initializes with the budgetPerWeek URL parameter or the defaultBudgetPerWeek from getCareCostEstimatorConfig. Calculates and displays the difference between the current totalWeeklyCost and the target budget, and exposes simple guidance messages or indicators for under-budget, on-target, or over-budget states.

Collects user contact details (full name, email, phone) for the current weekly care plan estimate and submits them using submitEstimateRequest. Ensures that an active draft estimate exists before allowing submission. Handles basic client-side validation before calling the interface and manages loading, success, and error states for the submission process.

Displays confirmation details after a successful submitEstimateRequest call, including the message and estimateRequestId returned by the interface. Indicates that the estimate request has been submitted and the team will follow up using the provided contact details. Provides optional actions to adjust the plan further or to start a new estimate session, depending on business rules.

Synchronizes key parts of the estimator state with the URL query string so that the page can be reloaded or revisited with the same configuration. Reads preset and budgetPerWeek on initialization and, after changes to preset, target budget, or the selected services model (when feasible), updates the URL parameters via the browser history API without reloading the page.

layout.grid_system: Implicit single-column layout on a centered max-width container, likely aligned to an 8px or 4px Material grid. Content column roughly 600–720px wide with large outer margins.
layout.layout_pattern: All interactive elements sit in a single, vertically stacked content column that is centered on the page. The column uses a max-width of about 600–720px (roughly 40–50% of the viewport on desktop) with wide, equal left and right margins. At the very top, outside any specific component, a simple nursing-care themed icon and the page title/intro mimic the mockup’s warning icon and heading block. Directly beneath that header block, the estimator components appear in linear order: the service-selection-form is first, followed by the weekly-plan-summary-panel, then the budget-input-and-guidance, then a self-contained estimate-request-form card, and finally a horizontal row of primary and secondary actions at the very bottom of the column (tied to the estimate-request-form). Non-visual components (page-init-controller, url-state-sync) do not render UI and therefore do not interrupt this linear flow.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: All interactive elements sit in a single, vertically stacked content column that is centered on the page. The column uses a max-width of about 600–720px (roughly 40–50% of the viewport on desktop) with wide, equal left and right margins. At the very top, outside any specific component, a simple nursing-care themed icon and the page title/intro mimic the mockup’s warning icon and heading block. Directly beneath that header block, the estimator components appear in linear order: the service-selection-form is first, followed by the weekly-plan-summary-panel, then the budget-input-and-guidance, then a self-contained estimate-request-form card, and finally a horizontal row of primary and secondary actions at the very bottom of the column (tied to the estimate-request-form). Non-visual components (page-init-controller, url-state-sync) do not render UI and therefore do not interrupt this linear flow.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The top 20–25% of the viewport is mostly whitespace, occupied only by the centered icon and page title/description, giving a calm entry into the tool. The middle 50–60% of the viewport contains the main interactive estimator controls: service selection, summary, and budget, all within the narrow central column. The estimate-request-form card and its CTAs occupy the lower 20–25%, with extra breathing room above and below the button row to echo the mockup. Vertical gaps between major sections (between each main component) follow a 24–32px rhythm, while the very top and bottom of the page maintain larger margins (~64px+) before any content and after the final buttons. All components span essentially the full width of the content column, never stretching into full viewport width.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density stays low throughout: each component is visually simple, with one clear purpose and generous spacing between fields and text blocks. The service-selection-form and budget-input-and-guidance are grouped into concise, single-column fields with clear separation between logical groups. The weekly-plan-summary-panel uses limited, high-value information (totals, key highlights) rather than a detailed table, and the estimate-request-form is presented as a clean, minimalist card with a handful of fields and ample padding. The design avoids side-by-side form fields or compact, spreadsheet-like layouts; instead it prioritizes clarity, legibility, and a sense of safety appropriate to a care service.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: User attention is guided from top to bottom: first the icon and page title at the top center, then immediately into the service-selection-form as the main interactive starting point. After configuring services, the eye naturally drops to the weekly-plan-summary-panel, which quickly clarifies the impact of their choices. From there, the flow continues to the budget-input-and-guidance block, which helps them evaluate affordability. Below that, the estimate-request-form card appears as an optional, but visually distinct, next step similar to the card in the mockup. Finally, the bottom-aligned horizontal CTA row provides a clear endpoint, with the primary action button visually dominant and positioned at the bottom right of the content column, mirroring the mockup’s call-to-action emphasis. When visible, the estimate-request-confirmation panel replaces the form content in the same space, maintaining the same vertical flow and avoiding any disruption in the column structure.

layout.overall_description: The Care Cost Estimator page looks like a calm, minimalist system screen inspired by Material Design: a light, neutral background with a single, centered content column floating in generous whitespace. At the very top, there is a large amount of empty space before a simple nursing-care icon sits at the center, just above a bold, left-aligned page title (“Care Cost Estimator”) and a short explanatory sentence. This header block is centered within the column but the text itself is left-aligned, matching the mockup’s look. Below this, the main estimator components stack one after another, always aligned to the same narrow column. The service-selection-form occupies the upper-middle portion, followed by a concise weekly-plan-summary-panel that reflects the chosen services and costs. Slightly below that, a budget-input-and-guidance section helps users compare their plan to their budget. The lower-middle of the page is anchored by a light-gray estimate-request-form card with comfortable padding, clearly separated from the background as an optional but trustworthy next step. At the bottom of this card—and effectively at the bottom of the page’s main content band—a horizontal row of two buttons appears, right-aligned: a solid primary button for submitting the estimate request and a lower-emphasis secondary button for alternative navigation, echoing the mockup’s button row. The entire layout feels airy, with consistent vertical gaps, no sidebars, and a smooth top-to-bottom reading path that keeps users focused on one decision at a time. Invisible infrastructure components (page-init-controller, url-state-sync) do not alter spacing or visual hierarchy, so the page remains a clean, uninterrupted single-column experience.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component does not render any visible UI. Conceptually, it sits at the top of the page hierarchy, just before the visible header icon and title section, but it occupies zero visual space: no height, no width, and no background. It should not affect the spacing between the top of the viewport and the first visible elements. Its presence is purely functional—initializing estimator data and page state—so the visual layout remains entirely defined by the other components and the global header block.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-rendered utility component with no spatial footprint; it behaves as an invisible controller that does not influence layout flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The service-selection-form is the first visible interactive block below the page icon/title header. It is centered within the page but constrained to the single content column, spanning roughly 90–100% of the column width (max 600–720px), leaving wide margins on both sides. There is a generous vertical gap of about 32–40px between the header text and the top of this form, preserving the airy feel. Inside the form, fields are arranged in a simple top-to-bottom stack: section heading at the top, then explanatory text (if any), then grouped inputs (service checkboxes, hour sliders, dropdowns) each occupying the full column width. Logical groups of fields are separated by 16–24px vertical spacing, and the entire form has internal vertical padding of about 16px at the top and bottom to separate it from adjacent sections. The form’s background generally blends into the page background (no heavy card border), keeping it feeling integrated with the main flow rather than isolated, but it may use subtle dividers or light section labels. The bottom of the form sits about 24–32px above the weekly-plan-summary-panel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level element that participates in normal document flow; it scrolls with the page and does not stick or float.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The weekly-plan-summary-panel appears immediately below the service-selection-form, aligned to the same centered content column and matching its width. A vertical gap of around 24–32px separates it from the form, clearly signaling that this is a distinct, interpretive layer rather than more input fields. Visually, the panel is a clean, low-density summary area: a short label or title at the top (e.g., “Your weekly plan”), followed by one or two key data points (total hours, estimated weekly cost) and minimal supporting text. Content inside is left-aligned, with each line or row separated vertically by about 8–16px. The panel’s background can either remain the same as the page or use a very subtle tint, but it should be lighter and less visually framed than the estimate-request-form card below, ensuring it reads as part of the core tool rather than an ancillary feature. The panel’s height is moderate—typically occupying about 1/6 to 1/5 of the visible content column height—so it can be scanned quickly. A 24–32px gap below this panel leads into the budget-input-and-guidance section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level element flowing within the column; no sticky behavior, always appearing just below the service-selection-form as the user scrolls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The budget-input-and-guidance component is positioned directly under the weekly-plan-summary-panel, maintaining the same column alignment and width. It begins after a vertical gap of roughly 24–32px, preserving the rhythm of distinct sections. The top of this block has a concise heading (e.g., “Your weekly budget”) aligned left, followed by a single-line or very short multi-line instructional paragraph in smaller text, then the main budget input field (e.g., a numeric input or slider) spanning most of the column width. The input is visually prominent within this section but still simple, with plenty of whitespace around it. Any guidance or feedback text (e.g., “Your plan is slightly above your budget”) appears directly below the input, left-aligned and separated by about 8–12px of vertical spacing. Overall, this block is compact, occupying about the same vertical space as or slightly less than the weekly-plan-summary-panel, and it ends about 24–32px above the estimate-request-form card, making that card feel like the next, optional step after setting a budget.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Regular block element that scrolls with the page; it stays in sequence between the summary panel and the estimate-request-form and does not exhibit sticky or floating behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The estimate-request-form is presented as a distinct, self-contained card that visually mirrors the optional feature card from the mockup. It sits below the budget-input-and-guidance block with a 32–40px vertical gap, which makes it feel like a separate, optional step. The card is centered in the same content column and spans 100% of that column width up to a max of about 600–720px. It uses a light-gray or subtly tinted background with rounded or slightly softened corners, a subtle shadow or border, and internal padding of roughly 16–24px on all sides. At the top of the card is a bold section title (e.g., “Request a personalized estimate”) and a short, low-density description (one or two lines), both left-aligned. Below this, the contact fields (name, phone, email, preferred contact time) are arranged in a single vertical stack, each field taking full card width and separated vertically by about 16px. There are no side-by-side inputs; instead, spacing and typography ensure clarity. The lower portion of the card contains the action area: a horizontal button row aligned to the bottom right of the card. This row typically has a primary button (e.g., “Send request”) on the right and a lower-emphasis secondary action (e.g., “Skip for now” or “Back to plan”) to its left, separated by about 8–16px. There is generous vertical padding (16–24px) between the last input field and the button row, and an additional 24–32px of padding below the buttons, mimicking the breathing room in the mockup’s CTA area. The bottom of the card sits 48–64px above the page’s lower edge, maintaining a calm, not cramped, finish.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Block-level card in normal flow. When the user scrolls, it moves naturally with the page. On submission, its internal content may be conditionally replaced by the estimate-request-confirmation while preserving the card’s position and dimensions.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The estimate-request-confirmation panel appears in the same spatial slot as the estimate-request-form card, effectively replacing its inner content while preserving the card’s outer dimensions and position. When active, the card’s header area shows a clear confirmation title (e.g., “Request sent”) with a simple, reassuring icon or status mark aligned near the top-left inside the card, mirroring the mockup’s use of a strong icon and heading but at a smaller, card-scaled size. Below the title, a short paragraph (two to three lines) explains what happens next (e.g., response time, follow-up details), left-aligned and surrounded by generous white space. Any additional information, like a reference number, is presented as a small, low-key line of text near the bottom of the card content, similar in visual weight to the mockup’s technical error code. In most cases, the CTA buttons in the card’s lower-right corner remain but change purpose (e.g., “Back to care plan” as primary, “Close” as secondary), keeping the familiar horizontal button row. The vertical spacing inside the card remains consistent: 16–24px from top border to title, 8–16px between title and paragraph, and ample padding below the text before the button row. The card still sits roughly 48–64px above the page bottom, maintaining an airy, final state.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally rendered block that occupies the same layout slot as the estimate-request-form’s inner content. It does not change the card’s overall footprint; it appears after form submission and scrolls normally with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component is non-visual and does not occupy any space in the layout. It synchronizes the estimator’s state with the URL behind the scenes. Conceptually, it is attached to the page’s root or wrapped around the main content column, but it introduces no additional padding, margins, or visible elements. The vertical stacking and spacing between all visible components (forms, panels, card, buttons) remain unaffected by its presence.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Invisible, zero-footprint utility component that neither affects nor participates in the spatial flow of the visible UI.

name: About Us
title: About Us
description: Displays dynamically loaded information about the home nursing care provider, including mission, values, history, team credentials, and accreditations, and provides a path to contact the team.

core_features: Load About Us content via getAboutUsContent on page initialization and make it available to all sections (mission, values, history, teamHighlights, accreditations).
Display the organization’s mission in a dedicated section when mission data is present in the getAboutUsContent response.
Render a structured list of organizational values using the values array from getAboutUsContent, adjusting layout based on the number of values returned.
Show the organization’s history text from getAboutUsContent with a collapsible 'preview/full' behavior controlled by URL parameters.
List team highlights from the teamHighlights array, including each member’s name, role, credentials, and bioSnippet, with an option to expand individual bios.
Present accreditations from the accreditations array with name and description fields, emphasizing credibility and compliance.
Provide in-page navigation that lets users jump to specific sections (mission, values, history, team, accreditations) and syncs the current section to URL parameters.
Offer contextual 'Contact our team' calls to action that navigate to contact.html with URL parameters indicating the source and context section.
user_workflows: Workflow: First-time visitor reading About Us — 1) User opens about.html without URL parameters. 2) about-content-loader calls getAboutUsContent and waits for a successful response. 3) Once data is loaded, mission, values, history, teamHighlights, and accreditations sections render. 4) User scrolls or uses the in-page navigation to move between sections. 5) If the user wants to reach out, they click a 'Contact our team' CTA, which opens contact.html with source=about and fromSection set to the currently viewed section.

Workflow: Direct link to a specific section — 1) User opens about.html with a section parameter (e.g., about.html?section=team). 2) url-state-manager reads the section parameter and sets the initial active section state. 3) about-content-loader fetches data using getAboutUsContent. 4) After data loads, the page scrolls to or focuses the requested section (e.g., team-highlights-list) and visually indicates it as active. 5) User may change sections using the navigation, which updates the section parameter in the URL without requiring a full page reload.

Workflow: Viewing a specific team member in detail — 1) User navigates to about.html with section=team and teamMember set to an encoded identifier derived from a team member’s name, or selects a team member from the team-highlights-list. 2) about-content-loader calls getAboutUsContent and passes the result to team-highlights-list. 3) team-highlights-list resolves the teamMember parameter against the teamHighlights array and expands the matching member’s card by default. 4) User can click on other team members to expand or collapse their bios; each click updates the teamMember parameter to reflect the current selection. 5) From an expanded team member card, the user can click a 'Contact our team' CTA, which opens contact.html with source=about and context=teamMember along with the encoded team member identifier.
interactions: User clicks an in-page navigation link (e.g., 'Team') in about-section-navigation; url-state-manager updates the section query parameter accordingly, and the page scrolls to the relevant section.

User clicks a 'Read more' control in history-section to expand the full history text; url-state-manager updates a historyView parameter (e.g., historyView=full), so reloading the page preserves the expanded state.

User clicks on a team member card in team-highlights-list; the selected card toggles between collapsed and expanded states, and the teamMember URL parameter is set or cleared to match the current selection.

User clicks a 'Contact our team' CTA from any section; contact-cta-global constructs a URL to contact.html that includes source=about and fromSection equal to the section where the click occurred, then triggers navigation.
If getAboutUsContent fails to load (network or server error), about-content-loader displays an error state with a retry control; when the user clicks retry, getAboutUsContent is called again.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page reads and writes several URL parameters to manage state without using localStorage. The 'section' parameter (e.g., about.html?section=mission|values|history|team|accreditations) indicates which section should be active on load: if present and matching a known section, the corresponding component scrolls into view and is highlighted; if absent or invalid, all sections render and the page treats the top section as active. The 'teamMember' parameter identifies a specific team member to auto-expand within team-highlights-list; it is derived from the team member’s name (for example, by encoding or normalizing the name) and is resolved against the teamHighlights array; if no matching member is found, the parameter is ignored. The 'historyView' parameter controls whether history-section starts in preview or full mode, with values such as 'preview' or 'full'; if missing, the component defaults to preview mode for long content and full mode for shorter content based on content length. Optional additional parameters such as 'view' can be used by components like values-list to choose between compact or detailed display modes; components must treat unknown or missing parameters by falling back to safe defaults. When users interact with navigation links, read-more controls, or team member cards, url-state-manager updates the corresponding parameters using pushState or replaceState to avoid a full reload. All components derive their current state from the URL on initialization and subscribe to URL changes so that bookmarking or directly sharing URLs reproduces the same About Us view.

components.functionality: Initializes the About Us page data by calling getAboutUsContent once on page load or on user retry, manages loading and error states, and exposes the resulting content object (mission, values, history, teamHighlights, accreditations) to child components via a shared data context or props.

Reads initial state from the URL query parameters (section, teamMember, historyView, view), provides this state to other components, and updates the URL when components request state changes, ensuring that the current view is bookmarkable and shareable.

Displays navigation controls for available About Us sections (e.g., mission, values, history, team, accreditations) based on which fields are present in the getAboutUsContent response, indicates the currently active section using URL.section, and requests URL updates and scrolling when a user selects a section.

Renders the mission text from getAboutUsContent.mission when available, exposes an anchor so it can be scrolled into view when URL.section=mission, and can notify url-state-manager when it becomes the active section (e.g., via intersection observer).

Displays the list of organizational values from getAboutUsContent.values. The component can render values in compact or expanded form depending on URL.view (for example, compact mode might show a subset with a control to reveal all values), and can participate in active-section logic when URL.section=values.

Displays the organization’s history from getAboutUsContent.history, initially in preview or full mode based on URL.historyView and content length heuristics. Provides user controls to expand or collapse the full text and informs url-state-manager to update historyView so that the chosen view persists across reloads and sharable URLs. Also participates in the active-section tracking when URL.section=history.

Iterates over getAboutUsContent.teamHighlights to render a list or grid of team members showing name, role, credentials, and a bioSnippet. It expands the team member whose identifier matches URL.teamMember, if any. User clicks on a team member card to toggle its expanded state; the component then requests url-state-manager to set or clear URL.teamMember. It also works with URL.section, becoming the active section when section=team.

Renders each accreditation from getAboutUsContent.accreditations, showing accreditation name and description. Supports active section highlighting when URL.section=accreditations and can be targeted via in-page navigation.

Provides reusable 'Contact our team' calls to action that can be placed in mission-section, history-section, team-highlights-list, and accreditations-list. When clicked, it constructs a navigation URL to contact.html including query parameters such as source=about, fromSection (derived from the section where the CTA is rendered or clicked), and optionally a teamMember identifier when invoked from an expanded team member card.

layout.grid_system: Implicit single-column layout on a centered max-width container, likely aligned to an 8px or 4px Material grid. Content column roughly 600–720px wide with large outer margins.
layout.layout_pattern: All content lives in a single, vertically stacked column centered on the page. The main content column has a max width of about 640–720px and sits in the middle of the viewport with generous left and right margins, following the mockup’s single-column, centered pattern. The scrolling order is: (1) the about-content-loader (only visible while loading), (2) the mission-section as the primary introductory block, (3) the about-section-navigation as an in-page quick-jump strip, then (4) values-list, (5) history-section, (6) team-highlights-list, (7) accreditations-list (treated visually as a card), and (8) the contact-cta-global as the final bottom call-to-action. All text is left-aligned within the centered column, and each component occupies its own vertical slice with clean separation.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: All content lives in a single, vertically stacked column centered on the page. The main content column has a max width of about 640–720px and sits in the middle of the viewport with generous left and right margins, following the mockup’s single-column, centered pattern. The scrolling order is: (1) the about-content-loader (only visible while loading), (2) the mission-section as the primary introductory block, (3) the about-section-navigation as an in-page quick-jump strip, then (4) values-list, (5) history-section, (6) team-highlights-list, (7) accreditations-list (treated visually as a card), and (8) the contact-cta-global as the final bottom call-to-action. All text is left-aligned within the centered column, and each component occupies its own vertical slice with clean separation.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Roughly the top 20–25% of the viewport is mostly blank, with the mission-section heading emerging in the upper-middle of the screen, mirroring the mockup’s large top breathing room. The central 40–50% of the viewport is dominated by the mission, values, history, team, and accreditation content, all kept within the narrow column and separated by 24–32px vertical gaps. The accreditations-list is placed in a light card with extra internal padding (16–24px) to echo the mockup’s card. The lower 15–20% of the initial scroll shows the contact-cta-global with generous vertical spacing above and below, giving the final action plenty of breathing room. The content column itself takes about 40–50% of the viewport width on large screens, keeping side margins wide and clean.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density remains low. Each section (mission, values, history, team, accreditations) has short paragraphs or concise lists with clear line spacing and ample whitespace between them (24–32px). The accreditations card and the final contact CTA receive particularly generous padding to visually separate them from the rest of the content and to emphasize safety and trust. No component feels crowded; there are no side-by-side columns or dense tables, only vertically stacked, easy-to-scan blocks.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye is drawn first to the mission-section heading in the upper center of the content band (taking the place of the mockup’s warning icon + heading), then moves down to the mission body. Immediately below, the about-section-navigation provides a compact, system-like strip of in-page links, similar in subtle hierarchy to the mockup’s small technical line. From there, the eye follows the strictly vertical flow: values-list, then history-section, then team-highlights-list. The accreditations-list, visually wrapped in a light card, attracts attention next as a trust-focused block. Finally, the user’s gaze settles on the contact-cta-global at the bottom right of the column, which mirrors the mockup’s strong primary button at the lower part of the layout. Overall, the flow is top-center to mid-left text, down through a single column, ending in a clear, right-weighted primary action.

layout.overall_description: The About Us page is a clean, minimalist, single-column layout centered on a neutral background, echoing a modern system-style screen. A tall band of whitespace at the top lets the page breathe before the content column begins. The main content column—about 640–720px wide—is centered horizontally and runs vertically down the page, with all text left-aligned within it. Near the upper-middle of the viewport, the mission section introduces the provider with a clear, bold heading and a short, calm block of text. Just beneath it, a slim in-page navigation row lists the key sections (Mission, Values, History, Team, Accreditations) in a simple, utilitarian style. As the user scrolls, they encounter a sequence of well-spaced sections: core values as a vertical list, a concise history block, and a list of team highlights, all separated by consistent 24–32px vertical gaps. Slightly below the midpoint of the page, the accreditations are grouped inside a light gray, softly outlined card that visually stands out while remaining understated, underscoring trust and compliance. At the bottom of the content column, with generous whitespace above and below, a prominent right-aligned contact action appears, styled like a primary button, giving a clear end to the reading flow and an obvious path to reach the team. Everything scrolls linearly, with no sidebars or competing focal points, maintaining a calm, trustworthy, and highly legible experience.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-content-loader appears centered within the same main content column used by the page content. While loading, it occupies roughly the central 40–60% of the viewport height, starting slightly below the top whitespace zone (about 64–96px from the top of the viewport). It spans the full width of the column (up to about 640–720px) but remains centered on the page with wide side margins. The loader can present as a vertical stack of skeleton lines or blocks that mimic the shapes of the mission heading, short paragraphs, and lists, aligned left within the column. There is clear vertical spacing between skeleton blocks (16–24px), preserving the low-density feel. When loading completes, this component disappears entirely, revealing the mission-section at the same vertical position.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Overlays or replaces the main content within the central column while active, centered horizontally, and scrolls with the page as a standard block; not fixed to the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The url-state-manager is a non-visual, structural component with no direct presence in the layout. It does not occupy any explicit space in the content column and does not alter spacing between visible components. Functionally, it sits conceptually behind the about-section-navigation and the content sections, ensuring that scrolling and anchor states are reflected in the URL, but visually the page appears unchanged whether it is present or not.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-rendering utility component with no spatial footprint; it does not affect layout flow, positioning, or scrolling behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-section-navigation sits within the central column, directly below the introductory part of the mission-section. After the main mission heading and its initial paragraph, there is a vertical gap of about 24–32px, and then the navigation appears as a slim, horizontal row of text links or pills. It spans nearly the full width of the content column (around 90–100% of the column width) and is centered on the page, but the labels themselves are left-aligned within the bar, echoing the mockup’s left-aligned text in a centered container. Vertically, the navigation takes up a compact band (around 40–56px tall), with 12–16px of padding above and below the link row. There is another 24–32px of whitespace below this navigation before the values-list starts. On small screens, the nav remains a single horizontal line that can wrap into two lines if necessary, maintaining a linear feel rather than turning into tabs or a sidebar.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block element in normal document flow, scrolling away with the content; no sticky or fixed behavior by default.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The mission-section is the first major visible content block inside the central column and acts as the primary focal area at the top. It begins after a generous top margin of approximately 64–96px from the top edge of the viewport, helping it occupy the upper-middle portion of the first screen. The section’s heading (e.g., “About Us” or “Our Mission”) is set near the top of this column block, left-aligned but centered within the page by the column itself. Below the heading, with a gap of about 16–24px, one or two short paragraphs describe the mission and core purpose. The total width of the mission-section is constrained to the 640–720px column, with side margins making up roughly 25–30% of the viewport on each side on large screens. The vertical height of this section typically occupies about the middle third of the initial viewport, leaving room below for the navigation and a hint of the next section to peek into view.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block component in the main document flow, centered horizontally, expanding vertically with content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The values-list sits directly underneath the about-section-navigation, separated by a vertical gap of around 24–32px. It uses the same centered column width (up to 640–720px) and left-aligned text. Each value appears as a separate row in a simple vertical list, with 12–16px of vertical spacing between items. There is no dense bullet styling; instead, each value reads like a short sentence or label with minimal iconography, matching the minimalist, system-like aesthetic. The overall height of the values-list is modest—typically occupying no more than about 25–35% of the viewport height on initial view—with plenty of whitespace above and below to keep the section airy. At the bottom of the list, another 24–32px gap separates it from the following history-section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard scrollable block, fully participating in the linear content flow with no fixed or floating behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The history-section appears next in the vertical sequence, starting approximately 24–32px below the last item of the values-list. It remains within the same central column width and is left-aligned. At the top of this block, a subtle section heading (e.g., “Our History”) is followed by 12–16px of space and then one to three short paragraphs summarizing the provider’s background. Paragraphs are spaced by about 12–16px to maintain legibility without appearing dense. The section typically occupies a moderate vertical band—perhaps 30–40% of the viewport height depending on history length—before giving way to the team-highlights-list below, separated by a further 24–32px. The overall feel is calm and narrative, mirroring the explanation text in the mockup but with more human-centered content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block in normal scroll flow, centered within the page via the main column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The team-highlights-list is placed below the history-section with a vertical gap of roughly 24–32px. It uses the same column width and left-aligned text, visually continuing the single, linear narrative down the page. A compact heading (e.g., “Our Team” or “Clinical Leadership”) sits at the top of this block, followed by a 12–16px gap and then a list of concise highlights such as qualifications, years of experience, or specializations. Each highlight is a single line or very short phrase, stacked vertically with 12–16px spacing between items, avoiding any complex layout like multi-column cards. Overall, the block occupies a modest vertical slice—similar in scale to the values-list—and sets up the transition to the more visually distinct accreditations card below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard scrollable block, following the linear content flow without fixed positioning.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The accreditations-list is presented as a visually distinct card within the same central column, echoing the mockup’s self-contained card element. Positioned about 24–32px below the team-highlights-list, it spans nearly the full width of the content column but is inset with a light gray background (or subtle border) and generous internal padding of around 16–24px on all sides. Within the card, a left-aligned heading such as “Accreditations & Standards” appears at the top, followed by 12–16px of vertical space and then the list of accreditations. Each accreditation is stacked vertically with 12–16px spacing between items, potentially with a compact, system-like label styling. The card’s height may occupy roughly 25–40% of the viewport depending on the number of items, but the padding and separation prevent any visual crowding. Outside the card, there is a relatively larger gap—about 32–40px—before the final contact-cta-global, emphasizing that this is a distinct trust-and-compliance block before the user is invited to take action.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a normal block-level card element within the linear flow, not fixed; it simply adds a padded, elevated container within the main column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-cta-global sits at the bottom of the content column as the final, prominent action. It is positioned about 32–48px below the bottom edge of the accreditations card, creating a clear visual separation between the informational content and the call to contact. The CTA is styled as a primary button-like link aligned to the right edge of the central column, mirroring the mockup’s lower-right primary button in a horizontal button row. On large screens, the link’s right edge lines up with the column’s right boundary, leaving ample blank space to its left and additional whitespace below (around 48–64px) before the page footer or viewport edge. On smaller screens, the CTA remains right-aligned within the column, maintaining the same sense of directional emphasis. The single action is large enough—using 12–16px vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding—to feel confident and tappable without any decorative flourish.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block element inside the main column, positioned at the end of the content; scrolls normally and is not fixed, though its spacing gives it a clear endpoint feel.

name: Contact Us
title: Contact Us
description: Page where users can view contact details for the home nursing care service and submit general or non-urgent inquiries via a contact form, with clear guidance on which channel to use for urgent versus non-urgent needs.

core_features: Retrieve and display current contact information (phone numbers, email addresses, physical address, urgent care guidance) using getContactInfo.
Provide a contact form for general or non-urgent inquiries that submits data through submitContactInquiry.
Use incoming URL parameters (subject, inquiryType) to prefill and configure the contact form state.
Offer clear guidance and inline cues to help users choose the correct contact channel for urgent care versus general questions, based on urgentCareGuidance and available contact info.
Show inline confirmation or error messages after contact form submission, based on the response from submitContactInquiry.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Viewing contact details and urgent care guidance
1. User navigates to contact.html from header or footer navigation.
2. On page initialization, the page shell component calls getContactInfo with no parameters.
3. When getContactInfo returns, the contact-info-panel displays the phoneNumbers list, emails list, and address object as provided by the API.
4. The urgent-care-guidance-panel displays urgentCareGuidance, helping the user understand when to call, email, or use the form, according to the returned text.
5. User can click on any phone number to open the device’s dialer, or click an email address to open their email client.

Workflow 2: Submitting a general inquiry from another page with predefined subject/inquiryType
1. User clicks a "Contact" or "Need help?" link from booking_manage.html, about.html, or index.html.
2. The source page navigates to contact.html with URL parameters subject and/or inquiryType set (for example: contact.html?subject=...&inquiryType=...).
3. On load, the contact-page-shell parses the URL and sets the initial state of the contact-form: the subject field is pre-populated with the subject parameter value, and the inquiryType field is pre-selected to match the inquiryType parameter value if it matches one of the available inquiry types.
4. User reviews or edits the prefilled subject, selects or changes the inquiry type if needed, and fills in fullName, email, phone, and message.
5. User clicks the submit button, triggering submitContactInquiry with the current field values (fullName, email, phone, subject, message, inquiryType).
6. While waiting for the API response, the form submit button is disabled and a loading state is shown.
7. When submitContactInquiry returns with success=true, the submission-status-banner shows the returned message inline and the form either clears or remains filled according to configured behavior; no page navigation is required.
8. If submitContactInquiry returns success=false, the submission-status-banner displays the returned message as an inline error and the user can adjust their input and resubmit.

Workflow 3: Submitting a general inquiry directly from Contact Us without prefilled parameters
1. User navigates to contact.html directly (no subject or inquiryType URL parameters).
2. On load, the contact-page-shell initializes the contact-form with empty field values, and inquiryType has no preselected value.
3. User fills in fullName, email, phone, subject, selects an inquiry type, and writes a message.
4. User submits the form; submitContactInquiry is called with the current field values.
5. The submission-status-banner shows success or error messages according to the API response.
6. If desired, the application may update the URL to include the subject and inquiryType used, so that a page refresh maintains those values (e.g., using history.replaceState to update query parameters).

Workflow 4: Adjusting inquiry type and preserving state via URL
1. User arrives at contact.html with or without initial URL parameters.
2. User changes the inquiryType selection in the contact-form (for example, switching from a general question to a booking-related question type available in the application).
3. On change, the page may update the inquiryType query parameter in the URL (e.g., contact.html?inquiryType=currentSelection&subject=currentSubject) without reloading the page, to keep the URL in sync with the form state.
4. If the user shares or bookmarks the URL, another user loading that URL will see the inquiryType and subject fields pre-populated from the query parameters and can continue the interaction from that state.
interactions: Clicking the header or footer navigation links to open contact.html, optionally with subject and inquiryType URL parameters when coming from other pages.
On page load, automatic call to getContactInfo to retrieve phoneNumbers, emails, address, and urgentCareGuidance, populating the contact-info-panel and urgent-care-guidance-panel.
Clicking on a phone number in the contact-info-panel triggers a tel: interaction, passing the selected number from the API response.
Clicking on an email address in the contact-info-panel triggers a mailto: interaction, using the email address from the API response.

Typing in the contact-form fields (fullName, email, phone, subject, message) updates the local form state and may also update corresponding URL parameters for subject and inquiryType where configured.
Selecting an inquiry type in the contact-form (inquiryType field) updates the form state and may update the inquiryType query parameter in the URL.

Clicking the form submit button triggers client-side validation (e.g., required fields not empty) and, if valid, calls submitContactInquiry with fullName, email, phone, subject, message, and inquiryType.

Receiving a successful response from submitContactInquiry (success=true) updates the submission-status-banner to display the returned message and puts the form in a submitted state (e.g., disabling fields or allowing another submission depending on configuration).
Receiving an unsuccessful response from submitContactInquiry (success=false) updates the submission-status-banner to display the returned message as an error while keeping form fields editable.

If getContactInfo fails or returns incomplete data, the page displays only the parts of contact info that are available from the response and leaves other sections blank, without injecting hardcoded fallback values.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page reads and writes state primarily through URL query parameters and transient in-memory form state. On initialization, the contact-page-shell parses the URL for subject and inquiryType parameters (e.g., contact.html?subject=...&inquiryType=...). If subject is present, its decoded value is used as the initial value of the contact-form subject field; otherwise the subject field is initialized empty. If inquiryType is present, its decoded value is used to attempt to preselect an inquiry type option in the contact-form; if there is no matching option, the inquiryType field starts with no selection. Changes to the subject or inquiryType fields can optionally cause the page to update the corresponding query parameters using the History API, so that the URL reflects the current form state without a page reload. The success or error state of a submission is maintained in in-memory state within the submission-status-banner and contact-form components after calling submitContactInquiry. The page does not rely on localStorage or static hardcoded data; all contact details (phoneNumbers, emails, address, urgentCareGuidance) come from getContactInfo on each page load, and form submissions always go through submitContactInquiry.

components.functionality: Initializes the Contact Us page, orchestrates data fetching via getContactInfo, parses URL parameters for subject and inquiryType, and passes data and handlers down to child components such as the contact-info-panel, urgent-care-guidance-panel, contact-form, and submission-status-banner.

Displays the list of phone numbers, email addresses, and physical address returned by getContactInfo. Provides click interactions to initiate calls or emails using the exact values from the API response. Does not introduce any static labels or values beyond what the application provides for labels in phoneNumbers and emails.

Shows the urgentCareGuidance text from getContactInfo to explain how users should differentiate between urgent and non-urgent situations and which contact channel to use. This component only renders the guidance text as provided by the API and may format it or break it into paragraphs if the text contains delimiters; it does not add any additional static guidance content.

Provides fields for fullName, email, phone, subject, inquiryType, and message. Initializes subject and inquiryType from URL.query.subject and URL.query.inquiryType if present. Performs basic client-side validation and, on submit, calls submitContactInquiry with the current field values. Emits events to update URL parameters corresponding to subject and inquiryType when configured. Disables the submit button and indicates loading while waiting for the submitContactInquiry response.

Displays inline status and feedback messages after the contact form is submitted. When submitContactInquiry returns, it reads success and message and shows a corresponding success or error state. It does not define its own static message text; it only displays the message returned by submitContactInquiry and derives the visual state (success vs error) from the success boolean.

Keeps the subject and inquiryType query parameters in sync with the contact-form state without reloading the page. On initial load, it reads URL.query.subject and URL.query.inquiryType to seed the form; when the user changes subject or inquiryType, it updates the query parameters using the browser History API. This enables deep-linking, bookmarking, and returning to the same prefilled form state.

layout.grid_system: Implicit single-column layout on a centered max-width container, likely aligned to an 8px or 4px Material grid. Content column roughly 600–720px wide with large outer margins.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a single, vertically stacked content column centered on the page. Inside this column, elements flow linearly from top to bottom: page-level context (title and brief description) at the top, then the contact details, followed by the urgent-care guidance card, then any submission-status banner, and finally the contact form with its button row at the very bottom of the column. Everything is horizontally centered as a block, but all text inside is left-aligned.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a single, vertically stacked content column centered on the page. Inside this column, elements flow linearly from top to bottom: page-level context (title and brief description) at the top, then the contact details, followed by the urgent-care guidance card, then any submission-status banner, and finally the contact form with its button row at the very bottom of the column. Everything is horizontally centered as a block, but all text inside is left-aligned.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Apply a max-width of roughly 640–720px for the central column, centered within the viewport with wide left and right margins (each about 25–30% of the viewport on desktop). Reserve a generous top margin (about 15–20% of the viewport height, ~64–96px) before the first text element, then space each major section (info panel, guidance card, form) with 24–32px vertical gaps. The bottom of the page keeps extra breathing room below the form’s button row (~64px) to echo the top spacing and maintain a light, safe feel.
layout_strategies.content_density: Keep density low: minimal text per section, large vertical spacing between blocks, and comfortable internal padding (16–24px) inside the urgent-care guidance card and the form. Form fields are stacked with clear separation, avoiding tight clusters. There are no decorative elements; only essential text, subtle dividers, and flat backgrounds are used, following a utilitarian, Material-style system look.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Guide the eye from the top center downwards: the user first encounters the page title and brief description, then the concrete contact details, then a visually distinct light-gray urgent-care guidance card in the mid-screen zone, then any submission-status banner just above the form, and finally the contact form itself with a right-aligned primary submit button as the terminal focus. Overall flow is top-center context → mid-column guidance → bottom-right primary action, mirroring the warning-to-CTA progression of the mockup.

layout.overall_description: The Contact Us page appears as a calm, minimalist system screen: a light, flat background fills the full viewport, with a single narrow content column centered on the page. After a generous band of whitespace at the top, the user sees a simple page title and short explanatory line, left-aligned within the centered column. Directly beneath, the contact info panel lists phone, email, and address in a clean, text-only block. Slightly lower, a light-gray card spans the full width of the column, clearly calling out what to do for urgent or emergency needs and subtly separating urgent phone guidance from non-urgent form use. Below this, a slim status area is reserved where a submission-status banner appears when needed, before the main contact form. The form itself runs down the central portion of the page, each field stacked with generous spacing. At the very bottom of the column, a compact button row aligns to the right edge of the form, with the primary submit button in a clearly visible accent color and any secondary action button just to its left. The overall effect is a modern, Material-inspired, system-style layout with a clear, top-to-bottom narrative ending in a single decisive action.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page shell occupies the full viewport width and height, acting as a neutral background layer. Inside it, a single central content column is used: it is horizontally centered, with a max-width of approximately 640–720px and responsive side margins so that on desktop it takes roughly 40–50% of the viewport width. The top of this column begins after a generous vertical offset of about 64–96px from the top of the viewport, leaving the upper 15–20% mostly blank to create a calm, system-like feel. At the top of the column, a simple, left-aligned page title (e.g., “Contact us”) and one-line description sit as the first visible content. All subsequent components—contact-info-panel, urgent-care-guidance-panel, submission-status-banner, and contact-form—are stacked vertically within this column, each separated by 24–32px of whitespace. The column extends downwards with enough bottom padding (~64px) so that the final button row in the form does not sit flush against the viewport edge, maintaining balanced top and bottom breathing room.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-page container with a centered, fixed-width content column that scales responsively but does not scroll independently; scrolling is handled by the browser window as content height exceeds the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-info-panel is placed near the top of the central content column, directly below the page shell’s title/intro block, with about 24–32px of vertical space separating it from the heading. It spans the full width of the column (up to the 640–720px max) and is left-aligned within that column. The panel has modest vertical padding (around 16px top and bottom) but no heavy card styling—typically a plain background matching the page or a very subtle divider line above or below, keeping it visually light and utilitarian. Text within the panel is left-aligned and arranged in a simple vertical stack: primary phone number at the top, then email, then physical address, each separated by small 8–12px gaps. The panel’s height remains compact, occupying roughly the upper third of the main content area, while leaving enough space beneath it for the more visually distinct urgent-care guidance card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level element that participates in the normal vertical flow, expanding vertically as its text content grows but always maintaining the same horizontal alignment and margins as the rest of the content column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The urgent-care-guidance-panel sits immediately below the contact-info-panel, with a clear 24–32px vertical gap between them so it reads as a separate section. It spans the full width of the central column and is styled as a light-gray card, subtly raised in prominence compared to the plain background. The card uses generous internal padding—approximately 16–24px on all sides—so that its heading and guidance copy feel spacious and easy to scan. All text is left-aligned, with a bold or semi-bold heading at the top (e.g., “For urgent or emergency needs”) and supporting copy below, clarifying when to call instead of using the form. The card’s content is short, so its height is modest, occupying roughly a quarter to a third of the mid-page vertical area depending on text length. It acts as a focal block in the middle of the page: visually more prominent than plain text above and the form below, but still subordinate to the eventual primary action in the button row. The lower edge of the card is separated from the next element (submission-status-banner or, if absent, the contact-form) by another 24–32px gap to maintain the same vertical rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static card-like element fixed within the vertical flow; it grows vertically with content but maintains consistent padding and alignment. It does not stick or float; when the user scrolls, it moves normally with the rest of the content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The submission-status-banner is reserved as a thin, full-width strip directly below the urgent-care-guidance-panel and immediately above the contact-form. When present, it sits in the same central column, matching its width and left alignment. It has modest vertical padding—about 12–16px top and bottom—and a flat, high-contrast background color (e.g., green for success, red for error, or amber for warnings) that sets it apart from the page background while remaining firmly in the minimalist, system-style language. The text inside is left-aligned, short, and single-line or at most two lines, resembling a technical status message. The panel occupies only a small vertical slice—typically less than 10% of the visible column height—so it feels like a lightweight system notification inserted into the flow. When hidden, the layout closes the gap so that the contact-form shifts up directly beneath the urgent-care-guidance-panel, maintaining continuous vertical rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally rendered inline element: appears and disappears without overlaying other content, pushing the contact-form down when visible and releasing that space when not. It scrolls with the page and is not fixed or sticky.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-form occupies the lower portion of the central content column, positioned directly below the submission-status-banner when it is visible, or directly below the urgent-care-guidance-panel when the banner is absent, with about 24–32px of vertical spacing above it. It spans the full width of the column and is left-aligned. Inside, form fields are arranged in a vertical stack with generous spacing: each field group is separated from the next by 16–24px, and the form has internal padding (around 16–24px top and bottom) to prevent fields from feeling cramped against the page edges. Labels and inputs align along a single left margin, following a clean, system-style look with minimal borders. Near the bottom of the form, after the last field, there is a larger vertical gap (~24–32px) before the CTA button row. The button row is right-aligned within the form: the primary submit button sits at the far right edge of the column, and any secondary button (e.g., “Clear form” or “Cancel”) appears directly to its left with about 16px horizontal spacing. This placement echoes the mockup’s paired CTA pattern and makes the primary action the final, high-salience point in the visual flow. The form’s bottom edge sits roughly 64px above the bottom of the viewport on larger screens, preserving comfortable whitespace below the actions.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, flow-based element that expands vertically with its fields. It scrolls normally with the page; no part of it is fixed or sticky. The button row remains at the bottom of the form, not floating, so users reach it by scrolling through the fields.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-url-state-sync component operates behind the scenes to synchronize URL parameters with the contact page state—such as pre-selecting a topic in the form or triggering a specific submission-status-banner. It does not render any visible UI and therefore occupies no width, height, or visual space in the layout. It sits logically at the page-shell level, conceptually wrapping or observing the other components without altering their positions.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual utility component with no spatial footprint; it neither affects the document flow nor introduces any fixed or floating behavior. All layout remains entirely defined by the visible components.

name: FAQs
title: FAQs
description: Page that loads and organizes frequently asked questions about home nursing care services, pricing, booking, and care logistics. Users can browse by section, filter questions, and navigate to the contact page if their question is not answered.

core_features: Load FAQ sections and questions via the getFaqContent interface and render them in expandable groups.
Auto-focus and expand a specific FAQ section based on the optional section URL parameter.
Allow users to expand/collapse individual questions within each section to view answers.
Provide a text-based FAQ filter that narrows visible sections/questions client-side without additional API calls.
Offer quick navigation to a specific FAQ section by updating the section URL parameter from within the page.
Display a fallback call-to-action link to contact.html with a predefined subject when users still need help.
user_workflows: 1) User lands directly on faq.html without URL parameters -> 2) Page calls getFaqContent once -> 3) All sections are rendered in a collapsed or default state -> 4) User scrolls and clicks on a section header to expand it -> 5) User clicks on a question to toggle its answer visibility -> 6) User can expand multiple questions across sections as needed.

1) User clicks an FAQ-related link from index.html or services.html that includes faq.html?section={value} -> 2) Page reads the section parameter from the URL on load -> 3) Page calls getFaqContent -> 4) After data is loaded, the page computes a normalized identifier from each sectionTitle and compares it to the section URL parameter -> 5) The matching section (if any) is auto-expanded and optionally scrolled into view -> 6) User can still navigate to other sections or questions normally.

1) User starts reading FAQs and uses the search input to type a keyword or phrase -> 2) As the user types, the page filters the in-memory FAQ data returned by getFaqContent, matching against question and answer text -> 3) Sections with no matching questions are temporarily hidden or marked as having no results -> 4) User clears the search input to restore the full FAQ list.

1) User reads through FAQs and does not find a satisfactory answer -> 2) User scrolls to the end of the FAQ list and sees a 'Still need help? Contact us' action -> 3) User clicks the action -> 4) Browser navigates to contact.html with the subject URL parameter set to 'Question not answered in FAQs' -> 5) User can then submit a more detailed inquiry on the contact page.

1) While viewing the FAQs, user clicks on an internal shortcut to a specific section (e.g., from a section index at the top) -> 2) The click updates the browser URL to faq.html?section={derivedSectionId} using history APIs without reloading the page -> 3) The URL state manager reacts to the changed section parameter, sets the corresponding section as active, and scrolls it into view -> 4) If the user shares or revisits this URL, the same section will auto-expand on load.
interactions: On initial page load, the page triggers a data fetch using getFaqContent and renders FAQ sections and questions based on the returned structure.

When a user clicks a section header, the page toggles the expanded/collapsed state for that section; only state in memory and the URL parameter (for the currently focused section) is updated, no new API calls are made.
When a user clicks on an individual question row, the page toggles the visibility of that question's answer within its parent section.
When a user types into the FAQ search field, the page updates internal filter state and re-renders the FAQ list so only questions whose question or answer text match the search term remain visible.
When a user clears the search field (via a clear button or by deleting text), the filter state is reset and all FAQs from getFaqContent are displayed again.
When a user clicks a section shortcut or tab in the section index, the page updates the section URL parameter and activates the corresponding section as expanded and scrolled into view.

When a user clicks the 'Still need help? Contact us' link, the browser navigates to contact.html?subject=Question%20not%20answered%20in%20FAQs, allowing the user to continue their journey to the contact form.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is driven by a combination of URL parameters and in-memory component state. The section URL parameter is read on initial load and whenever the URL changes. After getFaqContent returns data, each sectionTitle is transformed into a normalized identifier (e.g., lowercased, spaces and non-alphanumeric characters replaced with separators). This derived identifier is compared to the section parameter. If a match is found, that section is auto-expanded and optionally scrolled into view. If no match is found, the page uses a default (such as all sections collapsed or the first section expanded) without altering the URL. When the user interacts with the section index to jump to another section, the page updates the section parameter in the URL via the History API, then applies the same normalization-and-match logic to set the active section. The search term is stored only in in-memory state and never persisted to the URL or localStorage. Expanding/collapsing questions and sections is also handled entirely in in-memory component state. The only persistent navigation-related state between page loads is the section URL parameter and the outgoing navigation to contact.html with its subject parameter.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading, URL parameter parsing, and distribution of FAQ data and active section state to child components.

Reads and interprets the section URL parameter on page load and on URL changes, computes normalized identifiers from section titles, and exposes the currently active section identifier to other components. Also updates the section URL parameter when the active section changes due to user interaction.
Renders a list of available FAQ sections (using sectionTitle from getFaqContent) as clickable items that let users jump directly to a section and update the section URL parameter.
Provides a text input for users to filter FAQs. Maintains the current search query in component state and notifies the FAQ list to update its filtered view based on the query.

Displays all FAQ sections returned by getFaqContent as an accordion-like structure. Each section shows its title and a list of question items. Sections can be expanded/collapsed, and each question within a section can be toggled to reveal its answer. Respects both the active section derived from the URL and the current search filter.

Displays a message when the current search filter yields no matching FAQs across all sections. This component only becomes visible when getFaqContent has successfully returned data but no questions remain visible after filtering.
Shows a persistent call-to-action at the end of the FAQ list that navigates the user to contact.html with the subject parameter set to indicate their question was not answered in the FAQs.

layout.grid_system: Implicit single-column layout on a centered max-width container, likely aligned to an 8px or 4px Material grid. Content column roughly 600–720px wide with large outer margins.
layout.layout_pattern: All FAQs content sits in a single, centered column with a max width around 640–720px, following a strict top-to-bottom stack. The FAQ page controller defines a neutral full-width background and a vertically centered band where all visible components appear. Near the top of this band (after some open space and the page heading handled by the page shell), users encounter the FAQ search form first, then a short gap, then the FAQ section index as a light, card-like navigation block. Below that sits the main FAQ accordion list (or the empty state in the same slot), and finally a bottom-row contact CTA aligned to the right. There are no sidebars or split columns; every component aligns to the same central axis with left-aligned text inside.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: All FAQs content sits in a single, centered column with a max width around 640–720px, following a strict top-to-bottom stack. The FAQ page controller defines a neutral full-width background and a vertically centered band where all visible components appear. Near the top of this band (after some open space and the page heading handled by the page shell), users encounter the FAQ search form first, then a short gap, then the FAQ section index as a light, card-like navigation block. Below that sits the main FAQ accordion list (or the empty state in the same slot), and finally a bottom-row contact CTA aligned to the right. There are no sidebars or split columns; every component aligns to the same central axis with left-aligned text inside.
