layout_strategies.space_allocation: Roughly the top 20–25% of the viewport is mostly blank vertical space plus the page title and a one-line explanation (handled by the page shell), with the FAQ search form occupying only a small portion in this region. The central 40–50% of the viewport is dedicated to the section index and the FAQ accordion list, which stretch down the page as users scroll. The lower 15–20% visually belongs to the final call-to-action row, with the contact CTA button anchored to the right and surrounded by generous breathing room above and below. Horizontally, the central content column uses about 40–50% of the viewport width on desktop, centered, with wide left and right margins of neutral background.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density stays low and controlled: only one or two interactive elements per vertical slice, with clear separation between them. The search bar, section index, and FAQ list are spaced using 24–32px gaps to avoid visual crowding. The section index is contained in a light, padded block to visually separate it from the main list. The contact CTA stands alone on its row with extra vertical padding, mirroring the sparse, safety-first feeling of the original warning mockup.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye is led from the page title (outside these components) down to the FAQ search form as the first focal interaction, then to the section index card with its clearly labeled categories, then into the FAQ accordion list where most reading happens. If there are no results, the empty state replaces the list and becomes the central focus. Finally, at the bottom of the content column, the eye lands on the right-aligned contact CTA button, echoing the strong bottom-right primary action of the original warning screen. Throughout, the linear vertical flow and centered column make navigation predictable and calm.

layout.overall_description: The FAQ page appears as a calm, minimalist, Material-style screen with a wide, neutral background and a single, centered content column. After a comfortable band of empty space and the page’s main heading (handled outside these components), a prominent search bar sits near the upper-middle of the viewport, spanning nearly the full width of the central column. Below it, a light-gray, card-like section index presents FAQ categories in a tidy, left-aligned block. As users scroll, a vertically stacked accordion of FAQ sections fills most of the page’s middle, with generous gaps between sections. When no FAQs match a search or filter, that area is occupied by a centered, understated empty-state message. At the bottom of the column, separated by clear breathing room from the FAQs, a single primary contact CTA button sits in a horizontal row, aligned to the right edge of the column, with additional empty space below it before the end of the page. The entire layout feels airy, secure, and utilitarian, mirroring the system-like clarity and vertical flow of the original warning mockup while adapting it to FAQ browsing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The FAQ page controller acts as the structural frame for the layout rather than a visible component. It spans the full viewport width and height, applying a solid, light background color and centering a single content column in the middle of the page. This column has a max-width of approximately 640–720px, is horizontally centered, and uses wide left and right margins (each roughly 25–30% of the viewport on desktop). The controller provides generous top and bottom padding for the entire FAQ experience, with about 64–80px of open vertical space above the first visible FAQ element and a similar amount below the final CTA. Inside this column, it stacks the other FAQ components vertically in order: heading/intro (from the shell), then faq-search-form, then faq-section-index, then either faq-section-list or faq-empty-state, and finally faq-contact-cta at the bottom.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Defines the main centered column and vertical spacing; otherwise participates in normal document flow with no fixed or sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The URL state manager is non-visual and does not render any user-facing interface. It operates behind the scenes to synchronize filters, selected FAQ sections, and search queries with the URL. In the layout, it occupies no physical space and does not alter spacing; its effect is only seen indirectly through which FAQs are visible, which section is expanded, and whether the empty state replaces the list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: No visual footprint; purely logical component with no impact on spatial layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The FAQ section index sits in the central column directly below the search area, separated from the search form by a vertical gap of about 24–32px. It is presented as a low-contrast, light-gray card-like block that spans nearly the full width of the central column (about 90–100% of the column width), with 16–20px of internal padding on all sides. The block is horizontally centered with its text and category labels left-aligned inside. It occupies roughly 15–20% of the column’s visible height on first load, but collapses visually as users scroll past. Inside this block, category labels (chips or simple text links) form a single or multi-line row, but the entire index remains a single object in the vertical stack. Above and below the section index card, there is enough spacing (24–32px) to clearly separate it from both the search form above and the FAQ list below, echoing the standalone recommendation card from the warning mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sits in the normal vertical flow of the central column; not sticky or fixed. As the user scrolls, it moves off-screen like any other block.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The FAQ search form is positioned near the top of the content column, becoming the first interactive element users see after the page heading and introductory text. It sits roughly at the transition between the top 20–25% and the central band of the viewport. The search bar itself spans about 90–100% of the central column width, centered, with left-aligned contents and a height similar to a Material search field (around 48–56px). There is a generous margin of about 32–40px above it (after the heading/intro) and about 24–32px below it before the section index, maintaining low density and a sense of breathing room. On smaller viewports, the search form continues to use the full available width with consistent padding to the screen edges, preserving the single-column feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element in the normal flow, appearing near the upper portion of the column; scrolls with the page and does not remain fixed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The FAQ section list forms the main content block of the page and occupies the majority of the central vertical band. It is placed directly below the section index, with a vertical gap of about 24–32px, and stretches down the page as an accordion-style list. The list aligns with the central column, spanning nearly 100% of its width, with each accordion section taking the full width of the column minus its internal padding. Individual section headers and answers are left-aligned, and each section is separated vertically by 16–24px to preserve a low-density, scannable feel. On first load with typical content, the visible portion of the list comfortably fills the central 40–50% of the viewport height, extending further as the user scrolls. When the list is present, it fully occupies the main content slot between the section index above and the contact CTA below, leaving a larger 32–48px gap before the CTA row to visually distinguish primary browsing from the final action.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Occupies the main scrolling region in the normal document flow; expands vertically as FAQ content grows, with no fixed or sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The FAQ empty state appears in exactly the same vertical slot as the FAQ section list and replaces it entirely when no questions match the current filters or search. It is positioned directly below the section index with a similar 24–32px gap. The empty state is rendered as a centered, compact panel within the column: it may occupy around 50–70% of the column’s width (narrower than the full column to feel lighter) and about 25–35% of the visible viewport height, creating a compact, clearly isolated message area. The panel is horizontally centered, with its text left-aligned inside and ample internal padding (16–24px). There is sufficient white space above and below the panel (24–32px to the section index above and 32–48px to the contact CTA below) to keep the layout airy and to emphasize that this is a distinct, temporary state, similar to how the warning mockup isolates its central card content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Mutually exclusive with the FAQ section list, occupying the same region in the vertical flow; static and scrolls normally with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The FAQ contact CTA is positioned as the final element in the content column, serving as the page’s primary action if the FAQs did not resolve the user’s need. It appears below either the FAQ section list or the empty state, separated by a generous vertical gap of about 32–48px. The CTA is rendered as a single primary button-style link within a horizontal row that spans the full width of the central column. Within this row, the button is aligned to the right edge of the column, mirroring the bottom-right primary action pattern from the warning mockup’s 'Back to safety' button. The button itself is robust in size (e.g., 48px tall, 160–240px wide) and stands alone on its row with minimal adjacent elements, emphasizing its importance. Below this row, there is an additional 48–64px of empty space before the end of the page content, reinforcing a calm, conclusive feel at the bottom of the layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Anchored at the bottom of the FAQ content stack within the main column; participates in normal scrolling and is not fixed or sticky.

name: Privacy Policy
title: Privacy Policy
description: Displays the dynamically loaded privacy policy text, including last-updated information, and supports deep-linking to sections and a print-friendly view.

core_features: Fetch and display the complete privacy policy content using the getPrivacyPolicyContent interface.
Show the policy's last-updated date as returned by the interface.
Render the policy HTML safely, preserving headings, lists, and links from the contentHtml response.
Provide an in-page section navigator (table of contents) derived from the headings inside contentHtml.
Support deep-linking to specific sections via URL parameters so users can share or revisit particular topics.
Offer a print-friendly mode controlled by URL parameters to facilitate printing or saving the policy.
Handle loading and error states when retrieving the policy content, with an option to retry.
user_workflows:
Read the privacy policy:
1. User opens privacy.html (e.g., via footer link).
2. Page initializes and calls getPrivacyPolicyContent with no parameters.
3. While waiting, a loading state is shown.
4. On success, the page displays the lastUpdated value and renders contentHtml.
5. User scrolls through the policy or uses the section navigator to jump to specific sections.

Open a specific section from a shared link:
1. User clicks or opens a link like privacy.html?section=<sectionId>.
2. Page loads and calls getPrivacyPolicyContent.
3. After contentHtml is rendered, the page parses headings to identify a section whose identifier matches the section URL parameter.
4. If a matching section is found, the view scrolls/focuses to that section and optionally highlights it.
5. The section navigator selection is synchronized with the current section.

Switch to print-friendly view:
1. User clicks a 'Print' or 'Print view' control on the privacy policy page.
2. The click updates the URL to include a parameter such as view=print without reloading the document content.
3. The page applies print-friendly behavior (e.g., hides navigation and focuses on the policy content only) based on the view parameter.
4. Optionally, the component triggers the browser's print dialog.
5. When the user exits print mode (using a control or browser navigation), the URL parameter is updated (e.g., view=default), restoring the standard layout.

Handle loading or error states:
1. User navigates to privacy.html.
2. getPrivacyPolicyContent is called.
3. If the request is pending, a loading indicator is shown instead of content.
4. If the request fails, an error message is shown with a 'Retry' action.
5. On 'Retry', getPrivacyPolicyContent is called again.
6. On success, normal policy display resumes.

Navigate within the policy and update sharable URL:
1. User clicks a section title in the table of contents.
2. The click updates the URL parameter section to the section identifier corresponding to that heading.
3. The view scrolls to the section and marks it as active in the navigator.
4. User can copy the URL from the address bar; sharing this URL ensures others land on the same section when they open the page.
interactions:
Initial page load:
- Event: DOMContentLoaded or equivalent page initialization.
- Action: Call getPrivacyPolicyContent; set internal state to loading.
- Result: On success, store lastUpdated and contentHtml in state and render components. On failure, show error state.

Retry loading policy:
- Event: User clicks a 'Retry loading policy' button in the error view.
- Action: Call getPrivacyPolicyContent again; set state to loading.
- Result: Replace error view with loading; on success, render policy as normal.

Select policy section from navigator:
- Event: User clicks an item in the section navigator (e.g., a heading representing a section).
- Action: Update URL query parameter section to the selected section's identifier using history APIs; scroll or focus to the relevant heading within contentHtml.
- Result: Section content is brought into view; navigator highlights the selected section.

Follow in-content anchors or links to subsections:
- Event: User clicks an anchor link inside contentHtml that points to another heading within the policy.
- Action: Intercept hash or internal link if needed, map to a section identifier, and update the section URL parameter accordingly.
- Result: Browser scrolls to the relevant subsection and the navigator state is synchronized.

Toggle print-friendly view:
- Event: User clicks a control to switch to print view.
- Action: Update the URL parameter view to print; optionally call window.print according to page logic.
- Result: Page components adjust to print-focused display based on the view parameter.
- Event (exit print view): User clicks a 'Back to standard view' control.
- Action: Update the URL parameter view to default or remove it.
- Result: Standard layout behavior resumes.

Keyboard navigation:
- Event: User tabs through focusable elements (e.g., section navigator items, controls).
- Action: Focus events highlight the current control; pressing Enter/Space activates the same behavior as clicking (e.g., changing section, toggling view).
- Result: Page remains usable without a pointing device.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page state is fully controlled by the getPrivacyPolicyContent response and URL parameters, without using localStorage.

URL parameters:
- section: Optional. Represents the identifier of a specific policy section to focus on.
  - When present on initial load (e.g., privacy.html?section=<id>):
    1. The page loads the policy content via getPrivacyPolicyContent.
    2. After rendering contentHtml, the page analyzes headings (e.g., h2/h3) to derive section identifiers (for example, from id attributes or a deterministic transformation of heading text).
    3. If the derived section matches the section parameter, the page scrolls to that heading and highlights it as active.
    4. The section navigator component selects the corresponding item.
  - When changed via user interaction (clicking a navigator item):
    1. The page updates the URL's section parameter via the History API without reloading content.
    2. The active section state is updated and the view scrolls to the corresponding heading.

- view: Optional. Controls the display mode of the page.
  - Supported logical values may include 'print' or a default when absent.
  - When view=print:
    1. Non-essential layout components (such as surrounding site navigation) can be hidden by the application using this state.
    2. The policy content is emphasized for printing.
    3. A component may trigger the browser's print dialog or wait for the user to initiate printing manually.
  - When view is absent or set to another supported value:
    1. The page uses the standard reading layout.

Internal state (not persisted in storage):
- loading: boolean – true while awaiting getPrivacyPolicyContent response; controls whether loader or content is rendered.
- error: nullable – holds error information if getPrivacyPolicyContent fails; controls whether the error component is shown.
- lastUpdated: value from getPrivacyPolicyContent; used to display metadata.
- contentHtml: value from getPrivacyPolicyContent; rendered into the content viewer.
- sections: derived list of section objects (e.g., id, title, DOM reference) parsed from contentHtml; used by the section navigator.
- activeSectionId: tracks which section is currently active (from URL parameter or scroll position); synchronized with section URL parameter.
- viewMode: derived from view URL parameter; used to toggle between standard and print-friendly behaviors.

All changes to activeSectionId and viewMode should update the URL query parameters so that the address bar always reflects the current state, allowing sharing and bookmarking.

components.functionality: Initializes the page by calling getPrivacyPolicyContent, manages loading and error states, and provides lastUpdated and contentHtml to child components.
event_handlers: onInit: Triggered when the page loads; calls getPrivacyPolicyContent with no parameters and sets internal loading state.
onFetchSuccess: Receives the response object { lastUpdated, contentHtml }, stores it in component state, clears any existing error, and notifies dependent components to render.
onFetchError: Stores error details in state and exposes an error flag for the error/retry component.
onRetry: Invoked by child components to re-invoke getPrivacyPolicyContent after an error; resets loading and error state.

components.functionality: Displays metadata about the privacy policy, including the last-updated date, and provides controls for print view or other policy-level actions.
event_handlers: onViewModeChangeClick: Updates the view URL parameter (e.g., toggling between standard and print view) using the History API without reloading data.
onPrintClick: Sets the view URL parameter to a print-oriented value and may dispatch an event to trigger the browser print dialog.

components.functionality: Builds a navigable list of policy sections by parsing heading elements from contentHtml and enables users to jump to specific sections while updating the URL state.
event_handlers: onContentParsed: After contentHtml is rendered, extracts heading information (e.g., text and identifiers) to construct an array of sections used to render the navigation list.
onSectionClick: When a section entry is clicked, updates the URL parameter section to the corresponding section identifier using the History API and scrolls the viewport to that heading.
onURLSectionChange: Listens for changes in the section URL parameter (e.g., via back/forward navigation) and updates the active item in the navigator accordingly.
onKeyboardActivate: Handles keyboard activation (Enter/Space) on a focused section item to perform the same actions as onSectionClick.

components.functionality: Safely renders the policy HTML returned in contentHtml, preserves structure and anchors, and synchronizes scroll position and highlighting based on the active section.
event_handlers: onRender: Renders the HTML from contentHtml into the document using a safe method; after rendering, signals other components (like the navigator) that headings are available for parsing.
onSectionParamChange: When the section URL parameter changes, locates the corresponding heading element and scrolls it into view; may apply a transient highlight to indicate focus.

onInContentAnchorClick: Intercepts clicks on internal anchors within contentHtml; if the target corresponds to a known section, updates the section URL parameter and scrolls accordingly rather than causing a full page reload.

components.functionality: Centralizes logic for handling the view URL parameter, determining whether the page is in standard or print-friendly mode and informing other components to adjust behavior.
event_handlers: onInit: Reads the initial view parameter from the URL and sets the current view mode state.

onViewParamChange: Listens for changes in the view parameter (via navigation or controls) and updates layout-related flags that other components can use to adapt (e.g., hiding non-policy UI while in print view).
onToggleViewModeClick: Toggles the view parameter between supported values (e.g., default and print) using the History API; does not re-fetch policy content.

onPrintRequested: If view mode is set for printing, this handler may programmatically invoke the browser's print dialog (subject to application logic) and then optionally restore the previous view mode.

components.functionality: Displays an error state if getPrivacyPolicyContent fails and provides a retry action that re-attempts loading the policy.
event_handlers: onShow: Listens for error state from privacy-content-loader and renders an error message with contextual information (without hardcoding policy content).
onRetryClick: Invokes the onRetry handler of privacy-content-loader to call getPrivacyPolicyContent again.
onURLChange: Optionally clears transient error messages when the URL changes in a way that indicates a navigation refresh (without forcing a reload).

layout.grid_system: Implicit single-column layout on a centered max-width container, likely aligned to an 8px or 4px Material grid. Content column roughly 600–720px wide with large outer margins.
layout.layout_pattern: All components sit in a single, centered content column with a max width of about 640–720px, following a strict top-to-bottom order. From top to bottom: the metadata bar (with page title and last-updated info) and view-mode controller share the top row of the content column; the section navigator appears as a self-contained, light card directly beneath; the main privacy-content-viewer occupies the central portion as a long, scrollable text area; the data-fetcher and error-retry components control/replace that central region as needed. Everything is centered as a block within the viewport, but text and controls inside the column are left-aligned, mirroring the warning mockup’s pattern.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: All components sit in a single, centered content column with a max width of about 640–720px, following a strict top-to-bottom order. From top to bottom: the metadata bar (with page title and last-updated info) and view-mode controller share the top row of the content column; the section navigator appears as a self-contained, light card directly beneath; the main privacy-content-viewer occupies the central portion as a long, scrollable text area; the data-fetcher and error-retry components control/replace that central region as needed. Everything is centered as a block within the viewport, but text and controls inside the column are left-aligned, mirroring the warning mockup’s pattern.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The central content column uses roughly 40–50% of the viewport width, centered with wide left/right margins. The top 15–20% of the viewport is mostly whitespace with the metadata bar and view-mode controls sitting in the upper part of the column. The section navigator and the top part of the content viewer occupy the middle 40–50% of the screen. Large vertical padding at the top and bottom (around 64px) creates breathing room. When an error occurs, the error-retry component occupies a compact but visually dominant block in the center of the column, similar in vertical distribution to the original warning screen, with extra vertical whitespace above and below it.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density stays low: generous vertical gaps (24–32px) between metadata, navigator, and content. Each interactive element (navigator card, view-mode toggle, error retry message) has comfortable internal padding (around 16–24px), with no crowded elements. Text lines are relatively short due to the narrow column and are spaced with ample line-height. The section navigator is compact but still airy, and the view-mode controls are compact icons or pill buttons, not a dense toolbar.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The user’s eye is drawn first to the metadata bar at the top of the column (serving as the equivalent of the heading in the warning layout), then to the right-aligned view-mode controls in that same horizontal band. From there, the eye moves down to the light-gray section navigator card, which stands out as the next focal element. Then the flow continues into the main body text of the privacy-content-viewer, which forms a long scroll track. If an error occurs, the error-retry component temporarily becomes the primary focus: a centered icon and heading in the mid-upper section of the column, followed by an explanatory message and a prominent retry button at the bottom, echoing the warning pattern’s top-to-bottom flow.

layout.overall_description: The Privacy Policy page is presented as a calm, minimalist, single-column layout floating in the center of a full-width, light background. A narrow content column, about 640–720px wide, is centered horizontally with generous margins on both sides and about 64px of whitespace at the top and bottom of the viewport. At the top of this column, a simple metadata bar displays the page title and last-updated date on the left, while the view mode controls sit on the right, both aligned along a single, clean baseline. Below this, a light-gray, rounded card-style navigator lists key policy sections, serving as an at-a-glance map of the document. The main privacy text flows beneath, left-aligned and easy to read, occupying most of the vertical space as the user scrolls. Spacing between each section is generous, keeping the page feeling open and trustworthy. When the policy fails to load, the central text area is temporarily replaced by a focused error block: an icon and message centered in the column with a strong primary retry button at the bottom, matching the warning-screen visual hierarchy while maintaining the overall minimalist, medical-service-appropriate tone.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The privacy-content-loader is not directly visible as a standard UI block but governs the central portion of the content column. In the default (loading) state, it occupies the same vertical band reserved for the privacy-content-viewer, starting approximately 96px below the top of the content column (after the vertical space and metadata area). Within this band, it centers a small loading indicator and status text both horizontally and vertically relative to the visible part of the column: a simple spinner or progress indicator appears at the center, with a short system-style line of text immediately below it, using the same left-aligned-but-center-block approach (the loading text is left-aligned relative to a narrow virtual text column under the centered spinner). The loader respects the same max-width as the main content (about 640–720px) and keeps at least 32px of whitespace above and below the loading indicator to maintain low density. When loading completes successfully, this component effectively yields its space to the privacy-content-viewer; on error, it hands over the same area to the privacy-error-retry component. It scrolls with the page and never fixes itself; it always remains within the central column bounds.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Occupies the main content band during loading; swaps its visual presence with the privacy-content-viewer or privacy-error-retry. No fixed or sticky behavior; it scrolls in place with the rest of the content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The privacy-metadata-bar sits at the top of the central content column, starting around 64px below the very top of the viewport to echo the generous top margin of the warning mockup. It spans the full width of the content column (about 640–720px) but not the full viewport. Inside, the key elements are arranged in a single row: on the left, the page title (“Privacy Policy”) in a bold, slightly larger font acts as the anchor for the user’s eye, mirroring the strong heading in the original warning screen; directly beneath or to its right in smaller text, the last-updated date and any version identifier appear. The metadata bar height is relatively modest (roughly 56–72px total including padding) with 16–24px of vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding on both sides. There is a clear separation below it—a 24–32px vertical gap—before the section navigator card starts, maintaining the low-density feel and clear segmentation of sections.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element at the top of the scrolling content column; it does not stick or float but scrolls out of view as the user moves down the document.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The privacy-section-navigator appears directly below the metadata bar, separated by roughly 24–32px of whitespace. It is rendered as a self-contained, light-gray card that visually resembles the recommendation card in the warning mockup: slightly contrasted background (e.g., very light gray), subtle rounded corners, and a thin border or shadow kept minimal to stay within the utilitarian style. The card spans nearly the full width of the content column (leaving a small 16–24px inset padding on all sides) and has generous internal padding of about 16–20px vertical and 20–24px horizontal. Within the card, section links are left-aligned in a vertical list, each item separated by an 8–12px gap, so the list feels airy rather than dense. The card’s total height will vary depending on the number of sections, but it visually occupies a concise block in the upper-middle of the initial viewport. Below the navigator, a larger gap (around 32px) opens before the beginning of the main privacy-content-viewer, emphasizing that the navigator is a distinct, optional helper rather than part of the continuous legal text.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: By default, it scrolls with the content as a normal block. Optionally, on larger screens, the top edge of the card can become sticky to the top of the viewport once scrolled to (within the content column bounds), maintaining quick access to section links without breaking the single-column layout. On smaller screens, it remains non-sticky to avoid crowding.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The privacy-content-viewer is the main body area of the page and begins immediately below the section navigator card, with a consistent 32px vertical gap to visually separate navigational controls from the legal text. It occupies the full width of the central column (about 640–720px), with the actual text content left-aligned and padded by roughly 16–24px from the edges of the column to improve readability. The text flows in a single vertical stream, forming the central 60–70% of the overall page scroll length. Headings within the policy create internal structure, but there are no columns or sidebars; references and deep-link anchors are simply inline or placed directly above the relevant headings, preserving the linear flow. At the very bottom of the rendered text, additional vertical space (about 48–64px) is left before the page footer area, echoing the breathing room around the buttons in the warning mockup, but adapted to a non-CTA legal page. When in print-friendly mode, this viewer maintains approximately the same column width but may remove or soften background fills and borders; it still occupies the same spatial region. If loading fails, this entire region is replaced by the error-retry component, which uses the same width and padding but with a much more compact vertical layout centered in the upper half of this band.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard scrollable content zone that grows vertically as needed. It does not fix or float; it simply follows the natural document flow. In print mode, it controls page breaks but still adheres to the same virtual column width.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The privacy-view-mode-controller is a compact control cluster aligned to the right edge of the same horizontal band as the metadata bar. Visually, it sits at the top-right corner of the content column, horizontally aligned with the baseline of the title text in the metadata bar. It consists of one to three small controls (e.g., a toggle between ‘Standard’ and ‘Print view’ and possibly an icon for deep-linking or copying the URL). These controls are presented as minimal, text or icon buttons with 8–12px of internal padding and 8–12px gaps between them. The whole controller cluster occupies roughly 20–25% of the content column width and does not exceed the height of the metadata bar. A small horizontal gap (around 16px) separates it from the right edge of the column to maintain visual balance. When the viewport narrows significantly (e.g., on mobile), the controller can wrap onto a second line and align left below the metadata text, still separated by a tight vertical gap (8–12px) to preserve the minimalist, stacked feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Anchored to the metadata bar region; it scrolls away with the metadata bar and does not remain sticky. It has no overlay or floating behavior, keeping the experience clean and unobtrusive.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The privacy-error-retry component takes over the vertical space normally used by the privacy-content-viewer when an error occurs. It is positioned within the same central column, starting roughly where the top of the main text would begin—about 96–120px below the top of the metadata bar—thus placing the error message in the mid-upper portion of the viewport. The layout inside this component intentionally mirrors the warning-screen pattern: at the top center of its area, a medium-sized warning or info icon (e.g., outlined circle with exclamation mark) is displayed, occupying a small but visually strong region. Below the icon, centered across the column but with the text itself left-aligned in a narrow internal text column, comes a bold heading such as “We couldn’t load the privacy policy.” A multi-line explanatory paragraph follows, with key technical information or domain details in bold, mirroring the explanatory paragraph in the mockup. Beneath that, a smaller, subdued line shows an error code or diagnostic hint. All these elements are separated vertically by 8–16px gaps, keeping them distinct but cohesive. Further down, a light-gray recommendation card—similar in style to the section navigator card—can optionally suggest actions like checking the connection or trying again later. Finally, at the bottom of this error block, a horizontal row of action buttons is right-aligned within the content column: a primary ‘Retry’ button in a strong color (e.g., blue) sits on the right, with an optional secondary ‘Back’ or ‘Go to homepage’ button to its left, separated by about 16px. This button row sits in the lower part of the component’s area with extra whitespace (24–32px) above it and at least 48px below before any footer, echoing the breathing room around the CTAs in the original warning screen.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: It conditionally replaces the privacy-content-viewer area when an error is detected, using the same centered column width and scrolling behavior. It is not fixed or modal; it scrolls with the page but is typically entirely visible on first load of the error state due to its compact vertical height and the generous white space around it.

name: Terms of Service
title: Terms of Service
description: Displays the website and service terms retrieved from the backend, and supports section navigation, deep links, and print-friendly viewing.

core_features: Load the latest Terms of Service content and last-updated date via getTermsOfServiceContent on page initialization.
Render the returned HTML terms content (contentHtml) as readable legal text.
Display the last-updated date (lastUpdated) in a prominent informational area.
Generate a navigable table of contents (ToC) from headings inside the loaded terms content to help users jump between sections.
Support deep-linking and auto-scrolling to specific sections of the terms using URL parameters and/or hash (e.g., ?section=... or #section-id).
Provide an in-page text search and highlight feature driven by a URL parameter (e.g., ?q=...) to help users find specific clauses in the terms.
Offer a print-friendly mode controlled by a URL parameter (e.g., ?mode=print) to simplify printing or PDF export of the terms.
user_workflows:
View Terms of Service:
1. User navigates to terms.html (via footer link or direct URL).
2. Page initializes and calls getTermsOfServiceContent.
3. When the response arrives, the page shows the lastUpdated value and renders contentHtml.
4. The ToC is generated from headings within the rendered content.
5. If a section-related URL parameter or hash is present, the page scrolls to and highlights that section.

Navigate to a specific terms section from another page:
1. User clicks a link elsewhere on the site that points to terms.html with a section parameter or hash (e.g., terms.html?section=booking-cancellation or terms.html#booking-cancellation).
2. On load, the page calls getTermsOfServiceContent.
3. After rendering contentHtml, the section-navigation logic locates the corresponding element (matching the section parameter or hash) and scrolls it into view.
4. The ToC entry for that section is highlighted as active.

Use in-page section navigation (ToC):
1. User opens terms.html and waits for content to load via getTermsOfServiceContent.
2. The generated ToC lists the detected sections (derived from headings in contentHtml).
3. User clicks a ToC item.
4. The click handler updates the URL (e.g., updating the section parameter and/or hash without reloading the page) and scrolls smoothly to the target section.
5. The selected ToC entry is marked as active and remains in sync if the user uses the browser back/forward buttons.

Search within terms text via URL parameter:
1. User enters a search term into the in-page search field and submits the form.
2. The search component updates the URL with a q parameter (e.g., terms.html?q=entered-term), using history APIs to avoid a full reload when possible.
3. A search-highlighting routine scans the currently rendered contentHtml text and visually highlights matches.
4. If the user navigates back/forward or reloads with q present, the page again applies the highlight logic after getTermsOfServiceContent completes and contentHtml is rendered.

Open terms in print-friendly mode:
1. User clicks a "Print view" control on the Terms of Service page.
2. The click handler updates the URL with a mode parameter (e.g., ?mode=print, preserving other parameters like section or q when possible).
3. The page state logic detects mode=print and applies a print-friendly configuration (for example, simplifying layout and optionally auto-opening the browser print dialog if desired by the implementation).
4. If the page is loaded directly with mode=print in the URL, the same configuration is applied after content is loaded and rendered.
interactions:
Initial content load:
- Trigger: Page load.
- Behavior: A data-loading component calls getTermsOfServiceContent with no parameters.
- Result: The page enters a loading state until lastUpdated and contentHtml are available, then it exits loading and renders the content and metadata.

Table of Contents item click:
- Trigger: User clicks a ToC link representing a section of the terms.
- Behavior: Prevent default full-page navigation, update the URL's section parameter and/or hash using history.pushState or history.replaceState, then scroll the target section into view.
- Result: The relevant section is visible and the corresponding ToC entry becomes active.

Section deep-link handling:
- Trigger: Browser loads terms.html with a section query parameter and/or hash, or user uses browser back/forward to a URL with such parameters.
- Behavior: After getTermsOfServiceContent completes and contentHtml is rendered, a navigation controller reads the current section parameter/hash from the URL, finds the matching element in the DOM, and scrolls/highlights it.
- Result: The page opens directly at the requested section.

Search form submit:
- Trigger: User types text in the terms search input and submits.
- Behavior: Prevent form's default full-submit, update the q parameter in the URL, and run a highlight routine on the rendered content to mark all occurrences of the search term.
- Result: Matching text segments are highlighted; if no matches exist, optional in-page feedback can be shown.

Print-view toggle:
- Trigger: User clicks a print-view toggle button.
- Behavior: Toggle the mode URL parameter to print or remove/alter it to return to normal mode; when mode=print is detected, apply print-friendly classes and optionally call window.print depending on implementation.
- Result: The layout adapts for printing; users can print or save as PDF from their browser.

Back to top / section reset:
- Trigger: User clicks a "Back to top" control.
- Behavior: Scroll to the top of the content container and update the URL to clear or reset the section parameter/hash.
- Result: The user returns to the start of the terms, and the ToC deselects specific section highlighting.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page relies on URL parameters and hash to manage its state without using localStorage. Key parameters:
- section: A string that identifies a specific section in the terms content. When present, after getTermsOfServiceContent returns and contentHtml is rendered, the section-navigation controller attempts to match it to an element ID or data attribute within the rendered HTML and scrolls to it. Clicking ToC entries or in-text links updates the section parameter, and it is preserved when adding/modifying other parameters.
- q: A string representing the current in-page search query. When q is present, after contentHtml is rendered, the search-highlighting logic runs and highlights portions of the text that match q. Submitting the search form updates q in the URL. Clearing the search input and submitting again removes q from the URL and removes highlighting.
- mode: A string controlling view mode (e.g., print when the user wants a print-optimized view). On load or when the URL changes, if mode indicates print mode, the page applies print-specific configuration to the layout components. Toggling print mode updates this parameter while preserving others like section and q.
- Hash (#...): If a URL hash is present, it is treated similarly to the section parameter and may represent an element ID inside contentHtml. On load and on hashchange events, the navigation controller scrolls to the corresponding element. Updates to the ToC or in-text links may use hash, section, or both to ensure standard browser deep-link behavior.
The overall state is recomputed whenever URL parameters or hash change (via direct navigation, back/forward, or programmatic history updates). Content is always sourced from getTermsOfServiceContent, and the page does not cache terms text outside the current session or persist it via localStorage.

components.functionality: On page initialization, calls getTermsOfServiceContent to retrieve lastUpdated and contentHtml, manages loading and error states, and passes the loaded data to child components.
Displays the lastUpdated value returned by getTermsOfServiceContent in a readable format so users know when the terms were last modified.

Safely renders the contentHtml returned by getTermsOfServiceContent into the main content area. Exposes hooks for other components to inspect the rendered DOM (for ToC generation, section navigation, and search highlighting).

After the terms content is rendered, scans headings within the rendered HTML to generate a table of contents. Each entry references an associated section element to support in-page navigation and deep-linking.

Reads and monitors the URL's section parameter and hash to determine which section should be in focus. On initialization and URL changes, it scrolls the matching section into view and coordinates with the ToC to mark the active section.

Provides an input field for users to enter a search query for the terms. On submit, updates the q URL parameter and triggers a highlight routine on the already-rendered content without re-fetching the terms.
Listens for changes in the q URL parameter and applies or removes visual highlighting of all occurrences of the search term within the rendered terms content.
Provides a control to switch the Terms of Service page into and out of print-oriented mode. Updates the mode URL parameter and applies a print-friendly configuration when mode indicates print view.
Allows users to quickly return to the top of the terms content. Scrolls to the top of the content area and clears or normalizes the section parameter and/or hash in the URL.

layout.grid_system: Implicit single-column layout on a centered max-width container, likely aligned to an 8px or 4px Material grid. Content column roughly 600–720px wide with large outer margins.
layout.layout_pattern: All components are arranged in a single, vertically stacked column within a centered content band. The page title (outside this spec) would sit near the top of the column, followed by metadata and utility controls (last updated, print mode, search), then the table of contents as a self-contained card, and then the main terms content. At the bottom of the column sit section navigation controls, with a floating back-to-top control anchored to the lower right of the viewport. When loading, the content loader temporarily replaces the entire central column.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: All components are arranged in a single, vertically stacked column within a centered content band. The page title (outside this spec) would sit near the top of the column, followed by metadata and utility controls (last updated, print mode, search), then the table of contents as a self-contained card, and then the main terms content. At the bottom of the column sit section navigation controls, with a floating back-to-top control anchored to the lower right of the viewport. When loading, the content loader temporarily replaces the entire central column.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The central content band spans roughly 40–50% of the viewport width (max-width ~640–720px), centered horizontally with wide side margins. The top 15–20% of the viewport is mostly blank, with the first text elements sitting slightly below center vertically on initial load. Vertical gaps between major blocks (metadata, search, TOC card, content, navigation row) follow a 24–32px rhythm, with generous top and bottom page padding (~64px). Utility controls like the print toggle and back-to-top control occupy small, unobtrusive areas at the right edge of the content band or viewport.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density is intentionally low. Each functional group (metadata, search, TOC, content, navigation) is clearly separated with generous vertical spacing. The TOC appears in a light, card-like container with ample inner padding, and the navigation controls at the bottom use full-size buttons with comfortable hit areas. The main terms text is left-aligned and uses a readable line length; headings are clearly separated from paragraphs with at least 16–24px of vertical space.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Visual flow starts near the top-center of the page with the page title and last updated indicator, then moves slightly up and to the right to the print toggle, back down through the centered search bar, into the TOC card, and then flows linearly down through the terms content. At the end of the document, attention is drawn to the right-aligned navigation buttons, and finally to the floating back-to-top control near the lower-right of the viewport. Search highlighting subtly reinforces the flow by drawing attention to relevant text inside the terms content.

layout.overall_description: The Terms of Service page appears as a clean, minimalist, single-column layout floating in the center of a full-width, light background. A tall band of whitespace at the top gives the page a calm, formal feel. Within this space, a narrow content column—about 640–720px wide—is centered on the screen. Near the top of this column, the page title and a small last-updated line anchor the user, while a compact print-mode toggle sits aligned to the top-right edge of the column. A full-width but low-profile search bar follows, centered within the column and surrounded by plenty of breathing room. Below it, a subtle, light-gray card spans most of the column width, listing the main sections of the terms in a tidy, vertically stacked table of contents. This card visually mirrors a recommendation card in a warning screen: lightly elevated, distinct, but not flashy. The bulk of the screen is occupied by the main terms content, rendered as left-aligned text with clear hierarchy and generous spacing between headings and paragraphs. As the user scrolls down this long content area, search-matched phrases are simply highlighted in place without altering the layout. At the very bottom of the column, a right-aligned row of navigation buttons allows users to move to the previous or next section, recalling the dual-CTA row of the original mockup. Throughout scrolling, a small, unobtrusive floating back-to-top control hovers near the lower-right of the viewport, aligned horizontally with the central column, ready to bring the user back to the top while preserving the minimalist, utilitarian aesthetic.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When the terms are being fetched, the loader occupies the central content band in place of the usual components. It is vertically centered within the viewport’s central area, with at least 64px of top and bottom margin before hitting the page edges. Horizontally, it is centered within the same max-width column (about 640–720px) used by the final content. If it is a spinner or skeleton, it appears as a single, modest visual element in the very middle of the column, with plenty of empty space around it to maintain the sparse, system-style look. Other components (metadata, search, TOC, content, navigation) are either hidden or visually subdued until loading completes.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Center-aligned within the main content column; conditionally replaces the main content stack while data is loading; non-sticky and scrolls normally if content exceeds viewport height (e.g., skeleton states).

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The last-updated indicator appears near the top of the central column, directly beneath the page title (which sits outside this spec) with a vertical gap of roughly 8–16px. It is left-aligned within the column, occupying only a single line of text that spans no more than half the column width, giving it a light, metadata feel. There is approximately 24px of vertical space between this indicator and the next block (the search form), preserving the low-density, formal presentation. On wider screens, it visually anchors the left edge while the print-mode toggle balances the right edge of the same horizontal zone.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the vertical flow; scrolls with the page; does not stick to the top on scroll.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The terms HTML renderer is the main content block, occupying the central 40–50% of the viewport width as a left-aligned text column within the centered container. It starts roughly 24–32px below the bottom edge of the TOC card, clearly separated as the primary reading area. Headings generated within this renderer are left-aligned and spaced so that each section heading has at least 16–24px of vertical space above it, maintaining the low-density feel. Paragraphs maintain a comfortable line length (around 60–80 characters) and are spaced at 8–16px between each other. The renderer’s content extends vertically, often creating a long scrollable area that occupies the middle 50–60% of the page height on each viewport fold. No sidebars or columns appear within this area; it remains a single, uninterrupted reading column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrollable content within the main column; expands vertically as needed; respects the global max-width and remains centered on larger screens and full-width (with consistent padding) on very narrow screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The TOC generator renders as a self-contained, light-gray card positioned between the search form and the main terms content. It sits roughly 24–32px below the search form, spanning almost the full width of the central column (with 16–24px inner padding on all sides). The card’s content is left-aligned, listing section titles in a vertical stack with 8–12px spacing between items. The card itself occupies about one-third to one-half of the visible viewport height when fully expanded, but never feels dense due to generous inner spacing and limited typography. Its visual weight is slightly subdued compared to the main content but more distinct than metadata, echoing the optional feature card from the warning mockup. On narrow screens, it continues to span the full column width; on wide screens, it remains centered in the column with the same max-width.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block within the vertical flow. Optionally, on tall desktops, it can become sticky within the column once scrolled into place, but it should not break the single-column feel or push content into multiple side-by-side columns; any stickiness should keep it aligned with the main content column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The section navigation controller is positioned at the very bottom of the main content column, appearing 32–40px below the last paragraph or list item of the terms HTML. It renders as a horizontal row of one or two buttons aligned to the right edge of the column, echoing the dual-CTA layout from the warning mockup. The primary control (e.g., “Next section”) is a prominent, filled button placed at the far right, while any secondary control (e.g., “Previous section”) appears as a tonal or outlined button immediately to its left with an 8–16px gap. The row itself occupies roughly the bottom 15–20% of any given viewport fold once scrolled into view, with additional whitespace below it to keep it from feeling cramped against the page edge, especially in print mode.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the flow, anchored at the bottom of the content stack; scrolls normally with the page. In print mode, it may appear directly after the content but should not be sticky or floating.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The search form sits near the top of the column, just below the last-updated indicator. It is centered horizontally within the column and spans roughly 60–80% of the column width, with a minimum inner width on small devices to keep it usable. The input field has comfortable vertical padding and is accompanied by a simple label or placeholder, maintaining the utilitarian, Material-like look. There is a vertical gap of about 24px above it (from metadata) and 24–32px below it (before the TOC card), so the search area feels like its own breathing zone and not crowded by other elements.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in the vertical flow; scrolls with the page. On very narrow screens, it expands to nearly the full width of the column with consistent side padding. It does not stick to the top of the viewport when scrolling.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The search highlighter does not create a separate visual block; instead, it operates within the text rendered by the terms HTML renderer. Matches are highlighted inline using a subtle high-contrast but flat background color that is clearly visible against the page background while preserving readability. The highlighting respects the existing text layout, not changing line breaks or spacing; it simply overlays color on the matched words. Highlighting appears wherever matches occur throughout the main content, naturally guiding the eye along the existing reading flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Purely inline behavior within the main content; no independent positioning. It scrolls as part of the text and does not affect the spatial layout beyond color emphasis.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The print-mode toggle is a compact control (such as a simple text button with an icon or a small switch) placed at the top-right corner of the central content column. It sits horizontally aligned with, or just slightly above, the last-updated indicator, creating a subtle horizontal bar of metadata and utilities. There is about 16px of padding from the right edge of the column to the toggle, ensuring it does not feel flush with the screen edge. Vertically, it shares the same top whitespace as the title and metadata, remaining within the upper 20–25% of the viewport. It is visually low-key but easy to locate when scanning the top-right area of the content band.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the top portion of the main column; scrolls out of view with the rest of the header content. When activated, it can adjust the layout (e.g., removing the TOC card, expanding content width, hiding floating controls) but its own position in the normal mode remains fixed in the top-right of the content column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The back-to-top control appears as a small, unobtrusive floating button anchored near the lower-right corner of the viewport once the user has scrolled beyond the initial screen. It is positioned so that its center aligns roughly with the right edge of the central content column, sitting about 24–32px inward from the viewport edge and 24–32px above the bottom edge. Its size is modest (similar to a Material FAB or slightly smaller), ensuring it is easily tappable but does not compete with the section navigation buttons. It floats over the page content with a subtle shadow or clear outline, preserving the minimalist, system-style feel. On very narrow screens, it may shift slightly inward to avoid overlapping core text.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed position relative to the viewport (not the content); only becomes visible after the user scrolls down past a certain threshold. Remains fixed during scrolling and hides again when the user returns to the very top of the page. It is suppressed or repositioned in print mode so it does not appear in printed output.
